First of all, just so you know it, the "GUI" you are using is a combo of an x windows server slapped on a native CLI, on top of that is your WM, if you use gnome you have a DE built ontop of your WM too, that means that it's basically not progman on dos but fileman+ie on progman on dos. IOW, Gnome is to Linux as a third party window manager is to progman (Win 3.1) on Dos.

IF you want to get technical we could do this further, you don't have a clue so stop talking.

So you are going to install KDE and be a "tinkerer" i have no idea what that means but if it means getting to know your system, well, seriously...

If you want to get to know your system then install something without a gui, learn how to build things, learn how to configure scripts.

Install something like NetBSD (i didn't say Open for a reason here since it's secure by default) and set it up so hard so you can't even hack it even if you know the weaknesses, let others try too.

Now THAT is tinkering... "installing kubuntu", seriously... lol

### Post by TimelessRogue on 2005-12-31
Firstly, let me say that Linux DOES work ... every recent installation I have done has worked "out of the box" so to speak.  Without going into details, those installations have been two different laptops and four very different desktops, two of those built from scratch.  The only problems I have had were caused by me when I messed about where maybe I shouldn't have been or without having all the info I should have.  That said, Linux has come a ways since my first experiences in the early days of RedHat.

If there's a problem, it is that Linux is TOO tweakable ... it can be fiddled with way too much for what has become labeled the "average" user.  Let's face it:  we're all fiddlers and tweakers.  And when we do and it appears to break, we jump and shout and whine until that word gets spread far faster and far wider than when it works just fine.

But ya know what?  It is also much more fixable than Windows.  Sure I've had problems ... more than once.  But I've never had a "crash and burn" that wasn't a relatively easy fix.

So I guess that's my answer:  we all fiddle with it and when something goes awry the word goes out that "Linux doesn't work!"  And that, in my humble opinion, is a major setback in Linux being more widespread.

'Course then there's the question of how many vendors are selling Linux boxes ... all set up and running just fine ...

---

### Post by qferret on 2005-12-31
The one MAJOR thing that is holding Linux back is.........itself.

There isn't any ONE linux. This is what many of the people that DO use linux like. If there is a feature you don't like, YOU can change it. Unfortunately, this leads to multiple distros and forks in software developement. If manufacturers were to preinstall linux on all of their PC's....which distro would they choose? If there were 30+ flavours of Windows XP out there, all different with the exception of the explorer shell (or kexplorer, or MWindows, or whatever...you get the point), Windows would hav a substantially smaller user base, as it would have the exact issues that linux has now....inconsistency.

### Post by TimelessRogue on 2005-12-31
Ah, but qferret there IS only one Linux ... Linus' kernel.  There are, however, a goodly number of "strains" if you will ... variations on the theme ... different ways of using that one kernel, of putting it all together.  Which is not in an of itself a bad thing ... other that it can lead to some confusion as to which will serve our individual purposes the best ... for there is one that is best of each of us individually ... without being locked into the Master OS.

One advantage to having those variations available is exampled the the mini-Linux packages.  Another is the live-cd distributions ... useful not only as a way of trying Linux on both your system and your own self to see if it will work for you and your current configuration but also, as it turns out, to be able to actually run Linux from that cd without needing to install to a harddrive.  Yet another is the advantage of being able to do updates, upgrades and complete new kernel installations without HAVING to reboot your system after each and every minor  or even major software update.

So ... long live the ONE Linux ... and the constant variations on that theme!

### Post by dada1958 on 2006-01-06
I personally think that it also is a matter of time. People will, like myself, get sick and tired of those costly upgrades of their OS's and apps.

Look what happened to XPress, it was way too expensive and crappy, Quark treated their customers like shit so people were getting sick and tired of XPress and switched to InDesign.

Linux is evolving to the ultimate computer experience, it's just a matter of time. Playing with Ubuntu gives me more satisfaction than with Mac OS X Tiger because Apple's "Think Different" slogan is nothing more than "Buy Different".
My next computer won't be a Mac but a self build one.

### Post by John Dooley on 2006-01-16
Many find Linux confusing because of the great amount of choice. This is what a marketeer would call customer confusion. Up to a few years ago, most linux books appeared to be recycled Unix administation books. Most people are not interested in Postgres, NIS, Amanda on tape drives, but want to compose vidoes, games, do their taxes, build photo scrap books, word processing, etc.. Recently there are more books emphasising these areas and the authors are more helpful in making choices.

In the Linux world there is nothing like Visual Basic which is a RAD development system that has over 3 million active developers. This development environment and language allows many to contribute application who otherwise might be discouraged because their main skill lies not in computer science but in education, business, psychology, medicine, etc..

Python is a vastly superior to VB but there is the old problem of too many choices for a development environment. The recent Apress Beginning Python book listed about a dozen development environment tools and no help was given on making a selection. To the Linux world insider, this looks like a good thing. To the out sider, this looks like the Linux world is still making up its mind. So Uncle Bill gets to make the choice. 

Potential bridge developers(that is - developers that write application for others) will go to the book store and see 20 books on Visual Basic developement but not one on Python development - despite seing half a dozen superior Python language books. No author or publisher sees a market for a book that features a Python development environment - perhaps they are waiting for a winner - and perhaps these developers will also wait... Meanwhile, in Redmond there are no problem with making choices even if they are quite rotten choices.

One of the reasons Microsoft made great headways was their MSDN (Microsoft Developer Network) program, which enabled developers that were close to the customer base to help the end user. MSDN supplied tools, training and listened to developer feedback(I used to be a member). Visual Basic development was a poster child for the success of this program.

Ubuntu with its great Python support is almost there. A Python developers option of Ubuntu installation would put all the pieces in place. Once the developer base is expanded beyond the computer geeks (BTW I am one - 20 years experience in C/C++ and assembler before that) then the lever is in place to move the world to Linux.

---

### Post by oliwally on 2006-01-17
I've been using different distros for the last 3 or so years now, simply because my brother is a computer-head and mentioned linux to me. He also told me it would be easy...... well, I'm not sure if I agree with that.

I have no formal computer training. Just an ordinary windows user who knows a touch more than Joe Average and who likes to fiddle with computers. I have made Linux, and particularly K/Ubuntu (which in my opinion is the most user-friendly so far), work on my computer(s) by asking lots of questions on forums. Most questions have been answered by very helpful individuals solving my problems for me. I don't actually always understand what it is they tell me to do. I just do it and things start working. Yes, if I had the time I might even educate myself about the hows and whys, but really, most times I just want the computer or application to work -  I don't really care how or why (although I do enjoy fiddling with computers and getting them to do what I want). I suspect that most average computer users have that same attitude - just make the computer / program work, don't waste my time by asking me to configure this or that. Of course, most people reading this would disagree with that attitude, but then we're on this forum because we have a deeper interest in computers than most people. That's why I'm here. I enjoy playing with it. It's a hobby.

Most of my friends have never heard about Linux. So when they ask me if it's ok to use and if it's easy to use, I have to be honest - unless all you want to do is internet, email, word-processing, install it only if you're prepared to spend some time working it out and solving some problems. No, it's not as easy and straightforward as windows. Use it only if you want to make it a bit of a hobby. Don't use it if you simply want a working computer that gives you relatively little trouble. (Yes, I can hear the boos and hisses - Windows does give us troubles, but not too often if you're just doing standard stuff). Installing some programs can be a right-royal pain in the butt in linux. You have to find the version for your distro (if you can find it) and then rather than double-clicking on the installation file, you have to open a shell window and ....... most of my friends turn around and walk away at that point. Synaptic (although good) is not always the answer either because the repositories may not have the current version of the app you want. 

Even just on that point - repositories. Try explaining what that is to your average windows end user. Explain what they do, why linux users need them and how they are used, and watch their reaction. Be honest now - they will give you a blank stare and ask 'why bother with all that? just use windows and find a program, double click and away you go'

Then there are the sound issues in Linux. I've installed some midi software recently and then tried to make it work. Not something totally out of the ordinary - just wanted to be able to listen to and edit some midi files. It took me hours and hours of research, forums and configuring this file and that. Far out - in windows I installed similar software in 5 minutes and had no problems with anything. Everything worked. 

I'm sorry but in my opinion Linux is just not ready and easy enough for most people. Yes, I know - it's what people get used to and at present they're used to windows. But you can't argue with a 5 minute installation of software in windows, compared to a 5-day problem-solving exercise to install similar app in Linux. There just seems to always be 'some' issue in Linux. There is always something that doesn't quite work as it should. For example - after working well for weeks, suddenly one sound chanel does not work when I boot into Kubuntu. I have to re-start the sound server to bring it back to normal. This just never happens on my windows computer, and if it does, I simply re-install a driver or a program.

I don't know too much about computers, but I thought Linux would be more user-friendly by now. I obviously don't understand all the issues involved, but I really would have thought that Linux would be further evolved already (and that some of the apps would have slicker interfaces). Perhaps I'm being unrealistic and hoping for too much.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-01-21
[quote=oliwally]I'm sorry but in my opinion Linux is just not ready and easy enough for most people. Yes, I know - it's what people get used to and at present they're used to windows. But you can't argue with a 5 minute installation of software in windows, compared to a 5-day problem-solving exercise to install similar app in Linux. There just seems to always be 'some' issue in Linux. There is always something that doesn't quite work as it should. For example - after working well for weeks, suddenly one sound chanel does not work when I boot into Kubuntu. I have to re-start the sound server to bring it back to normal. This just never happens on my windows computer, and if it does, I simply re-install a driver or a program.[/quote] In other words, Linux distros don't come preinstalled on most people's computers they buy.

[QUOTE=erlyrisa]
Why did'nt I try to migrate to linux earlier -- I didn't have time. - when it comes to just getting everything up and running and using in under a day (with partitions, plenty of hardware and a network of let's say 3 comps) well then Windows is still a fore runner.[/QUOTE] In other words, Linux distros don't come preinstalled on most people's computers they buy.

Don't lie. Windows isn't easy to install and configure. I've installed Windows *from scratch* (not restore disks that came with the computer). It's not easy, especially when you don't know what driver to use or where to find it from a reliable source. Ubuntu recognized all my hardware straightaway. All I had to do was add a couple of lines to my /etc/X11/xorg.conf file to get my optimal monitor resolution.

It's such a double standard.

Windows comes preinstalled and preconfigured and people think it's easier. Uh, duh! Of course, it's easier. Someone else did the work *for you*.

Something goes wrong with Windows, "Oh, that's odd."
Something goes wrong with Linux, "Yeah, it's not ready for ordinary users yet."

The God-honest truth: if you don't have time to install an operating system, don't install an operating system--I don't care if it's Windows *or* Linux. And if you don't have time to learn a new way of doing things, don't use a new operating system--I don't care if it's Linux or Mac OS X.

     My lady friend watches my struggles and sez "Why would I ever want to go thru what you are suffering in
trying to get Linux up. ..  When my W$-ME works most of the time.??"   [ Of course she has not counted the number

     When I got 10 copies of Ubuntu, I thought " A ha... now we shall see an out-of-the-box easy install and up and running with all features needed for a good user.   NUTS.!

### Post by facefur on 2006-01-21
I'm someone who has given Linux as fair a shake as any one could, and I'm stil not really happy with it.  Don't get me wrong, if you are installing to a clean HD, it works fine, and the Ubuntu GUI is the best of any other Linux distro I've tried (Red Hat, Madrake, Gentoo).  The basic office automation (Open Office), games, editors, etc. are fine - maybe better than Windows.  

I just tried to do a clean install of Breeze Badger.  I backed up my /home partition, inserted the CD, and it was off and running....until I hit the partitioner.  Now, I spent some time during the Hoary Hedgehog installation to set up my machine for dual boot, and created the '/', '/boot', '/usr', and '/home' and 'sawp' partitions based on recommendations.  This new installer will NOT let me just keep them and just install the new software required for 5.10...it even said so.  The partitioner gives me totally different names for the partitions, and just left me so confused that I just quit the installation process entirely.

I have enough experience to grasp disk partitioning, dual booting, the Linux file system, etc., and I'm just pissed at this moment.  I should NOT have to go through this with a real-world grade operating system intended for the average computer joe - who would be completely lost.  This is NOT about pre-installed vs. fresh install.  I've done Windows installs and reinstalls many times, and neither process is a piece of cake, but every Windows install has succeeded.  I cannot say the same for Linux.  During my early Mandrake days, I almost put my install CD's into the shredder until someone finally let me in on the "noapic" secret.

Now, that's one reason why Linux is not ready for prime time with the rest of the world.

A second reason is that most of the Really Good Applications (tm) are built for Windows, and do not exist for Linux.  For example, there are no equivalent apps like Quicken (no, gnuCash is not it's equal.)  My music composition program, Noteworthy Composer, still has no equivalent (I've looked at the Linux versions).  TuboCAD, an excellent CA program, has not been, and likely will not be ported  because it's strongly tied to the Windows COM/DCOM libraries.  I have found no sustitute for Print Shop, a really handy desktop publishing/ poster/flyer/card program.  There might be some of these out htere, but I have no clue where to start searching, and don't particularly want to.

Thirdly (then I'll shut up), I bought myself a very nice HP DesignJet 100 printer for CAD work.  Imagine how disappointed I was to discover when I really took Linux seriously that HP does not support Linux for it, and does not intend to.  I had someone on a newsgroup tell me how stupid I was not to check whether it would work with Linux.  After three days of following leads in the LinuxPrinting web site, I finally realized that my only hope is to activate an old Windows box as a print server, and pray that I can figure out how to use Samba to connect the Linux machine to the printer.  That's a "not ready for prime time" solutio to my problem.  

I still spend 60% of my time with Ubuntu as the running OS, because a lot of my time is on the browser or email client - which are just as capable as anything in Windows, but it will NOT be my "real OS" until I can stop fighting with it for anything beyond "surfing the web."

You might not like what I have said, but you need to take it seriously if the community has any hope of bringing Linux into real competition with Bill Gates.

---

### Post by rusty61 on 2006-01-27
i think that more than anything, people are just used to windows and don't like the idea of change. when you get comfortable with one thing, it's hard to make the switch over to something new. otherwise i'm not really sure why it isn't more widespread.

### Post by promet on 2006-01-28
I think that the single biggest problem is ease of configurability. Linux is, in my opinion, far superior to windows. I think for the average end user though, getting the little customizations right (sound, browser plugins, unusual hardware setups, etc.) that make your OS feel "homey", are terribly difficult for your average windows user.

There are many different competing systems, apps, servers etc. competing for prominence in the Linux "universes". Now, to a true computer enthusiast who has a more than casual interest in hacking core system customizations, this is a wonderful thing. To someone, though, who just wants to install their OS and play a new DVD; this is precisely the type of thing that gives them gray hairs.

Sure, they could hack it into shape, but then they'll have to get into the dark world of Linux sound server configuration, apt-get/synaptic syntax and methods, win32 codec idiosyncrasies; and so on.

I love to tinker with computers and learning more about hardware, software and the real bare-bones intricacies and even I sometimes say to myself, "man, this just should not be this difficult".

I, luckily though, appreciate the amount of time, effort and ingenuity that have gone into bringing Linux to the point where it is now; and, as I say, it is, essentially, far superior to windows (and if one doesn't understand that, I think that one doesn't actually understand computer technology all that well).

Bottom line? Linux is an incredibly powerful tool and a beautiful thing,  but its front end usability learning curve is simply too steep for the "vanilla" user. The root (literally ;) ) level control Linux allows for the x86 platform is, IMHO, unsurpassed and is seriously only limited by one's imagination. 

Let's face it though, most people want a supremely simple level of plug-and-play and configurability and while Linux is imminently capable of this; the complex and widely varied development process for Linux often cannot readily provide this; at this point. I believe that will, however, happen one day soon. I think that all of our interest and concern will make that a certainty. Hey Bill Gates, you're fired...

---

### Post by kewl1uk on 2006-02-04
People are just too scared to install it because they're not familiar with it. For example, my son loves my Ubuntu installation and his son (my grandson) loves the games. I tell him to install it and he says he'll have to learn how to use it and the programs all over again. I tell him he can have it on the same disk as windows, dual boot and get used to it gradually and he says it might do something bad to Windows and in any case dual booting is too inconvenient. And so on.

Basically it's all very well to have a desktop which provides an easy transition from Windows to Linux. But the programs they'll run are different and they really do have to learn all over again. The other problem is familiarity: It's one thing for us who are already using Linux to explain how easy it is to use but it's another to accept it as an alternative to something already familiar with.

### Post by njmf on 2006-02-08
Well, all what are you complaining about is games, some 3rd party stuff, etc...
I would add one big 'setback' - proffesional audio engineering! There is not enough (one can hardly tell there is at all) professional music production tools for Linux, as there is for Windows and Mac. Consider this situation (perfectly normal situation): proffesional music engineer uses his WindowsXP machine and is used to pro sound apps like Nuendo, Cubase or ProTools. Does he need to get working on Linux just because it is more stable/secure/whatever OS? Because it is OpenSource? There are thousands of VST plugins and pro-audio apps for Windows and Mac which just won't be ported to Linux - there is large background on that. Steinberg has no need to write OR allow porting of ASIO (or VST or anything else) system on Linux, he has his market in Windows/Mac world. It is technology which has been developed for maybe 15 years on Windows and surely longer for Mac, and won't be easily turned to work on Linux.

Well, this is just an example, what did I try to say is that there is not enough proffesional multimedia support for Linux - not just sound, but Video engineering, too. There are some alternatives in this domain, but that just isn't enough. Blender ([www.blender.org](www.blender.org)) might be a Linuxes breakthrough in 3Dmodelling/rendering/video post-production world because it is extraordinary designed app, but I doubt that there will be good enough music production support in long time.

So, a bit offtopic, but the only things that hold me in Windows now are sound production and (temporarily, because of shitty modem) internet.

### Post by RagingOcelot on 2006-02-09

I don't think he's got the time or space here to explain why he thinks Windows is better.  Maybe he's too busy trying to get a driver to work or to prevent his X server from crashing all the time or refusing to load up in the first place.  Linux is seeming more and more to me to be some sort of wet dream for tinkerers and just a hassle for people who actually want to create something with the software or God forbid, just listen to a song.  Speaking of audio, why are there so many sound systems?  What's the point?  Can a certain band of people just work on getting one universal sound system working first before they rebel and start working on another one?  Does the Linux world have too many rebels, perhaps?  

I come from the gooey, slip-slide of a world of OS X.  I can listen to music and wait to hear instant messages - at the same time!  Wild.

I'd love to see my tablet not crash my system after I unplug it.  It'd be great to not have to shout on my microphone so that people on Skype or Gizmo can hear me.  I really would like to break completely free from the proprietary OS world, but the lack of support/interest in Linux is making that idea pretty difficult to fathom.

### Post by Alapelli on 2006-02-09
Basically peoples don't or won't take time to learn something new. They want what Window offer them: An Icon, I click on it and the program start.  Some 
peoples don't even want to learn more than that.  These are the first segment
of the population who use computer as a basis.  Internet browsing, being the 
main attraction for those peoples to the computers.

The second segment want to learn how those machine work and are ready to 
spend some time and will be ready to go further in their learning. IE: Learning
tab browsing in Firefox.  They have an idea of what is a Hard drive, but don't
ask them to find it on the Window GUI.  Ask them the usefullness of doing a 
defrag on a regular basis.  Not sure that you will get a lot of computer who 
will know the answer.

The 2 previous groups represent maybee 60 to 70 % of the users, so unless 
you provide them with a no brainer system like Xandros or Linspire, no way will they want to switch to Linux and even then.

For the remaining who would like to learn the Linux desktop, well most of the
documentation is for the System Administrator.  At this point I'm not ready 
nor interested in running a Server.  So were do I start to get a basic info
on Linux Desktop utilisation.  Altough looked a lot in Linux supportive computer store, didn't really find much documentation specific to the 
Desktops.   I would really like to find a "Basic" structured course on that 
would allow me to get a better understanding of the Shell.  Along with 
the specified lack of game and printer adaptability. Represent for some 
would be Linux user, a major stumble that discourage them on going further
in learning Linux.  ( Feel free to write me if you have any book or course 
suggestions )  

To conclude after trying many Distro. Ubuntu is the one that has the most
lively Forum, I was amazed to find that actually some questions were answered within 5 minutes.  I also liked the FAQ that allowed me to 
practice the basic by installing Applications.  But unless there is more
"Basic" how-to book and other starting basis on how to use Linux, well
the user are going to be the same many years from now.  People who
already have a medium to expert level expertise of Computer, but the 
Mr & Mrs Everybody won't have adopted Linux.

### Post by knives on 2006-02-09
For a start:
1. General computer-using population is used to Windows because "Its Just There". Changes is a scary thing for people, I know my folks nearly *aherm* themselves when they looked upon the creation known today as Ubuntu.

2.People are also ignorant. They think that "because its linux I won't be able to share stuff with my friends because they willl still be using windows".

3.The Games Market. Commercial games are almost exclusively built for windows OS, which is why most people who could probably tackle Linux don't.
Sure, there are emulators etc, etc, but they are still a work in progress and the newest games don't always work instantly through them. It's also why many Linux users still have a windows version running alongside Linux on their machine.

4.People are also greedy. There is no profit to be made in giving out copies of Linux, which I know is half the beauty of it, but, like I said, people are greedy and want money.

5.Bill Gates once slept with Satan for a night so now we have to wait for him to die before the pact is complete and the world is free of the curse created by this horrific event.:twisted: 

So, with all these in mind, I would say that the BIGGEST setback is not a fault on the part of Linux, but on a part of the world's population. There is nothing that Windows can do that Linux can't (Games included if they were developed for Linux), so I place the blame on the ignorance and the crudeness of "Human Nature". I know this is probably not where the thread was meant to go, for that I apologise.
I guess that makes my answer "other".

### Post by jumbodavid on 2006-02-10
The main reason linux has not caught on is well known to everbody in the linux commuity
Compared to windows, any application is usually good ie open office 2  V microsoft office Ms office is about £400 these days and does the same thing
It is the operating on a day to day basis for non -geeks
Example I tried to get a new printer installed Epsom DX4200
I had the help of a Ph D in Maths who works for Cisco
A guy who works for a firm that make computer systems in military helicopters
And TWO editors of rival linux magazines
After several hours and three bottles of wine it was installed
Two days laer trying to print a smple text email it crashed ( I did nothing illigal or silly)
The screen froze and the machine would not let me shut down
When I rebooted the printer didn't work
Untill it is a system that the man or women in the street can use, with a back up system ie a phone line were you can talk to someone, for a fee ie £1 a minute
it wiil not take off
I am one of those people who it is about time we had a real alterative to widows

Just for infomation I have run mandrake, I now run umbuto
I have used  Open office for all accounts, tax returns etc for 7 years, I also use thunderbird and firefox and all are good on umbuto so I am  not just  a stroppy  Brit
If you could only make linux more user friendly and stop saying it is ok for the ordinary desktop user, when it is not, it would take off

### Post by aysiu on 2006-02-10
[QUOTE=jumbodavid]
If you could only make linux more user friendly and stop saying it is ok for the ordinary desktop user, when it is not, it would take off[/QUOTE] A few responses to this:

1. Who's *you*? Do you really think Ubuntu developers read threads like this? They're too busy improving Ubuntu to be reading your (to be truthful) not-very-constructive criticisms. Hell, even a regular forum member doesn't have time to read all the pages in this thread (see a couple of posts above this one).

2. How exactly would Linux "take off" if people stop saying it's for ordinary users. Please explain.

3. When you say it's not "for the ordinary desktop user," what do you mean? 

If you mean I can't hand a Ubuntu installer disk to someone and say, "Here, plop this on to a blank computer and install it and configure it yourself. I guarantee it'll work right away," well, of course not. You can't say the same for Windows either. I can't hand a Windows installer CD to someone and say, "Here, plop this on a blank computer and install it and configure it yourself. I guarantee it'll work right away."

If you mean an expert or company (like Dell) installs and configures it, an ordinary user can't point-and-click to use common applications like a word processor, photo organizer, web browser, and email client, then I would say you're dead-wrong and haven't used any Linux distribution except those dedicated to server use.

### Post by Paloseco on 2006-02-13
I think that above FUDs and opinions, there are few thing that definitely help linux not to suceed:

1) Most of distros are not commercial, so developers do not earn money directly. With commercial ones, like suse, you pay the closed software and patents, not developers work.

2) There are lots of distros, each one with their package management. Even more, there are different graphical environments and each distro tries to cover lots of architectures. That is a hell for users. GNU/Linux efforts are weak and scattered, not like the Windows ones, where EVERYONE works for the same. That makes Windows be years fast from linux in mosts aspects.

3) The support for networking is really bad (not the hardware drivers, but the GUIs for configuring and running interfaces). Actually there are 6 or 7 programs that pretend to be the definitive wireless managers (pywireless, wireless assistant, network manager, netapplet, etc...), but no one results efficient in term of usability, easy of configuration and kind of connection that can manage.

### Post by OtrMan on 2006-02-13
I see two big reasons.  1.  Software installation is very difficult and often doesn't yield a working package.  The only way to get it working is to be a computer geek.   2. The focus of Ubuntu is to treat the person at the keyboard as an intruder; making them sudo just about everything.  But securing the internet with out of the box solutions is just an afterthought.

A shift of attitude is needed.  Make it user friendly and intruder unfriendly.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-02-13
[QUOTE=OtrMan]I see two big reasons.  1.  Software installation is very difficult and often doesn't yield a working package.  The only way to get it working is to be a computer geek.[/quote] This is not true at all. Any idiot can install software with Synaptic Package Manager or Add Applications. It's only when you go outside the repositories that software installation can become difficult. For people with simple needs (people like me), software installation is actually *easier* in Ubuntu than in Windows.

> 2. The focus of Ubuntu is to treat the person at the keyboard as an intruder; making them sudo just about everything.  But securing the internet with out of the box solutions is just an afterthought.

A shift of attitude is needed.  Make it user friendly and intruder unfriendly. This isn't true. Ubuntu secures the internet out of the box, *and* it secures your system as well. What's wrong with *sudoing* everything that makes **system-wide changes**? Mac OS X does that, too, and nobody complains that it's not user-friendly.

### Post by StueyB on 2006-02-14
I installed Ubuntu last night and ok, it was easy enough to get the base install going. However I decided to install arnieboys tweak kit.

Ok for an above average windows user, it would be difficult (not being patronising) because in Windows, you just **wouldnt** have to do this. And also in Windows in the base install you dont get "credits" etc on the installation screen of what should be a core part of the OS.

One other thing I noticed was that, to update core OS parts and simple tweaks it was pulling files from all over the place via wget. In Microsoft world, you just get EVERYTHING to run a base system with a heavyweight media player installed   and no need to go to all these diverse repositories.

Im not having a dig, im just saying it how I see it.

On the plus site I started to use "Bluefish" instead of MX2004 and it made me realise just how poor the quality of the outputted code in MX2004 is ! I managed to reduce my code by at least 20 lines per page on average because it really does suffer code bloat.

Time will tell how much this rocks but Im happy at the moment, having previously installed Ubuntu and gone back to Windows.

Lol the other thing that isn't a criticism, just a comment is, once its all configured its like "ok, what do I do now" ;)

Stuart

### Post by ahood on 2006-02-14
I admit, I have not read all of the replies to this post. I commend ThirdWorld for posting this thread. It is a very good one.

IMHO there are many factors holding back linux, but in the end I can think of 2 that I feel are significant.....

_(1) Lack of Marketing (publicity)_ - I don't see linux commercials on TV or radio. I don't see **desktop** linux ads in print media, although there might be some if one looks closely. The number of Windows magazines far outnumber linux magazines. 

_(2) OEM Support_ - computers don't come with linux installed by default. This puts widespread adoption of linux at a huge disadvantage. 

I like what I am seeing with Novell's work with the Xgl and the **eye-popping** eyecandy. The 3D features I see are just fantastic and I can hardly wait for these to become available in the repositories. 

Can the Xgl 3D work be the killer app that linux has been waiting? It could be, but I am concerned that it will be a missed opportunity because I believe that there needs to be a huge marketing effort to publicize the greatness of linux, especially since users have to make an extra effort of downloading it and installing it themselves (i.e., linux doesn't come pre-installed).

Dr. Hood

### Post by ahood on 2006-02-14
[QUOTE=Paloseco]GNU/Linux is still not ready for mass installation. Is not so easy as windows, speacially with the packages management and networking. Let gnu/linux be oem unsupported till it gets better.[/QUOTE]

Paloseco,

I hear you. Users experience with installation can be unpredictable given that hardware is so varied and hardware vendor support for linux is patchy (i.e., not widespread).

This is precisely why OEM support is needed. Rather than having users download and install linux on their system, which may have incompatible hardware, let the computer manufacturer put together a system with components known to work and install an easy to use linux distribution. Ultimately, saving the 'average joe' computer user the trouble. All they care about is getting a functional machine that is stable, don't have to worry about viruses, spyware, pop-ups, that will browse the web, get e-mail, write a letter, etc.

Just for the record, my personal belief is that linux is as easy to install as any other operating system available.

Regards,

Dr. Hood

---

### Post by ahood on 2006-02-14
[QUOTE=Paloseco]There are some basics that I consider mandatory for home users. I do not mean to be better to vista or macos in EVERY aspect. What I consider neccesary to be "better" are things that probably will be implemented in the near future. For example, I do not consider XGL a priority, although is a good enhacement as a visual effect it is. :-k[/QUOTE]

Paloseco,

IMHO, I anticipate that the Ubuntu developers would agree with you on this given the fact that I suspect that the fancy eye candy recently showcased by Novell (i.e., Xgl) will require proprietary 3D video drivers (Nvidia or ATI). Thus, these proprietary drivers won't get installed by default in Ubuntu, thus, it seems logical that Xgl and the 3D eye candy won't be installed at default either.

I personally would argue that the Xgl implementation I have seen is more than just insignificant eye candy. What I have seen are 3D effects that effectively extends the workspace available to the user in a way that can be grasped and valued immediately. I am referring specifically to the 3D effect that transforms the Desktop from a single plane surface (i.e., a sheet of paper) to a virtual cube that resides inside the monitor, and each side of the cube represents additional workspace. This has a huge potential to significantly change the way a computer users 'sees' the Desktop that they will want this capability.

I have been using linux for 5 years and I have not had much use of virtual desktops that all linux distributions provide. I have used both KDE and Gnome and virtual desktops went unused, until recently. 

About a 10 days ago, I installed the 3ddesk application, plus xbindkeys, which allows me to interact with the virtual desktops in a 3D way. This one application has had a significant impact on the value of virtual desktops. Since I installed 3ddesk, I now use all 4 virtual desktops every day all the time. I could not go back to not using 3ddesk. Well, I could, but it would be sorely missed. Based on the videos I have seen, I believe that the effect of Xgl on how users interact with virtual desktops will be many times greater than 3ddesk (not to take anything away from 3ddesk because it is a good app).

My 2 cents worth (hehe).

Dr. Hood

### Post by tweetybird on 2006-02-14
I've read a few pages of the replies to this post.
The first thing I noted is most of the posts are from Linux users.
You want to know why? - you need to ask Windows users.
I am one, so let me put my two cents in.

You need to know something about me first I think.   Have been computing since the first home computer available, the Texas Instruments TI64, I believe it was called.  Then on to the Commodore and up to now.  Was a gamer big time back then, so had to upgrade or just plain get a new computer about once every 9-12 months at the most for many years.  Current system is the oldest I have ever had, it's now two years old except for the video card, which is maybe 8 months old.  I also spent two years doing tech support a few years ago - a year for Dell and one for MS.

The number one issue of why Linux is not on more desktops is (if I listen to my friends who call me with their MS problems) they have never heard of it.

The number two issue - and always has been for the ones of us aware of Linux - which of the hundred, no that was 5 years ago, now it's two hundred, nope 2 years ago, now three hundred or so (who's counting) flavors should I try?

The number three issue and I would say this is definitely the biggest one so far - driver support for the latest devices hitting the street.  The people out there that would give Linux a shot are the gamers and techies that are building their own systems and need/want the latest greatest hardware.  You know the kind, The guy that just spent $400-$500 on the newest video card, he wants it to work.  He's willing to put up with some bugs at first, but he wants it to basically work.  Just spent $300 on the latest sound card so she could have 7.1 sound, she wants 7.1 sound.  I put a HDTV card in the machine, I want HDTV.  I don't want to spend the next 3 months reading and studying about HDTV, how the card is made, how the card functions, how it's broadcast and all the technical gobbledygook, I just want that HDTV picture on my monitor.  Just bought the newest mobo and new SATA drives to set up a RAID array for video stuff, I want my RAID.  If it wasn't for items like the these, I would have switched to Linux a number of years ago.

I never have understood all the reviews on Linux OS's.  Why do they install it to a machine with specs that I gave away 4 or 5 years ago.  I want to know if it works on the system I have now, not how well it works on my 3rd or 4th system back.  What's with this anyway? 

My first foray into Linux was maybe a year or so after DVD players hit - and no DVD support in Linux at that time at all, well I used mine a lot, and definitely couldn't play my games in Linux.  So, couldn't see any reason to boot back and forth between 2 OS's.

Another time - no TV card support - I have had DirecTV hooked up to my computer and been using it as a PVR for about 7 or 8 years now.  Then take the recorded files, edit out the commercials and burn them to DVD to build my own collection.  You ever try to use a Linux version of a DVD editor from even 3 years ago, you'd almost have to be an expert in how DVD's are made to be able to set the parameters to use the software.

Another time, had system st up with a SATA RAID array, guess what - no SATA RAID support in Linux at all at that time.

I use to try out Linux about once every 9 months or so.  This time it's been since right after I built my current system.    You can't ask the question on any forums about which one to try because you get answers like, "Whichever one works for you" (like a typical computer user wants to experiment with half a dozen different OS's just to find one that will support 1/2 his hardware, or as if that kind of response helps anyone that is even contemplating giving Linux a try) or "Suse is the best", "Mandriva blows them all away", "Linspire is great for newbies to Linux", "No, not Linspire, that's not a real Linux, they make you pay for all the stuff you can get for free with all the others", etc., etc. etc.........

So, I began like I always do, spent about two weeks just reading reviews about all the top versions in order to pick one out of the few hundred that are out there.  With the decision to try some I had not before.  This time (due to the reviews and what appears to be a pretty good forum) gave PCLinuxOS a shot with the LiveCD.  So, download the iso and burn it to CD (I would say, 95% of the people that call me for help with their MS systems would not even know how to do this).  

Get booted up and no screen that I can see, back to the forums and another couple hours of reading (right here is where half the MS users are going to give up).  Find out how to do a command line boot to get booted in vesa mode.  Hey now I've got a screen. Go in to try and set the resolution for my monitor (Dell's 2001FP) and I can get it to 1600x1200 but no dice on setting the refresh rate to one supported by the monitor.  Put in a music CD next, what do you know, no sound either.  Well, I'm not phased, not yet,  since the first thing I do when installing Windows is go out and grab the latest available drivers for all my hardware (except I've gotten a little smarter over the years, I download 'em to a separate disk before I do an install). No printer nor scanner either.  I'm still not phased.  Nice looking desktop and it found my high speed connection and am on the internet, so I figure lets install it to diskwhere I can tweak and get the right drivers installed and give it a good test.

1st install - no LILO, back to the forums, download and read the install instructions tow more times - 2nd install - LILO is ther now, but it won't boot into PCLinux, won't boot into Windows either - back to forums, do a search first on what's happening so I don't get the infamous RTFM (famous line for you Linux folks, come to think of it, maybe this should be in first place on the list), spend about 2 hours reading, can't find anything that helps me out so pose the question, get a couple responses, though not all that helpful, but enough to try 3rd install - no go, same issue.  This time I find posts that have my same screenshot, and multiple suggestions.  Come to find out it's some kind of issue with SATA drives that happens "sometimes", I'm just one of the lucky "sometimes".  Not one post with the same issue ever had a response back indicating issue resolved.  The posts all just died.  Now, with several hundred installs of Windows, have only had one occasion where I couldn't at least get into the OS and that one was due to some bad Ram I had bought to go with my first Win95 install.  Right here is where Linux is going to lose 99.5% of all the ones who are willing to give it a shot.

But no, not me.  I usually don't give up on Linux until I've got at least a hundred or so hours in it.  So here I am with Ubuntu loaded on my machine.  I must say no help to the "Official" install guide.  If it wasn't for posting a question about my partition setup and having someone point the way to an un-official guide, I never would have found how to set the swap partition.  I will say that this version does seem to recognize my vidoe card to some degree and auto configured my screen resolution correctly.  Don't know about the refresh rate, it says 0hz and won't let me change it.  Also, have sound - pretty good.  About a one minute search on the net and found what setting I could use to get my unsupported Canon printer to work.  Not bad.  Found out about Automatix reading the beginner forums and gave that a try.  Pretty awesome.  Rist thing I usually do an a fresh install is load the latest drivers for my vid card - currently ATI X800 XL - still have yet to find directions that this old Windows user can understand.  Looks like I'm going to have to read for another week or two, or break down and go buy me a 500 page Linux book just to install video drivers.

So there you have it, the experience of this windows user.  I haven't given up this time, yet.  But I will tell you if I can't update my video drivers, get my TV, HDTV and PVR working, edit and create DVD's from the mpeg files, get my dual monitors set up, get my scanner working.... within another couple of weeks I'll be back to using only windows again.  Maybe next time I build a system I'll look for the best hardware for my money that also works with Linux, instead of just the best hardware for the money.

---

### Post by ahood on 2006-02-15
Hey Tweetybird,

Wow! What persistence, which is great to read about. Just a couple of points I thought worth mentioning....


[QUOTE=tweetybird]I've read a few pages of the replies to this post.
The first thing I noted is most of the posts are from Linux users.
You want to know why? - you need to ask Windows users.
I am one, so let me put my two cents in.[/quotes]

I think it is fairly safe to say that most Ubuntu/linux users are/were Windows users.

> The number one issue of why Linux is not on more desktops is (if I listen to my friends who call me with their MS problems) they have never heard of it.

In general, this is true. However, over the past 6 months I have met people who have at least heard of linux that was truly unexpected. These people were sales people at the local store, people I talk to in a restaurant, and co-workers.

> The number two issue - and always has been for the ones of us aware of Linux - which of the hundred, no that was 5 years ago, now it's two hundred, nope 2 years ago, now three hundred or so (who's counting) flavors should I try?

I agree with this one up to a point. IMHO, I think it is important that the underlying kernel and availability of hardware drivers are the same between various distributions. Why should all distributions look the same? Why can't there be uniqueness to each distribution? IMHO, the numerous linux distributions is its strengths. Users actually have a choice! Yes, there is more responsibility of the user to think about his/her needs, but hey, we are not created equal. Our individual needs differ and we all don't think alike. We are humans, that is, we are capable of learning and adapting to a particular distribution that each of us like. 

> The number three issue and I would say this is definitely the biggest one so far - driver support for the latest devices hitting the street.  

I think it is safe to say that improved hardware vendor support is on all of our wish lists.

> So there you have it, the experience of this windows user.  I haven't given up this time, yet.  But I will tell you if I can't update my video drivers, get my TV, HDTV and PVR working, edit and create DVD's from the mpeg files, get my dual monitors set up, get my scanner working.... within another couple of weeks I'll be back to using only windows again.  Maybe next time I build a system I'll look for the best hardware for my money that also works with Linux, instead of just the best hardware for the money.

No, you certainly haven't given up and I encourage you not to give up. Here are a couple of things to keep in mind....

(1) Coming to grips with linux takes longer than a couple of weeks. It  may take years to really become proficient in linux. It all depends on the distribution you choose and your ambitions in proficiency.

(2) I think many Windows users stop using linux because they have the expectation that linux will work just like Windows, use the same programs as they did in Windows, use the same hardware as they did in linux, etc. IMHO, this expectation is grandios and almost certainly will result in failure. This not to say that a user needs isn't important, but it must be recognized that linux cannot to everything and isn't for everybody. It is just that to be successful with linux, the user recognizes its strengths and capitalizes on them.

(3) One thing I have learned about linux is that users must try and see if it works. There aren't going to be any guarantees and there shouldn't be an expectation of too much hand holding. After all, this is free stuff. Just try it and see if it works. If it doesn't, not much was lost and lot was learned.

Good luck.

Dr. Hood

### Post by N8MAN1068 on 2006-02-15
this is one I thought about on the way home:
Suite integration.

You buy XP. You buy Office2k3. Everything clicks together. Mailmerges, importing contacts, right click-add to desktop/send to/etc, MS Money/Accounting, etc.

I've got a over dozen programs I just installed to help my wife learn how to build a web page. I've only found 1 in the Applications list, and it's in the wrong place. The others? I have no freaking clue where they went. I'm sure I'll have to search my drive for them, and manually create a launcher.:-k 

However, since my work WSUS server reported about 4 different security updates this week, I'll stay Ubuntu. [-( 

I'll leave my feelings on the term 'Project' out of this post. Well...maybe not. 
No businesses, unless it's yours, will allow a 'project' application to be installed as a core application. Ever. Unless they don't care about downtime. When I select an application that I think can benefit a business group, I want to know that if 3 years down the road, I run into an issue, I'll have support, and not have to worry if the guy/gal who wrote the app. graduated college, got a job/married/kids and can no longer maintain it.

### Post by lummoxcooties on 2006-04-07
I feel that the biggest reason is **ease of use**. Not many people are willing to install an OS, especially if they are unfamiliar with it. I am new to ubuntu, and was concerned about getting a dual-boot to run  smoothly, but it was easier than I thought. And Ubuntu is considered easy, so most other distros would be more of a challenge. Unfortunately, my mom or any non-technical computer user still would have trouble, and the majority of people are not computer savvy. These people would not know where to start if they wanted to reinstall XP. The only reason Windows stays on top is because they are pre-installed when you buy a computer. (Re)Installing Windows is not extremely difficult, but is comparable to installing Ubuntu. 

The second reason is definitely 3rd party support. If all windows programs, cameras, and everything else could just be installed without having to install some sort of windows emulator, then people would be flocking to keep their already-paid-for and switch to linux.

Although I am not very familiar with linux, I think it has big potential.

### Post by SHodges on 2006-04-07
Anyone who voted/seriously defended microsoft's business model as a viable answer is, quite frankly, a deluded fool.  Linux doesn't reach the mainstream because it isn't preinstalled on more machines, and considering how much lower the price of a pc would be with linux installed, and how many more they would sell to new customers, it isn't that they're somehow "biased" against linux either.  Linux does **not** run the software that a lot of people want.  Linux is **not** user friendly, not even Ubuntu.  And please, none of that "well it's user friendly to ME" crap, frankly, no one gives a damn what's user friendly to a long time user of linux who is enough of a computer geek to post on a forum about it.  You aren't the grandma that just wants to double click and install something, you aren't the college kid who wants his wireless network to just work with his new laptop, you aren't the average user.  Typing sudo sh and a directory that a lot of people don't know how to get (and they shouldn't have to either before you come in calling 99% of the world "stupid" compared to yourself) isn't easy, it isn't even the least bit intuitive, it just works like crap compared to 'point and click'.  Linux doesn't run popular software, which means it has a limited *potential* marketshare compared to windows, Linux doesn't just work out of the box for a lot of people, which means tech support costs would go up by quite a bit at the very least, and even it's vaunted protection from viruses and malware would disappear into thin air once business picked up.  So yeah, Linux has **huge**, fundamental problems that keep it out of the hands of the average user.  To be honest, I'd say the biggest problem with linux in general would be the community itself.  You can't have community driven development of a product and expect it to be a smashing success if the community is filled with people who want to be "leet" and bash Microsoft for any frivolous reason that crosses their mind, considers any user that has problems with the very imperfect way linux does things "stupid", and just generally ignores or shifts the blame for any problem that linux has.  An easier method of installing progams from the net alone would go a long, long way.

### Post by SHodges on 2006-04-07
[QUOTE=aysiu]SHodges,

Here is a list of your faulty assumptions:[/QUOTE]
And the "refusing to admit anything is wrong in the first place" begins.  

1. Linux users have all been using Linux for a long time and are all computer wizards.[/QUOTE]
Just dumb.  Linux users have a reputation as computer wizards and geeks for a reason.  If you even CONSIDER using linux, chances are you know more about computers than your average user.  If you would dispute *this*, something that is generally accepted fact, then wow.  Have fun in fairy tale land, and have fun trying to get anything meaningfull done with Linux's development while you're there.  Linux isn't built with the average computer user in mind, and until it is, it's not going anywhere on the market.

> 2. Average users would need to *sudo* and whatnot if Linux came preinstalled.
If they'd like to use their computer at all, they're going to want to install things.  Go look at the install process for an ATI driver for instance, just to get your computer working the way it should.  Delete this, compile that, terminal commands everywhere, it's a mess, and if you have any intention of being used by average people AT ALL, it's a disaster.

> 3. Ubuntu = Linux. There are many different Linux distributions.
Yeah, because Ubuntu doesn't have a reputation as the most user friendly one, indicating that the problems would actually be more severe in other distro's.  Oh wait, actually it's the exact opposite of that.  What point were you trying to make again?

> 4. All Linux distros are community developed and not commercial.
A valid complaint since 99% of them aren't, and since most of the people that use linux *now* do so mostly because it's free and noncommercial.  But hey, you're right, there is Suse and Red Hat.  So let's add "needing to pay for an OS that is crippled by comparison of what is shipped as standard" to the list of things that Linux has screwed up right now.  Thanks for the comment, good catch.

> Check up on your facts before you make your judgments.
Make sure you have a valid point, or that any of my facts are wrong, or that I've presented facts in the first place that may be disputed and not opinions before you make cookie cutter forum responses.  I mean, my god, you're saying linux users aren't on average more knowledgeable than windows users, almost as a rule?  Wow.


> Edit: Not feeding the troll.
I'm a troll now for pointing out errors with linux?  For making an on-topic post?  And you're not the troll by making an inflammatory response that basically boils down to 'windows has cooties and linux is perfect, yay!'?  Amazing.  Before you call me a troll, I use Ubuntu has my ONLY OS, which is more than a lot of people on this website who only use it as a dual booting curiosity, which means I obviously liked it quite a bit more than them if I'm at least willing to use it on a more regular basis.  Pointing out obvious, well documented flaws in linux and the attitude of it's community, an attitude you've made a perfect example of with your post, isn't trolling.  Making dumb posts with nothing to back any of your claims up and then pulling out of it once you realize just how dumb it really was is more akin to trolling than anything I've done.

### Post by Kerberos on 2006-04-08
I agree with SHodges on most of his points to be honest.

I think whats keeping it back is an overly defensive community.  You'll be hard pressed to find anywhere on the web that is prepared to look at Linux in a critical light - people who do are all called 'trolls' apparently.

What I suggest is getting a LiveCD of Hoary (especially you Dapper users) and trying it out again.  No doubt the OS that you once defended to the death will be clunky and lack several features that are now indespensible to you yet at the time it was around nobody could say a bad word about it.

Sure it may be technologically better than Windows in most areas, but it is behind in others and lacks both the familiarity that most users have with Windows and also the software base and driver support.  To overcome these, quite frankly, insurmountable problems (nothing can be done about them) it needs to provide an experiance massively superior to Windows, not just 'as good as', to stand a chance of surplanting it.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-04-08
[QUOTE=Burgresso]In my opinion, hands down is "other" - usabillity. Even distros like Ark and Ubuntu are harder to learn then windows; anyone who says differently is either lying or endowed with amazing computer skills.[/QUOTE] Can you substantiate this remark with examples? I haven't found this to be the case at all. Let's look at a real example. 

The other day, one of my co-workers asked me how she could see her desktop without minimizing each window one at a time. Ordinarily, I'd tell her to click the "show desktop" button on the QuickLaunch toolbar, but Windows XP turns the QuickLaunch toolbar off by default. So I had to tell her to right-click the taskbar, go to toolbars, select "QuickLaunch" and then click on the "show desktop" button.

Could she have found that on her own? I don't know.

In Ubuntu, the "show desktop" button is there by default.

Is Ubuntu *always* easier to use? Of course not. One wouldn't think, for example, if you copied text from one application, closed the application, and then tried to paste the text into another application that the text would somehow disappear from your clipboard (or need another application open in order for it to stay on the clipboard).

I'd say overall, they're about the same in terms of ease of use. Some things are easier in Windows. Others are easier in Ubuntu. I'm giving real examples, not just making outlandish statements.

---

### Post by Jack Jebedee on 2006-04-08
I agree that 3rd-party support is the biggest single reason that Linux isn't catching on as well as it deserves to.  I dearly LOVE the way Ubuntu works, but there are two huge things holding me back from making it my **only** operating system.  I have a R/W CD that came with Nero for Windows and a menu of various programs that make life with it easier.  That's not available for Linux.  Ditto my ATI graphics card.  Ditto my sound card.  To Linux' credit, at least all of the devices worked in their most basic form except for the sound card, but I eventually found out how to open a terminal program and type in a few arcane commands that caused Ubuntu to use the sound card.  I can't change the vertical refresh rate of my ATI 9200 yet, but maybe that will come someday.

The second-biggest reason is an opaque method of program installation and uninstallation.  In this, Linux may be its own worst enemy.  I get the feeling that each version of GnuLinux -- Suze, Knoppix, Red Hat, Ubuntu, Linspire, et al -- must require a unique installation routine that isn't compatible with other versions.  If they were all the same, I could click on "Setup" and each program would use its own installation program to correctly install programs, device drivers and their updates.  That's pretty much how it works in Windows and it's a terrific plus.  I can download and install programs with two clicks and no fuss.

I have a program included with Ubuntu that's supposed to play movies.  I can't remember the name of it now.  I can download movies in several formats and the program automatically comes up to play it when I double-click on the downloaded file.  Then I get an error message:  "plugin required" and something about streaming.

I think an older version of Open Office was included with Breezy Badger.  I like it.  I'd like to replace it with the current version, but I don't know how.  (I'm running the current version in Windows, though.)

I also have older versions of Firefox and Thunderbird that were included with Breezy Badger.  I'd like to update them to current versions, but I don't know how.  I downloaded a Linux file from Mozilla and double-clicked on it, but nothing useful happened.

When I first started with Breezy Badger, it didn't know what to do with an ISO file, but now it does.  I think I must have downloaded something from the Ubuntu library that now knows what to do with ISOs.

Back in the early days of automobiles, there was no such thing as a motorist who was not also a mechanic (except for those who could afford to have a mechanic riding in his convoy).  People bought automobiles not for the fascination of making them run right, but rather to transport them from one place to another speedily, conveniently and safely.  Nowadays, cars are far more reliable and people don't need to be mechanics to drive them.  In the same way, people buy computers to do things.  They don't want to be mechanics in order to get the machines to run right.  They want to be able to use the machines speedily, conveniently and safely.  The Apple Macintosh and, to a lesser extent, Windows machines allow users to do that.  There are times when things go wrong and that's when a mechanic is needed.  But that is the exception, not the rule.  Linux isn't there yet.  Running Linux requires the user to be a mechanic when things go right.  It's a normal part of running Linux on a home desktop.  Some people enjoy the fascination of making a beautifully complex operating system set run cleanly, but I don't think that the majority of computer users share that joy.  I remember the thrill I felt when I wrote my first printer driver for a Commodore 64 because that's the only way I could get the printer to work.  But I'm no longer interested in tinkering with machines so that they'll do what I want them to.  Let far brighter minds than mine do it right in ways that I can't even aspire to.

I think Linspire and their CNR has the right idea.  Until we automate what, for 99+% of users, is cryptic drudgery, Desktop/Home-User Linux will continue to be hidden in the shadow of the two operating systems that do so.

... JJ

---

### Post by MS_Past_Away on 2006-04-08
Point well taken Omnios. Windows is their choice by manufacturer default. After servicing thousands of machines, right off the street for the average user, I can give testimony that at least 40% of the machine users did not care about; games, PC maintenance and their major concern was having a machine that would boot when they hit that on button. Maybe that a had a few card games and played music too. They cared most about not loosing their work and not having to see me. I have serviced machines that had not even been defragmented or backed up in almost four years. Forensic data recovery, here I come. 

There is a very large audience out there for Linux. As a community we need to try to find innovative ways to reach these users that need certainty in their machines. Security is also a large concern. Advanced users that understand the benefits of Linux operating systems know what they are driving on to the big bad www. I see a lot of machines to this day and I will show and tell Ubuntu. I would like to believe that people that trust a person with their most delicate information will listen to reason. 

The learning curve only hurts once. The benefits are considerable. Hardware firewall, a repository of safe free software and the courtesy of an antivirus for windows users sake. Spyware is almost something of the future and fewer maintenance costs. 

When I mention to someone; when was the last time you tuned-up your windows machine? Well they look at me like I have three heads. Anyone that has had to pay a service techs time knows how quick the bill adds up for software and labor. I can only speak for myself, Ubuntu is the bomb... 

I find the need to run seven machines and my wife runs two more. Her bags, well I think are at the door already. Linux has come a long ways. I believe that the average user can do this now. The benefits for them are too much to resist, free? For real? No hacks? For real...

Thanks for reading this
MS_Past_Away

---

### Post by fatbuttlarry on 2006-04-08
#1 for me is is other.  **Time. ** Eventually, GIMP will be as user friendly as photoshop.  Eventually, burning a CD will be as easy as NERO. (it isn't that hard right now actually).  Eventually, you'll be able to manage your hardware with one utility.  I do agree that CNR is in the right direction.  The Open + Pay OS'es are our savior here. As long as they keep utilizing thier time and money to get great code, such as XGL, CNR, Wine (codeweavers), it will slowly filter down to the free world, but this all takes time.

I think there are some landmarks that have been and will be implimented in the history of linux.

1. Kernel (thanks Linus)
2. Gui (thanks gnome, kde, etc)
3. Games (thanks id, epic, and the free ones of course)
4. Compatibility (thanks wine, and the thousands of cross platform and cross os coders)
5. User friendly (thanks suse, kde, linspire, klik, cnr)


Since game manufacturers are starting to recognize linux, its mainly up to the Desktop development teams, and the OS distributors (in conjuntion with the community's feedback) to slowly mainstream a compatible, userfriendly desktop for the masses.  Since '99 when I bought mandrake for 40 bucks at walmart, I've seen some amazing strides in the Linux world.  AOL, wireless, jump drives, blue tooth, cd burning... somehow it all works.  I couldn't believe my eyes when the Live Linspire CD got my laptop on my home network.  Then weeks later, I played Planet Penguin Racer on a brand new gateway with knoppix...  For those arguing that Linux is not for the average user, I start to question what an average user is.  I was an average user at one point.  Now, I surround myself with OSes and computers.  Linux is much more difficult to use than Windows 95 for one strong reason.  You can't just install something.  Infact, Microsoft had the installation thing down with windows 3.1 If i remember correctly.  Keep end users away from source code and command line, and they'll try anything.  

Another 7 years and I think we're going to be be looking back at the struggles, instead of looking forward at what should be changed.  Enough time, and all these setbacks will be history. : )

-FBL

### Post by Burgresso on 2006-04-08
Aysiu:

> 
Quote:**
[QUOTE]Originally Posted by Burgresso
In my opinion, hands down is "other" - usability. Even distros like Ark and Ubuntu are harder to learn then windows; anyone who says differently is either lying or endowed with amazing computer skills.
Can you substantiate this remark with examples? I haven't found this to be the case at all. Let's look at a real example. [/QUOTE]

Sure - an easy one is installing Java. In windows, its virtually always an operation that only requires clicks. In Linux, this *can* be the case, but usually always I've had to do command line tweaks to get it installed right or to get the sound on. Another is simply using a floppy drive - the fact that it needs to be mounted in some Linux distros is an obstacle, in Windows, it is always under My Computer and does not require command-line fun. 

Also, installation in general - I've only successfully installed about two programs successfully that were not in the repositories. In windows, you are virtually always offered a click-through interface.

Yet another is Media - Windows has its own media player that plays almost anything. I'm not sure I could figure out (with my skill level) how to play so many types of media types without the incredible automatix script. I've tried to configure media myself before, it the time and skill level was beyond me.

Many of things would be easier if I where better skilled in Linux - but the point is, from a usability point of view is that I shouldn't have to be. From a usability point of view, "everyone" - even your uncle/mother/massuese with poor computer skills would have a fair shot at figuring it out.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-04-09
Burgresso, while I agree that installation of non-repository software is easier in Windows, I know plenty of Windows users who cannot install even such a simple thing as Java.

I do think there are particular things that are more difficult in Linux, particularly if you have quirky software needs. I can't tell you how many people come on these boards saying, "How do I install *X* program?" and the first thing I'm thinking is, *Where did you even find that program? Why do you need it?*

But some people have a need to seek out obscure programs, and for those people I'd definitely say you need to learn how to compile from source or Linux is going to be very difficult for you.

For a large portion of "regular" or "average" users, though, the repositories and Automatix can probably take care of just about everything they need.

I'm not going to say *everything* is easier in Linux, but I won't concede (as per my previous example--I can give others as well) that *everything* is easier in Windows either.

I'm a firm believer that choosing an OS (Mac OS X, Windows XP, Ubuntu, other), one should assess her own needs, skills, and means, and see what's the best choice. It is *not* always Windows = easier, Linux = harder. And it's not always Linux = easier, Windows = harder.

### Post by Lovechild on 2006-04-09
[QUOTE=beforewisdom]1. 3rd party driver and software support.  If it doesn't run what the user can already get with what they got they just don't care.

2. Pefect MS office interoperability.  Its at an incredible level now, but people who are users and not enthusiasts don't care about that.  They just don't want hassles.

3. The user community.   Better with some distros, but still a problem.  Nobody likes getting a lecture ( or slammed ) by an adolescent or an arrested adolescent because s/he asked a question or said something that offends the honor of the operating system (please).[/QUOTE]

1) if it's free software there is no problem, the software is easily redistributable, if it's not free software it's not our problem - please complain to whoever is restricting your freedom not your distro vendor or community. The same goes with patented software - we would love to add functionality like codec support but it's not legal - please solve that problem for us without blaming us for following the law.

2. This is largely a fileformat level issue more so than an application level issue - if we had a common open format there would be no need to draw freedom restricting software onto your platform - please support efforts like OpenDoc to avoid this issue.

3. One of the great things about Linux is the community I agree - however if you are in need of urgent help and you don't like the replies you get from people who give up their sparetime to help you - please consider using one of the many paid for services to get tech support. I've tried to help out around the community for many years now and nothing ticks me off more than ungrateful users to whom I need to repeated information available using a simple google search. We are not here for your service, but for your aid and friendship - we share a passion, we are not paid tech supporters.

### Post by walterius on 2006-04-09
I stayed away from Linux for years because Windows software was much better--and much of it still is. For example, for all its polish and bells and whistles, Open Office cannot yet compete with Microsoft Office. Consider the GIMP and Photoshop; there is no comparison.

On the other hand, the ever-increasing complexity, cost, and bloat of Windows OS and software (and the concomitant cost of its hardware) helped drive me to Linux. 

And of course open source stuff just keeps getting better. Today I could live with OO.o. A year or so ago that would have been impossible. And Linux, I am told, runs well on a 486. (Ah, those were the days. I have fond memories of my 133 MHz 486, zoom, zoom.)

Considering the perceived complexity of learning and installing Linux--the proletariat community stays away from it in droves. One thing Msft does very well is make it so idiots--and there are a *lot* of them out there--can use Windows--*without* blowing it up. Even today, Linux is IMHO best used by the geeky.

Walterius Newbius
:twisted:

---

### Post by Spano on 2006-04-09
> Why does it need to be hard does it bultd up a social image for you?u
No, but I think it helps my own self image.  I'm not using Ubuntu "out of the box" I have become facinated with piecing together light weight minimal desktops based on a server install.  This is "hard" for me, but I enjoy the challenge.
Don't talk about it much socially because I've noticed the subject seems to bore my listeners. 
> Not being rude or anything but a user that read this will get the wrong impression of what linux is like.

### Post by Burgresso on 2006-04-09
> Make a system foolproof, and only a fool will want to use it. If Linux were "uber easy", then most of the knowledgable users might migrate to BSD to avoid having to deal with the foolishness that drove them from Windows.

> If linux came pre-installed and was uber easy I wouldn't like it as much.

Okay, that's cool, but I still think that easier linux (or specific linux distros become), the faster it will become widespread. 

"Hardcore" :)  (ie, very skilled) linux users will always be around, and there will always be users who like to edit their systems and customize it - and afterall, why not? It's fun. 

But if we assume that other users will want to leave windows and suddenly want to read up on how do these things (such as customise a system, etc) then I think we should take another look at our premises. Sure, some always do or will want to become linux experts, but many, many more don't. 

In a conversation about makeing linux widespread, we should remember that casual computer users (ie, %90 of them) usually want to use computers that are easy. This makes linux intimidating to them.

### Post by dawhoo on 2006-04-09
maybe this has been mentioned in the 39 previous pages, so if it's a repeat - forgive me.


The main reason I think Linux isn't mainstream is the RTFM attitude of most of the linux support forums I've ever used.  This attitude predominated every linux type forum I ever used for years.  It wasn't until 2 weeks ago that I installed Ubuntu.  It had been two years since I even attempted to install a linux distro.  

Now this place is WAY different and the 'RTFM attitude' is replaced by, 'let's see what we can do'.  That attitude of actually helping people is going to do more for Linux than any driver ever could.  You can have all the drivers in the world available, but if there isn't someone who will hold your hand on your first attempt to install one, you're going to lose users.

K/Ubuntu is the easiest install of any linux distro I've used, it has plenty of applications and it's fairly intuitive.  Finally, I think there may be a real challenge to the Microsoft desktop OS monopoly.
