### Post by sitedesign on 2006-04-10
[QUOTE=Bekker]I voted publicity, because a lot of people whom I tell I'm using linux don't know what I'm talking about. Most people in the IT-branch know what it is because they read magazines, but the average home user never heard about linux.
Low 3rd party support is just because linux isn't widespread so manufacturers don't care for it, there's no money for them.[/QUOTE]
I use linux for all my network installs (running on the server) and starting to even get it on some desktops too.

As for no money in it, I tend to charge the client about £2000 per server install including transfer of data from the old windows server and configuration of clients, The clients are always happy and gob smacked when I demonstrate the desaster recovery using a DVD backup with mkcdrec. Complete baremetal recovery in only 20 mintues including all users passwords, emails the lot. Best of al the support for the network is far cheaper if they have linux servers from us because it never goes wrong.

We still have some servers out there which I checked recently for another thread about uptimes which have an uptime of 4 years. Lets see windows doing that.

Any way back to the discussion. Linux needs the large software giants to provide linux versions of their packages (commercialy) like macromedia and adobe etc. Rumour has it that will be coming soon though. Heres hoping, then I can switch all the clients to linux as well.

### Post by davebav50 on 2006-04-11
Linux is not widespread because of several reasons - 
1. It is not as intuiative as Windows (but this also leads to security issues in Windows)
2. Its not taught widely at school the way Windows is
3. Most of the easily bought shrink wrap software is written for Windows
4. You can buy a PC with Windows pre-installed for around 250 pounds entry level
5. It is easier to hire Windows support personel
6. It is surrounded by jargon that puts people off.
7.95% of the established user base is Windows meaning there is a big intellectual investment in the status quo.
8.Its virtually no advertising or publicity budget to overcome these points and spread the word

---

### Post by BoyOfDestiny on 2006-04-11
[QUOTE=davebav50]Linux is not widespread because of several reasons - 
1. It is not as intuiative as Windows (but this also leads to security issues in Windows)
2. Its not taught widely at school the way Windows is
3. Most of the easily bought shrink wrap software is written for Windows
4. You can buy a PC with Windows pre-installed for around 250 pounds entry level
5. It is easier to hire Windows support personel
6. It is surrounded by jargon that puts people off.
7.95% of the established user base is Windows meaning there is a big intellectual investment in the status quo.
8.Its virtually no advertising or publicity budget to overcome these points and spread the word[/QUOTE]

1. I disagree. Many things are easier, and more intuitive. If you mean randomly going to webpages and downloading .exe's etc. Ok that's much easier in windows.

2. Depends what schools and what countries.

3. Easily bought. Sure. Go buy Office XP and adobe photoshop retail. It's easy if you have plenty of cash.

4. You can take that PC and install Linux on for practically nothing or nothing.

5. Don't know anything about this. I will however contend this is meaningless to the average user. They don't hire tech support personnel.

6. Right, windows Live, aero, longhorn, isn't jargon. I can't tell what any of those do by the name either. What jargon has anyone used about Linux around you? LAMP (that's programmers stuff). All I hear is free. open source, kernel, OS. At least when I talk about linux that's all that needs to be said IMHO.

7. I think that number is incorrect. 

8. You have word of mouth. Website galore. I haven't seen any TV commercials, but you never know. I'm sure Novell, Redhat, or IBM could pull it off. 

In my opinion the biggest setback for Linux is also one of the greatest motivators (I know, paradoxical). Microsoft was and frankly still is a huge reason why I'm glad to be using Ubuntu Linux (and only that.) 
Thanks Ubuntu devs and community.

### Post by Lovechild on 2006-04-11
[QUOTE=beforewisdom]You're right, but users who are just users and not enthusiasts don't care.  
[/QUOTE]

I hate it when users blame me for things I can't change - I elect to inform them as to WHY we sadly can't include things like DVD support and explain to people WHY and HOW the situation should be altered.

I don't give two bits about them not caring, you should never complain about an issue where you do not understand the foundation of the problem. In this case legality.

I simply refuse to turn into a criminal to appease people - I would much rather educate people, those who understand become informed, those who fail to see the problem and why they should engage themselves in the solution.. well those sadly we loose currently.

We don't really have much in terms of options here you know.

a) user education
b) wait for the patents to expire
c) become criminals
d) license the patents

A doesn't solve the issue now so it's not a good solution except longterm if we get enough backing. 
B is downright idiotic since that still doesn't solve issues like copy protection schemes like CSS and it doesn't solve
C is tempting, civil disobidience might be a good way to display the issue at hand (sadly this means we'll have to have a martyr and the idea of either being that martyr or making one of my fellow community members that is not appealing to me)
D is not an option in most cases as patent holders are not forced to even grant us licenses for a fee, it impairs the freedom on which the system is built and it violates the GPL - it will however solve the issue NOW and remain legal.

I'm not a big fan of D as I'm not a big fan of stupid laws, C and A seem like viable options, B being the last resort, at least that means we get fully legal MP3 support in 2010.
For corporate desktops that need support now, D is the only way forward shortterm - companies like Fluendo are offering most of the codec support you'd need (or are in the planning stages), all fully legal. Again this is not a good way to procede as we are not ensured the right to support a given format, but at least it provides legal support now.

---

### Post by SHodges on 2006-04-13
[QUOTE=aysiu]You say this based on the hundreds of Linux installations you've done...?[/QUOTE]
Sort of like your comment a few pages back about Windows being "hard to install".  Yeah, point and click installation that basically requires you to type a password and name on most computers and it's done is certainly the most difficult thing *I've* done.  And yeah, being a tech worker at a school with over 1,000+ computers, having installed Windows AND Linux on at least a hundred of them (each), I say that.  You'd think that having such a low opinion of the average computer user that you would believe *that* is hard, would cause linux to go a more 'user friendly' route, without all that pat-yourself-on-the-back "the command line is already user friendly guys, I swear" stuff.  Guess not.  



Really, do you guys hear yourselves?  Almost every time someone brings up a problem they've had with linux, you guys say something stupid like "oh well it worked on MY hardware, and obviously I am the example that the rest of the world follows, so you must be wrong" or just dodge the issue entirely to take a stab at windows, or just call them a "troll" (because someone running ONLY Ubuntu and whom brings up problems they would like to fix to make the OS better are obviously trolls out to hate linux).  And it's ironic that it's taking place in this thread of all places.  *Here's* your setback.  It isn't Microsoft, if you expect your competitor to just lay down then you don't belong in competition.  It isn't driver support either, vendors would write them for you if you had more users, and you'd have those if the OS wasn't so anti-easy.  The setback is people saying the command line is user friendly with a straight face, or insinuating that Ubuntu installation is actually EASIER than XP installation.  It's the self centered community that refuses to see any problems or setbacks in the first place.

Guess not.  



Really, do you guys hear yourselves?  Almost every time someone brings up a problem they've had with linux, you guys say something stupid like "oh well it worked on MY hardware, and obviously I am the example that the rest of the world follows, so you must be wrong" or just dodge the issue entirely to take a stab at windows, or just call them a "troll" (because someone running ONLY Ubuntu and whom brings up problems they would like to fix to make the OS better are obviously trolls out to hate linux).  And it's ironic that it's taking place in this thread of all places.  *Here's* your setback.  It isn't Microsoft, if you expect your competitor to just lay down then you don't belong in competition.  It isn't driver support either, vendors would write them for you if you had more users, and you'd have those if the OS wasn't so anti-easy.  The setback is people saying the command line is user friendly with a straight face, or insinuating that Ubuntu installation is actually EASIER than XP installation.  It's the self centered community that refuses to see any problems or setbacks in the first place.[/QUOTE]

The original poster is generalizing, and not even providing a proper anecdote. If these folks wouldn't making sweeping generalizations, they would get better replies. This person didn't even mention the problems. How is it hard for "bigginers". Is it an issue with xorg? Is it using synaptic? Also, bloat is vague, Ubuntu fits on one cd, or is linux bloated in general? Damn small Linux is 50mb. The only thing "true", is the issue with hardware support. Although I know people with bleeding edge hardware having things working, I'm willing to be some occasional odd piece won't work of the box for some. Fine. Worked out of the box for me, doesn't mean for everyone (my hardware is more than a couple of years old)

Also, could you provide where you got the numbers for users. From sites that check browsers and OS, there is a similar number of mac and linux users.

And uh, what makes Ubuntu's install harder than windows. Is it the lack of product activation, or using driver disks for onboard controllers...? Most people do not install windows. Once you get it up and running, you have to download loads of apps to make it useable (or buy apps). Please explain how a windows install is easier. Don't tell me it always works either.

And, guess what, commandline doesn't have to be hard. I wouldn't force anyone to use it or learn it. If they want to run an update from Add/Remove or synaptic, go for it. I use it all the time too. Sometimes I just want to type sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade. That is not hard, or is it? These problems are slowly dissapearing, and the ubuntu espresso installer, well it is so easy it's scary. How hard is it to pop a cd, and use the OS a bit, then decide to just install it, fast and easy.

Keep the criticism coming! Linux just gets better and better. Look around, a couple of years ago, manually mounting was a pain. Now we have plug and play in gnome (project utopia). Before it was just installing. I didn't even try debian until it had the new installer. What else was there, no 3d, now nvidia and ati are providing drivers for both 32 and 64 (not that they are great or anything), Linux has no place to go but up.

### Post by SHodges on 2006-04-14
[QUOTE=BoyOfDestiny]The original poster is generalizing, and not even providing a proper anecdote. If these folks wouldn't making sweeping generalizations, they would get better replies. This person didn't even mention the problems. How is it hard for "bigginers". Is it an issue with xorg? Is it using synaptic? Also, bloat is vague, Ubuntu fits on one cd, or is linux bloated in general? Damn small Linux is 50mb. The only thing "true", is the issue with hardware support. Although I know people with bleeding edge hardware having things working, I'm willing to be some occasional odd piece won't work of the box for some. Fine. Worked out of the box for me, doesn't mean for everyone (my hardware is more than a couple of years old)[/QUOTE]
It isn't bloated, I agree, but it certainly isn't easy for beginners either.  Ubuntu, the distro that advertises itself as the most user friendly of them all, can't even play .mp3's out of the box, recognize a large number of ati cards, HUGE problems with wireless, etc.  And yes, I know those last two have more to do with ATI and the makers of wireless cards, but that doesn't matter.  The why isn't even going to cross the mind of the user, all that matters is that it DOESN'T work, and that they have to jump through hoops to get everything the way it should be, if they can fix it at all.  I don't like Windows, I haven't used it as an OS on my computers for nearly a year now, and I doubt I'll ever be going back unless a slew of PC-exclusive games start seeing release.  But I can at least admit that Windows installation of a lot of stuff is a LOT easier.  Double clicking the catalyst installer .exe or just popping a cd into a drive and letting everything take care of itself is a lot easier than the stuff you have to do on the most user friendly flavor of linux.

> Also, could you provide where you got the numbers for users. From sites that check browsers and OS, there is a similar number of mac and linux users.
Are you still talking to me?  I don't think I said anything about macs period, or gave an overall market share for Linux and macs.  The only number I did give was the number of computers in my school that I take care of, which was a very rough estimate :confused:

> And uh, what makes Ubuntu's install harder than windows. Is it the lack of product activation, or using driver disks for onboard controllers...? Most people do not install windows. Once you get it up and running, you have to download loads of apps to make it usable (or buy apps). Please explain how a windows install is easier. Don't tell me it always works either.
I've never once had to use driver disks for onboard controllers, nor have I seen anyone need to do that.  Taking the absolute extreme of windows installation and acting as though it's common would be akin to me saying "I once heard of a guy who's monitor didn't work with linux, ergo no linux can display anything".  Ubuntu's installation of the OS isn't harder than Windows, but it certainly isn't easier either, they're both so easy that pretty much ANYONE could do it.  Ubuntu's installation of SOFTWARE on the other hand can be very difficult for the average user.  Requiring the user to type things into a command line is just ridiculous and feels like it's out of the wrong time period.  We have GUI's now, the average user doesn't need a command line now.

> And, guess what, command line doesn't have to be hard. I wouldn't force anyone to use it or learn it. If they want to run an update from Add/Remove or synaptic, go for it. I use it all the time too. Sometimes I just want to type sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade. That is not hard, or is it? These problems are slowly overbearingness, and the ubuntu espresso installer, well it is so easy it's scary. How hard is it to pop a cd, and use the OS a bit, then decide to just install it, fast and easy.
And that's exactly what I'm talking about.  "That is not hard, or is it?"  Are you kidding me?  You honestly expect the same users that you guys pretend would be incapable of installing windows to just know to type "sudo apt-get update" and all that garbage?  That's a massive double standard for user intelligence.  

> Keep the criticism coming! Linux just gets better and better. Look around, a couple of years ago, manually mounting was a pain. Now we have plug and play in gnome (project utopia). Before it was just installing. I didn't even try debian until it had the new installer. What else was there, no 3d, now nvidia and ati are providing drivers for both 32 and 64 (not that they are great or anything), Linux has no place to go but up.
I agree.  Criticism should be a welcome thing, not attacked.  Criticism of windows helped Linux get off the ground in the first place.


[QUOTE=Stormy Eyes]I know the difference, but I refuse to care. As far as I'm concerned, new users don't matter all that much. The ones who overcome the learning curve stop being "newbies"; the rest go back to Windows or buy Macs.

Sure, Ubuntu's slogan is "Linux for human beings". Guess what: having to learn, to grow, to make an effort is part of being human. There are animals that have everything provided for them without the need for effort on their part. Humans use them as pets and livestock.[/QUOTE]

"I don't care about new users"

Gee, I can't imagine *what* is keeping linux from getting the marketshare and the driver and software support that comes with it...

### Post by aysiu on 2006-04-14
[QUOTE=hotani]Notice how most of the HOWTOs on this site and elsewhere start with:
"1- Open a terminal window....."?

That's the setback.[/QUOTE] Why is that a setback? If you've given support on these forums, you know the easiest way to give help is saying "copy and paste this command." It's far more difficult to try to describe point-and-click behavior, and it's far more time-consuming to take screenshots and upload them (not to mention the amount of forum bandwidth this consumes).

I can give someone one command to install fifty programs. To describe how to do that in Synaptic via point-and-click would take several paragraphs. Actually doing the point-and-click to install fifty programs would take at least fifteen minutes.

Why is it so difficult for people to copy and paste commands? Highlight the command. Copy it. Open the terminal. Paste it.

---

### Post by hotani on 2006-04-14
*I* don't mind it. I worked on linux and unix for 6 years before ever even clicking on a GUI. But for the system to be *accepted* mainstream, the CLI has got to go. 

Mac OS X did it right, they focused on making everything accessible via GUI, but left the CLI in for the geeks... for us. I want it to be there, but I'd never suggest a Linux system to my mom that required CLI.

Ubuntu is so close to being there, and I think in the next year or two it might be to the point where I could safely recommend it to my mom or any other non-technical person.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-04-14
[QUOTE=hotani]
Mac OS X did it right, they focused on making everything accessible via GUI, but left the CLI in for the geeks... for us. I want it to be there, but I'd never suggest a Linux system to my mom that required CLI.[/QUOTE] No, I agree. CLI should not be required. In every possible case, there should be a way to do it via CLI *and* GUI.

However, I'm just saying that even when there's a CLI for things, it's still easier to make HowTos using commands--I'll always give people commands using apt-get or aptitude, even though almost all of those same things can be done in Synaptic Package Manager.

---

### Post by Jack Jebedee on 2006-04-14
[QUOTE=hotani]*I* don't mind it. I worked on linux and unix for 6 years before ever even clicking on a GUI. But for the system to be *accepted* mainstream, the CLI has got to go. 

Mac OS X did it right, they focused on making everything accessible via GUI, but left the CLI in for the geeks... for us. I want it to be there, but I'd never suggest a Linux system to my mom that required CLI.

Ubuntu is so close to being there, and I think in the next year or two it might be to the point where I could safely recommend it to my mom or any other non-technical person.[/QUOTE]

You hit it dead-center, Hotani.  There are some things about Ubuntu that just blew me away from the first install.  It recognized my video card (ATI 9200), my network card (Linksys) USB card readers and every single thing I had except an Awe 32 Sounblaster card.  Beautiful, but silent!  Windows didn't do that on its install.  Instead, it dropped to 16-color, low-res and insisted that I install 3rd-party drivers so that it could recognize components.

Then Ubuntu checks periodically for updates and installs those automatically after asking permission.  Get out!  I love this!

Ubuntu is still way ahead of me.  First, I found the cryptic commands that let the Soundblaster make noise.  Then I found the repositories and Synaptic.  Then (insert choir of angels singing) I found Automatix.  Incredible!  I'm a happy camper, just two steps away from waving goodbye to Windows.

No, there's no way in the world that my mother would be able to do what I've done... after pulling my hair out in a few places on the road to learning more about Ubuntu Gnu/Linux.  I don't think there's any way that she would be happy with any OS that can't run WordPerfect or PrintShop.  But the thing I love about Gnu/Linux in general is that it gives me a way to do the groundwork, then open a login for her that will make sure that she can't screw anything up.  I know there's a way to do that in Windows, but I haven't figured it out yet.  (Example:  I can login to Windows default root account -- administrator -- or to my own account.  Both ways are passworded, but both will let me login if I hit <Enter> without a password.  I haven't figured out how to stop that in Windows.  Ubuntu doesn't have that problem.)  There are instructions in the Wiki about how to mount Windows drives, but I haven't found any way in Windows to mount Gnu/Linux drives.  I can mount drives that are visible only to the first user in Ubuntu and there are instructions covering how to do it.  I can't do the same in Windows, though there may be a way to do that.  I just haven't been able to find out how.

Bottom line:  Ubuntu (my fave Gnu/Linux flavor) is incredibly versatile OS and I haven't even scratched below the surface.  Windows may or may not be as versatile.  I don't know -- and most don't -- because Windows essentially allows a user to be as OS-illiterate as one can be while still being a capable user.  Mac probably has the best of both worlds.  The GUI allows one to be OS blind while the CLI accomodates the OS savvy.

The thing I miss most about Windows is Outlook Express.  I haven't seen another e-mail program that comes close to it in form and function.  (Where can I find stationery for Thunderbird or Evolution?)  Overall, though, Ubuntu suits me just fine as it is and I'm ***really*** looking forward to seeing Dapper Drake in June.

... JJ

---

### Post by Jack Jebedee on 2006-04-15

Bullseye!  The computer community, for the most part, fires up their machine to accomplish a task.  Browse the web.  Edit digital photographs.  Write a term paper.  Create a customized sound or data CD.  They generally don't like to begin their trip by tinkering under the hood.  They want to turn the key and go.

### Post by Xilon on 2006-04-18
The ease of installation of *most* things is something that Ubuntu (/debian) has an advantage in over Windows. It's just so nice to just check which apps you want, click apply and wait for the downloads to finish :) with the new (i think it's new) "Add/Remove..." in dapper it's even easier!

The reason why Linux is prevented from being widespread is a paradox; people don't use Linux because there aren't as good linux alternatives for Windows apps (Photoshop, uTorrent, Shareaza and a few others I use, some may even argue that OpenOffice.org isn't up to scratch), but companies don't make native linux versions because there aren't enough people using it and hence it's not worth it.

That's the main reason why I held back from Linux all this time anyway. I tried Mandrake 9.0 when it came out which was my first Linux distro (not a good start tbh) which was a bout 2-3 years ago and it worked pretty nice, it was faster than Windows even though it was bloated and running KDE but the apps just weren't there. Now on Ubuntu I have kind of found alternatives for most things (foobar2000 => Listen, Konnekt => Kopete, uTorrent => Ktorrent, MS Office => OpenOffice.org etc.) but they aren't as good as the Windows apps. Having said that, if Photoshop were to be ported or if I found that The GIMP is easy to use (which it doesn't seem to be yet) and as powerful as Photoshop (I have heard that it is) then I would pretty much be ok with how my box is setup right now.

Of course the solution to this would be for more people like me to convert to Linux, the not-so-good alternatives would just be temporary until either the alternatives become better or until Linux gets very popular and companies start making more native apps. This might be easily done with publicity. I don't really see that in the near future because it seems like MacOS is a lot more popular than Linux (probably because of the ease-of-use) and there aren't many native ports for that either.

Another thing is games. A lot of PC users don't use their PCs to develope programs or webpages, download stuff, even write documents etc. they just want games and to surf the net or IM. Internet browsing is great in Linux, IM is ok (for MSN it's probably equal but not for my needs) but it pretty much sucks in terms of games. A few good ones run natively from what I've heard, and a lot work under Codega which you need to pay for, but the majority just don't run. Again this comes back to that paradox.

Edit: PS. I have yet to find an app that is better than the Windows counterpart. All the applications that I *use*, of course apart from the native ports, are just alternatives to the Windows counterpart. I would like to see it the other way around.

Of course for server use etc. Linux is way better.

The "biggest setback", I think, is MS making it too easy with Windows. Too many users seem to like "easy". Ah well, each to their own I guess.

### Post by kvonb on 2006-05-02
I've tried a lot of Linux and Unix flavours over the years starting with Free-BSD 3.2 in 1998, and I am thoroughly impressed with Ubuntu (breezy).

This is the first Linux I've ever tried that EVERYTHING worked on a standard install!

I first installed it on a clients Ying-Tong (unknown generic chinese brand) laptop and was amazed.

The most amazing thing was that my digital camera worked, and bugger me even my canon USB scanner!!!!!!!

I am now totally hooked and with a subscription to Cedega also enjoy quite a good gaming experience.

I will be installing Ubuntu on ALL the systems in my home, and once "Empire Earth" is working in either Cedega or Wine (the one game that doesn't work, which I play with my wife regularly), I can delete Windows permanently.

We should remember that Windows doesn't work "straight out of the box" in a lot of cases, we still have to load (and sometimes search for and download) drivers for certain peripherals which can be a right pain in the arse.  And as someone mentioned, when a prog or hardware doesn't work in windows, we automatically blame the 3rd party or hardware vendor.  When a prog breaks in Linux we blame Linux itself!  I think this is because most of the software we need in linux is already there and is seen to be a part of the O/S, whereas in Windows, we install it separately, hence "shifting the blame".  Whether this is by design of Microsoft or not is beside the point of Linux being an "accepted" desktop O/S.

And quite frankly I'm not keen on Ubuntu becoming a "popular" desktop O/S to the extent of Windows, just imagine all the virus/spy-ware/malware/script kiddy nonsense we would have to start worrying about.

I'm happy not to have a virus checker slowing my system down and constantly wanting updates, makes my life so much easier.  I don't even bother with a firewall, my Ubuntu main server takes care of that and I believe (from experience) that 99.9999999% of virus/hacking problems are windows based attacks anyway.  The script kiddies don't know what to do with Linux yet!  That would take effort having to LEARN something!

The best things about Ubuntu?  It's free, it works VERY well, it does everything that I want it to do, and it's FREE!  Now I realise that I mentioned FREE twice, but it is such an important point that I thought it was worth mentioning twice!

Also as truNWA says, the condescending attitudes are not present in the Ubuntu help and forums, this is a refreshing change, real people don't have time to read through pages of programmer written nonsense, they just want a real answer fast, they will remember the solution the next time.

I no longer have to worry about whether a copy of Windows is legal or not, and don't tell me that you ALL have fully legal copies of Windows! (I have ONE legal copy of WindowsXP, which can only be used legally on ONE machine at a time).  Going straight at last....YAY!

Sure I had to adopt the attitude "OK, not what I'm used to, but it functions and I can learn to live with that".

Sure the "super-neon-laser-thruster-upperbackswing-tittybollock-childish-font-colours-and-silly-girlie-graphics" don't work in the Linux version of my favourite messenger (read as - AD AND SPY-WARE FREE!!!!)  as cool as they did under Windows, but I can live without them!

A lot of people have done a lot of very hard and important work on Ubuntu, and I for one am extremely grateful to them and wish them every success.  Quite frankly I feel an urge to donate!  I'd better settle down a bit :).

Also keep in mind that the "Charter" of Ubuntu is "Free software", they only include software with an Ubuntu release if it is released under that particular license, the neat thing is that you can then "adulterate" it with non-free to your hearts content, there is even a support network for most things.

Try calling Microsoft for support on something they didn't sell, actually try calling Microsoft for support on something they did create AND sell for a large sum of money, they will charge you $200 then tell you it's probably the text file you downloaded 3 months ago so it's not their problem, nothing wrong with Windows!

I believe the inclusion of a standardised "control panel" type application and/or a system monitor/undo app like "System restore" are the things that could propel Ubuntu to the top.  The key to Microsoft's continuing acceptance is strandardisation (from a purely business point of view), always has and always will.  The pictures change with each successive Windows release, things look prettier, and they move the buttons around a bit, but under it all it is still the same as Windows 95.  People don't have to be re-trained to use it, and can figure out how to do something on each new release.  People are afraid of change, and don't want to look like "newbies" as someone else here put it.

The other key to success is mentioned in a book that is over 2500 years old (The Ancient art of War I think), and that is this: divide and conquer!  With so many versions of Linux, and even different desktop managers, there is too much diversity.  I say this purely from the business and "popularly accepted desktop O/S" point of view, a company will not retrain all it's staff to use a completely new O/S, even though it is free to obtain, the changeover cost would be enormous.

I've had many arguments with my bank about why they don't support Firefox!

I'm not judging business practices or decisions, just trying to look at this from the point of view with the most votes - business, and unfortunately this is where the decision to buy or use an O/S lies.

Small business could be the key to a "rebirth" of Linux, provide good apps for accounting and such mundane things, win a good user base and go from there.  Small business doesn't have the cost of retraining, buying new apps etc' as long as they can convert their data-files from the commercial to the free as in Open Office.

As for me, I now run my small business totally on Ubuntu and I am happy.

vive le revolution!

</RANT>

---

### Post by Compucore on 2006-05-02
The thing that I find as well that is the biggest set back here for me. ANd this is going back to when the good old modems were using UART chips on them. And I am refering to the internal modems that you could set the com port, irq and the what nots on them. Then plug it right into an available slot within your computer and boom it was ready to rock and roll with them. And that was up to 28.8 or 33.6 modems. Now with the PCI 56k winmodems. you can't even use those in your linux machine. because they took the uart chip out from it and its runs off of the OS like MS to create everything and make it work. The older ones you would never had a problem like that becuase it was hardware dependant and not software dependant on the OS to do the work. Last modem I had checked was with USR Robotics that still made true hardware internal modems still at one point. And worked in both windows and in linux. No one else made them anymore. ANd had gotten all the excuses that it was not worth the while having it made to work with linux. Some companies are just plain blind about making something worth working on more than just one OS. But thats just my two cents. 

Compucore

### Post by Stormy Eyes on 2006-05-02
[QUOTE=jeller]People that say windows user's are to lazy to learn are wrong,they dont need to learn just like not everyone is a mechanic a cook or a rocket scientist:-#[/QUOTE]

I'm a computer programmer by trade, but I don't let that stop me from learning how to cook, how to sew, how to perform simple repairs around the house, or how to maintain my bicycle or my car. I agree with Robert Heinlein, who wrote:
[quote="Robert Heinlein"]A human being should be able to change a diaper, plan an invasion, butcher a hog, conn a ship, design a building, write a sonnet, balance accounts, build a wall, set a bone, comfort the dying, take orders, give orders, cooperate, act alone, solve equations, analyze a new problem, pitch manure, program a computer, cook a tasty meal, fight efficiently, die gallantly. Specialization is for insects.[/quote]
Linux is for human beings, not insects. Don't complain about having to learn new things; you'll get no sympathy or respect from me.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-02
[QUOTE=jeller]
When you buy a new car you dont need to know how everything work's and how to fix it when it goes wrong,that's how linux will succeed when you plug something in and it work's.[/QUOTE] In other words, Linux will succeed when Dell sells more Linux-preloaded desktops and notebooks (not just that one Mandriva-preloaded notebook that's available to only students in France). That's the **only** time you can guarantee that you plug it and it works--it comes preinstalled and preconfigured.

After all, even if someone gave me all the proper parts for a BMW, I would hardly be able to construct it myself. What do I look like--a Gentoo user?

That said, hardware and software are *very* different things. You can't really compare constructing a car to installing an operating system. Hardware construction and repair has the potential for electrocution, short circuiting... even explosions--certainly irreparable damage to the hardware. Most software configuring (installation, repartitioning, etc.) doesn't have any noticeable effect on the hardware.

Learning to *use* your computer is like learning to drive a car and not grind the gears, learning to check the oil from time to time, fill it with petrol, not lean your tire up against the curb... etc. You don't have to be a car mechanic to know how to take care of your car, and you don't have to be a programmer or a computer technician to know how to install and configure and use Linux-based operating systems.

### Post by htinn on 2006-05-03
It's amazing to me how people just expect their printers and wireless network to "just work" in Linux. Uh, hello? Printers and wireless? Those things barely work in Windows IF they work at all.

The reason those things have problems working in BOTH Windows AND Linux is because there is a huge amount of competition. The hardware makers cut corners and don't hire enough people to write their drivers.

Bottom line: if you want quality printers and/or network, you need quality hardware. Don't just go to Best Buy or Walmart and expect it to work (in ANY OS).

### Post by frio on 2006-05-03
To answer this question honestly...

Linux is not fool proof, idiot proof, etc... (simply put)

As an experienced Windows user/support person (engineering discipline), I have semi-struggled to get Ubuntu working properly and effeciently on a relatively simple home network.

This includes secure wireless networking (WPA_supplicant), file sharing (samba), video resolutions... I could go on and on. I haven't even scratched the surface since I have not even attempted to edit video, stream audio, and others that I can easily do with Windows.

That being said, I now see the enourmous potential of Linux (Ubuntu inparticular) in becoming a mainstream OS. Let me explain.

As I mentioned, I am an experienced PC user. From that I became a PC support person. From that I realized that MOST people do not care about setup, or editing .conf files. MOST people want a GUI that works as expected. That is what made MS so successful. It does not matter that Linux is more stable. It does not matter that Linux is more secure. They must be able to easily accomplish the task(s) at hand. Linux is not there yet, only because of the lack of a GUI for a lot of common tasks.

Now with that being said. I used a version of Linux a couple of years ago, I believe it was Mandrake; don't rememebr the version. I was not impressed and left Linux alone, until I read the article in PCWorld about Ubuntu.

I decided to give it another try; completely different experience. The idea that I can run my PC(s) the way I want to... PC, file server, print server without paying rediculous amounts of $ for something that, if I had the time, could  (maybe) do myself (I admit, lot's of time)

If Ubuntu continues to be developed and listens to its users (GUI's, hard to beat), I will continue to use and promote (as I have been lately). If the semi-experienced user and up starts to feel the same way, I think we would start to see widespread use of Linux by people other than sys admins.

Sorry for the ramble... Just an honest answer.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-03
[quote=only my situation matters]You can't solve a problem if you keep ignoring it.[/quote] There are three things wrong with this statement:

1. Not everyone has this problem. You imagine that because printers have always worked in Windows for you and never in Ubuntu for you that that's what happens in all situations. It's not. Nor is the reverse true in all cases. It all depends on what hardware you're using.

2. Even if it were a problem, forum members "ignoring" it or not acknowledging its existence does not stop developers from moving forward. It wouldn't matter if a handful of us here thought, "Ubuntu's perfect. Let's just sit back, relax, and not improve it." We're not the people developing it. The developers are employees of Canonical, and they work hard to make each new release support more hardware, have more GUI frontends for configuration, and include newer versions of software. No one who's actually involved in Ubuntu's software development is sitting on her *** thinking, "Ah... nothing left to do."

3. There's no way that Ubuntu could possibly support **every** single printer out there, no matter how hard the developers work. Some printer manufacturers simply do not make Linux versions of their drivers or release their code so that Ubuntu developers can make their own drivers. Reverse engineering closed source Windows drivers is not easy.

So what does your whining amount to? In the long run, not much. It's just a drop in a sea of whine and whinge on these forums, and it does nothing to improve Ubuntu or help new users adopt Ubuntu.

[What's better than whining on the forums? Making a difference.](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=78741)

---

### Post by hermesrules on 2006-05-04
[QUOTE=earobinson]Where is the option to say that windows is just better? Or to say linux just needs time, or that linux is not designed to be used by the advrage user.
[/QUOTE]

Sadly, the "Linux is not designed to be used by the average user" thinking is what hinders Linux proliferation. I know there are a lot of arguments, historic too, not just technical, about how Linux started, what it represents, etc. But in my opinion, the reality is changing really fast. Take a look at distrowatch.com - the first few distributions with the thousands of clicks per day are the "easy" ones - Ubuntu, Fedora, Suse. Not Debian, not Gentoo, although the latter two are very high in the chart. Look the pace with which the Linux enthusiasts are multiplying, and I bet you will see exponential growth during the past two years that far surpasses the growth in all of the previous years of Linux development and evolution.

And the reason is simple - Linux will ONLY become more popular and more visible once the non-geek users find it not only difficult, but useful. I have a tendency to push my learning curves quite high, so I enjoyed learning by trial and error, playing with distros just for the heck of it. But users with below-average knowledge of computers would not be like this. Blame it on Windows, or on their ignorance, but in either case, Linux is getting more popular because it is getting more accessible and more friendly, NOT simply because it is "better".

If it weren't that late already, I'd probably start writing on a really long non-technical essay to elaborate further on my thoughts. I think the Ubuntu idea catches it so well - Linux for human beings. NOT for geeks...

And yes, one could reasonably argue about the uneven contribution of developers vs. regular users, and rightly say that without the geeky commitment, this would not be possible. It is true that regular users would just wait for and demand newer and newer functions, better eye-candy, improved support for wireless, multimedia, etc. But this is the path of future development, and it has to be cordially split among the whole community of Linux geeks, enthusiast, occasional users, or fed-up-with-MS-crap/model. We all use Linux for different reasons, and we should all respect our motivations and contributions.

### Post by Bloch on 2006-05-04
What is preventing Linux from widespread proliferation?

Clear documentation is a major part of it. I include under this: installation guide, printed books, quick start guide in the help menu of each application, pop-up wizards which offer a choice of simple or advanced.

Nicotine for example came with no help under the help menu, only "about Nicotine". Such a great piece of software, and otherwise so user-friendly. But you have to assign a directory to share before it will connect.  

Linux users rely heavily on information available on the web. This connects to a second "problem" - the number of distros, desktop managers, kernel versions, repositories, rpms etc. One never knows if the information will apply to your system. And the user is likely to stumble across a site which says "it's easy to install this software. Just unzip the tarball and ./configure make
In other words there is no distinction made in the linux world between power users and what you might call the consumer user. The ordinary user is liable to run into a software installation problem and fall into a bottomless pit in trying to solve it.

I use only ubuntu documentation, and the ubuntu world is big enough to cover vast areas of help, even those which apply to linux in general.
But if a website manager or hardware manufacturer produces guides for linux users they will have a hard time. Take a look at this:
[http://www.bbc.co.uk/radio/audiohelp_nix.shtml](http://www.bbc.co.uk/radio/audiohelp_nix.shtml)

I think in future there will be a split between hardcore linux users and one or two distros (ubuntu included) for the masses.

### Post by racoq on 2006-05-05
I think that the biggest setback that prevents Linux to widespread, is the lack of information of people, about Linux Distros.

Until recently all people thought that linux is only for experts, (Hell! Even i've thinked in the past like this). So i've found in forums, references to ubuntu, and so i've gave it a trie. What i can say, is that in my opinion fewer releases of linux are as easy to install as Ubuntu (abstracting the user of what packages he has to install in the standard installation, and making easy partitioning was a great idea).

Other thing that people think, is that for having a distribution of linux, we have to get 4 ou 5 cd's (that's anoying) or 1 DVD. For Ubuntu Dapper for instance 1 700 MB CD will do the trick (previous versions, a 650 MB was enough)

So i think publicity is not the major problem here. People know linux, but they don't feel enough motivation to try it, because they are affraid of the problems that they can have, and are unable to resolve, this idea is WRONG, the support given to the users in this forum is great!

I think what linux needs is encouragement, from stores who sell computers and hardware companies for people to use install linux, even if they have windows in their machines.

See for instance the lack of interest that hardware enterprises that build wireless cards give to linux. Thei're only interest , is to build wireless card for the dominant OS (Windows), although there are exceptions like SMC.

As for store who sell computers, they could give people an option for buying a computer with a windows version, or with a Linux version, and motivate the client that a user can do all the important tasks in linux, as it does on Windows. In linux we can break the copyright laws because it's absolutelly FREE.

Well sorry about the length of my reply, but it is all that i think about this subject.

### Post by TheCaptain on 2006-05-05
[QUOTE=paulgroovy]It's nice so many of you can get these simple things to work.  I've never had problems installing a printer in Windows.  The fact is that if Linux is to gain any sort of widespread acceptance, these things need to be working.  The average person doesn't want to spend countless days configuring his computer to work, he just wants it to work.  That is a fact.  They don't want to know the intricacies of cups or whatever else there is to know.  They just want things to work.  Despite these problems I'm hard pressed to switch from Ubuntu because of all the distros I've tried so far it's the one I like best.

### Post by bonefishchris on 2006-05-05

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-05
[QUOTE=bonefishchris]Nearly every time you need to look up how to do something in the wiki or in the forum, you get faced with a page full of command line instructions; even when you're trying to do something that's simple to do in Windows, like install a program. All that stuff needs to be do-able via the GUI or your standard user just won't bother.[/QUOTE] It is do-able via the GUI. It's just easier to give instructions via the command-line, and it's more difficult to describe accurately point-and-click steps. It also takes up more bandwidth to post screenshots online.

Let's take your example... for example.

Installing software. Here's my tutorial--totally command-line:
[http://psychocats.net/ubuntu/installingsoftware](http://psychocats.net/ubuntu/installingsoftware)

Here's someone else's tutorial--totally GUI:
[http://monkeyblog.org/ubuntu/installing.html](http://monkeyblog.org/ubuntu/installing.html)

Same ways of installing software, even simpler than in Windows--I just don't have the bandwidth to be doing all those screenshots, and I believe that screenshots, while making new users feel more comfortable, leave more room for error.

Copying and pasting an instruction is more likely to work than following instructions like, "Yes, then click on this box, and then after it reloads, right-click this," especially if interfaces differ. With point-and-click instructions, you're more likely to encounter situations where the tutorial says, "Then click on this button," and you're trying to follow the instructions and thinking, "Uh, I don't have that button." I think the MonkeyBlog tutorial is great, but people using KDE will have no idea where this "Synaptic Package Manager" is. They don't have Synaptic Package Manager. They have Adept.

Command-line works whether you're using KDE, Gnome, XFCE, Fluxbox or IceWM. It doesn't matter what theme you have installed or what your default text editor is. It's more reliable, no matter how intimidating it is, and it's still easier to give instructions via commands to copy and paste.

It doesn't mean these things aren't **do-able** via the GUI; it just means giving instructions in the GUI is:

1. more time-consuming
2. more bandwidth-consuming
3. more prone to user error
4. more prone to confusion when the interface doesn't match the instructions

Read more about this [here](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=59334). Your "I'm sorry, guys" comes off as extremely condescending, especially when your objections are unsubstantiated.

---

> **bonefishchris]
The question was, if I may paraphrase it said:**
>  I had to do research on the internet to find out how to install stuff in Mac OS X--a supposedly "intuitive" and "user-friendly" environment. I don't understand why people moving to a new operating system would assume they have nothing to learn.

You think you could sit a child or adult down at a Windows computer--someone who has never seen or touched a computer before--and say, "Install a new program," and they'd immediately search around the internet for some setup.exe and double-click it?

The only thing discoverable for "installing" software in Windows is in the Control Panel--something called *Add/Remove Programs*, which is mainly used for removing programs. I don't see any setup.exes there.

Ubuntu is far more intuitive. There's an item in the menus called Add Applications, and it actually adds applications (downloads and installs them for you) when you click the appropriate options.

I think you're just so entrenched in Windows that you forgot you had to learn how to use it. Windows is no more or less intuitive than Gnome or KDE. You're just used to it.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-06
[QUOTE=bonefishchris]However, as I remember, when you come across something you like the look of on the Web, you click on 'Download', follow the instructions on the web page that tell you to double click the .exe and Windows more or less does the rest.[/quote] You're assuming people already know to look on the web for programs with download pages.

> 
Anyway perhaps the example of installing software is bogging us down a bit. It was just supposed to be an example of how one seems to have to resort to the command line frequently, something which, in my opinion, your average user will resist or at least be very dubious about doing. After all they are being asked to use a kind of interface that they don't understand to give instructions they don't understand to a system they don't understand. I don't say it can't be learned. I just ask if people are willing to put the time in to do that, especially if they have been brought up with Windows as most people have. You're right, installing software was a bad example. There are *some* things you do, in fact, have to resort to the command-line for, but I think for the vast majority of basic functions that "average users" will need, everything is point-and-click in Linspire, Mepis, and PCLinuxOS (not necessarily Ubuntu). 

I define "average user" as one who would never install an operating system and probably doesn't even know what an operating system is, one who generally needs to ask others for help on how to troubleshoot (would never sign up for a forum to ask how to fix something, though), and who doesn't try out new software--basically has a routine.

Do I think usability can be better on many Linux distributions (including Ubuntu)? Sure--same for Windows, too, though. 

Could there be more GUI frontends for tasks in Ubuntu? Yes, and as long as people can still do those same tasks from the command-line, I don't think anyone will complain if those GUI frontends appear.

Is the lack of a GUI frontend for reinstalling Grub to the MBR (which, by the way Mepis has, even though Ubuntu doesn't) "the biggest setback that is preventing Linux [from being] widespread"? Hardly.

Think about your "average user," as defined above. She'll boot up her computer (press the "on" button), log in, click on the web browser button, check her email, look up a few recipes, check the news for that day, maybe the weather. She might do some online shopping. Maybe she decides to make a shopping list for groceries (for that recipe she found), so she fires up a word processor. During all this, she's listening to some music. After all that, she shuts down her computer. That's all GUI.

In fact, having needs myself that aren't entirely sophisticated, I'm hard-pressed to think of something I do with my Ubuntu computer that I **must** use the command-line for in *every* Linux distribution. Here are many things that have GUI frontends:

1. Browsing as root within a user account
2. Zipping and unzipping files (or tarring and untarring)
3. Changing the clock time
4. Adjusting the taskbar size
5. Launching applications
6. Installing software (though we've beat this one to death)

I mean, what does the average user do that can't be done via point-and-click?

> 
By the way, I'm not 'entrenched' in Windows, I wouldn't be here if I was:) I'm entrenched in Windows, and I'm here. While I've been trying hard to adopt Ubuntu, I still very much come with a Windows mindset, since I've been using Windows since the very beginning, and DOS before that. It's hard to get out of that. It's like learning a foreign language--you might be dedicated to learning it and spent years studying it, but your native accent will still come back to haunt you.

I think a good way to think about what Linux obstacles are on the desktop is to compare it to OS X. In many ways OS X faces the same obstacles Linux distributions do: small user base, lack of games and a lot of commercial applications (well, except Adobe and a few others), lack of hardware compatibility. Apple is able to get around this with a few tactics Linux distros could employ, I think:

1. **Marketing**--telling people that there's something special and different about Mac. Some Linux distros (like Linspire) try too hard to be like Windows, so when people use it... they expect Windows, and they get something different, so they're disappointed. For a large chunk of the 1990s, Apple kept advertising itself with the slogan *Think Different.**** Although grammatically incorrect, it was brilliant--at once appealing to people's desires to be individual, unique, and non-conformist, while at the same time prepping them for culture shock, letting them know not to expect a Windows-like experience.

2. **Branding**. There are very few Linux-branded computers, and the ones that exist are not easy to find. A lot of those Linux vendors, too, essentially distribute Windows computers and then put Linux on them (System 76 is an exception). Part of what makes Mac OS X user-friendly, even though it's different, is that Apple makes the hardware (or commissions it, at least), so you know Tiger is going to install okay on your previously Panther Mac. Most people install Linux on a built-from-scratch or previously Windows PC, and that's where they run into trouble if they haven't done their research.

3. **Branding third-party products.** This is mainly where my "average user" would run into trouble with a Linux distribution. You can get around #1 and #2, because anyone who's an "average user" using Linux probably had a Linux user install and configure Linux for her. I remember when my wife was looking for a printer for her Powerbook or a non-iPod MP3 player, she had to carefully look for that light-blue/dark-blue smiley face on products to see if things were Mac-compatible. Few products have a picture of Tux on them to let you know they're Linux-compatible. You have to find hardware compatibility lists online to figure that out.

And that's where the average user would run into trouble. In fact, my wife ended up getting another iPod because we just weren't sure some of the non-iPod MP3 players she was looking at would work with her Mac. They *probably* would--as most would just show up as a removable drive where you could drag and drop songs to--but most people don't want to shell out money for something that *probably* will be compatible with their systems. At least the printers had some Mac-compatibility in them (not all of them, though).

There are some people on these forums (not you, bonefishchris) who think that I and other Linux advocates deny that there's anything wrong with Ubuntu or Linux or that there's no way in which either could improve. I just want to set the record straight here--there's a lot Ubuntu and Linux in general could improve. I usually object to certain people's criticisms of Ubuntu/Linux for one or both of the following reasons:

1. They're simply not true. While there are things you could criticize Ubuntu/Linux for, often the things people criticize are not real problems, even though they may have reason to believe those are problems (say, people constantly giving command-line instructions when GUI options are available; or a weird hardware situation that causes an error for one person that most other people aren't experiencing).

2. People bring it up criticisms and make it sound as whining on forums is a way to make change or that if they didn't pipe up the Ubuntu developers would be twiddling their thumbs, not improving the OS. Just as a reminder to folks--if you want to help Ubuntu, contribute code, file bug reports, donate money, write documentation, and help other users. There's no other way to improve Ubuntu.

Again, this last bit is not targeted at you, bonefishchris--I just need to vent a little bit about something I've seen quite often here that's related to our little discussion.

*** For the curious among you, the grammatically correct way to do the Apple slogan would be either *Think "Different"* or *Think Differently*.

### Post by hermesrules on 2006-05-08
[QUOTE=RavenOfOdin]Personally, I think the Microsoft business model very much contributes to the lack of 3rd party support.

(Hint: Lawsuits)[/QUOTE]

You know, I am not sure I agree with you entirely, but I think I do see your point. I recently had a client (nothing to do with computers, as computers are like a hobby for me, not a profession), who was exploring some online opensource project management application, and as we were exanching emails, I saw their recent conversation with a coworker, who had identified the OSS thing, and suggested they give it a try. The response was: "I am not sure this is a secure system since these guys are volunteers"... You can't get a better example of how existing myths about OSS reinforce themselves through such thinking.

Well, think about this myth, and how spread it might be. I think it is such a common misconception that the OSS world, because it is de facto offered for free, must be less legitimate and therefore ignorant of any accountability standards. So, in fact, it is NOT MS's model only that contributes to the myth, it is also the very nature of OSS, which sometimes seems in a constant stage of develpoment and is potentially "breakable" (in terms of unmet dependencies). This is ONLY one side, but it is the one that only those of us who are not average Joes and Janes are able to recognize and know the complete story.

Is is somewhat of an oxymoron to say that Windows must be secure because it is paid. But, in reality, people do believe that there is much greater accountabilty behind something, which is made for money. And this is why they tend to trust it. Now put yourselves in the shoes of the computer stores - how would you sell (without a carefully thought out strategy) to a crowd which thinks that what you are selling lacks accountability? How many people, who are used to calling Microsoft on the phone and paying for that $250 a call DEMANDING a solution (for the price they are paying), would change to patient bug-filers at no cost? I bet not too many...

On the other hand, we can read plenty of "reports" from users around the forums to be switching from Windows to Linux, however, success stories of businesses doing the same move are not that plentiful, and I wish it were otherwise. To be able to counterattack Microsoft's business model, I think that the least we need is for businesses to start converting and translating that into success stories with wider publicity than just the forums...

Wow, too long again...

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-09
I've given it some thought, and something else that's a big setback...

**The ideal users for Linux** are usually the people who would be least likely to try it or install it:

People who have only a handful of programs they use (word processor, music player, web browser, email client, file browser), never update their software unless they have to, and rarely buy new hardware.

**The most likely to be unhappy with Linux** are usually the same people who give it a shot:

Windows power users who always want the newest versions of software, want random obscure software for very particular needs, often buy new hardware, want everything point-and-click (even advanced configuration options).

Thankfully, I'm somewhere in the middle. I'm advanced enough that I've actually bothered installing Linux, but my needs are simple (no weird obscure programs, no need to have the absolute latest version of each program).

---

### Post by darkenedday on 2006-05-09
I personally believe it's the same reason I was somewhat frustrated with linux wheni first installed Ubuntu, the average user does'nt like to have to work to get their PC running, they simply want it to run, windows comes pre-installed on 90% of the systems sold, and also, for majority of the apps that a person might want, the simply download and open the .exe and everything is done for them, no downloading a .deb or a tarball you have to unzip and navigate to yourself, no command line at all unless you wish to use it. Also supposrt for gaming is a big issue almost none of the games out there have support for linux built-in, and many people don't want a system that doesn't support what they want to do, sure if you happen to have much higher than average computer knowledge, then sure you can find a way to make alot of things work, but this is far beyond the capabilities of an average, and i dare say, lazy Windows user. Linux has a little way to go before it's ready for the masses of people who don't want to learn, and just want it to work. I can see this happening somewhere in the near futur with Kubuntu/Ubuntu/Xubuntu being they are already easier to use than many Distro's I have tried. Also might help if windows would admit defeat on ActiveX. Linux needs time, Windows will die off, linux I see beeing around forever, it's solid and safe, give it time and it will be the frontrunner I'm sure, also many people are saying Linux doesnt have enough publicity...](*,)  HELLO!!!! IT'S OPENSOURCE if you want it to have more publicity get out there and do something about it, burn some cd's hand them out with small HOWTO's and see what happens, make an add or two and stand in front of bestbuy, even a local channel commercial who knows, you're aloud to reproduce and distribute ubuntu/kubuntu/xubuntu as much as you wish, get the word out!

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-11
While I can understand why electroconvulsive would feel this way (just look back through electroconvulsive's posting history), I have never heard "Windows is easier to install" from people who have installed Windows on a System76 computer. And, really, that's the only fair comparison.

Install Linux on a Windows-preloaded computer--easy or difficult.
Install Windows on a Linux-preloaded computer--we don't know...

Considering that I have installed Windows 2000 and Windows XP from scratch (once each) and on computers that previously ran Windows (these were **not** Linux-preloaded computers), I can tell you right now it's not easy.

Installing an operating system from scratch always leaves open the possibility that hardware will not be detected. In Ubuntu, at least you have the advantage of running a live CD to see how well stuff will be detected. In Windows, no such luck.

Honestly, electroconvulsive, most of your problems stem from hardware detection. Buy a System76 and then complain. I know very few Windows users who installed Windows themselves. Most have Dell do it for them. If installing Windows were so easy, Dell, HP, Sony, etc. would not install it for you. They'd just give you a computer and a Windows CD (no peripheral CD with drivers, though). I can assure you if that happened, no one would be exclaiming that Windows is easier to install...

---

### Post by centered effect on 2006-05-11
I have not read everyone's posts so forgive me if I repeat someone.

I think what would help Linux out the most is:

1.  Have the distros/users pay money for codecs, drivers, etc.  I hate the fact that it is illegal for me to install the codecs in the US because I use a different OS.  Give the user the option to choose the free version without these codecs and one with.  

2. Write and petition to companies for them to make Linux versions of thier software.  These companies need to see a demand, then there will be a market for Linux.

{Adobe and Macromedia being one of them in my opinion. I question why Macromedia gave Linux the flash player, but not Flash - the program (or any of the Macromedia products for that matter).} 

3. Games.  Great games.  

One idea would be to make a game for Linux only and let the gaming world see it and play it etc. seeing the Linux environment, then slowly releasing it to other platforms. (ex Halo on Xbox, then to PC)

(I am waiting patiently for Quake 3 powered games for Linux, since the game source and the map editor - which doesn't work in Linux btw, source has been released.  Quake 3 is a great platform to create games from.)

4. Standardized installers.  .sh seems universal throughout the Linux distros.  So why not make it double-clickable like the .exe is?  Have the package-whataver.sh be able to look for dependancies and have sources available to have them downloaded from if the system doesn't have them (of course you would have to be root to install it).

5.  OSX anyone? Seriously, I am waiting for someone/company that will make a distro like what Apple did with BSD.  Ubuntu is not it in my opinion (the brown turns me away for one...).  And like OSX and Windows, someone has to bundle it with computers.  Make a system that people cannot ignore - perhaps a Mac mini killer? A $2-300 desktop system that is not crappy? 

6.  Don't try and follow, be a leader.  Make true alternatives.  Can't get Flash for linux, look at synfig ([http://www.synfig.com/)](http://www.synfig.com/)), and if they have thier own outout format, lets make a browser that accepts those formats (like the swf files) and get people to use them, show off their skills and get people excited about the product!! ( I had more, but my mind is giving me blanks lol!)

I think Linux is there.  No other OS comes bundled with a full office suite, image program, chat programs, etc. That is a huge plus to new users, so they don't have to get any other programs to start using their system.

### Post by Carrots171 on 2006-05-12
**The biggest obstacle is that Linux isn't pre-loaded on enough PC's.** Most people won't bother to install another operating system on their computer (which can be time consuming, difficult, and annoying, and this can be true for installing Linux or Windows). It's far easier just to buy a computer with an OS pre-loaded and to start using it. As long as computers don't come with Linux pre-loaded, the average person won't use Linux. 

Sure, 3rd party support is another issue, but look at it this way: Once Linux is more popular with the "average person", more and more companies will release drivers, make their products compatible with Linux, etc. Once enough companies provide support for Linux, it will grow further. And the first step towards Linux becoming popular in the mainstream is having computers come pre-loaded with Linux. It is simply not possible for Linux to become popular with the "average person" until he/she can go to Circuit City or Best Buy, find out about what Linux is and what its advantages are, and easily buy one and get tech support for it.

### Post by kencoe on 2006-05-12
It boils down to two simple issues which Microsoft grabbed to their advantage a long time ago. 

One- Most people buy what is easiest. Linux does not support a lot of formats that people want. Thery don't want to avoid sites that require Media player, or shockwave. Microsoft make media agreements to take advantage of this (like CNN). They also don't want to have to go through extra steps of installing add-ins. Microsoft puts it all in one "nifty" install, and makes installation of other programs just an issue of inserting the CD. This is also why home PC sales go up when a new OS is released. Many people will upgrade the entire PC to avoid the installation issues.

Two- Most people will always buy what they know first. They bought MAC when that was what was in school, then switched to buying PCs with DOS/Windows when Microsoft made it's big business drive in the 80's. People use windows at work, they learn windows at work, they use windows at home (because they don't need to re-learn). 

Answer those two issues, and windows is dead in the water. Nothing before

---

If a 'beginner' desktop contained icons for "Word Processor", "Video Player", "Jukebox", "Web Browser" and "HELP (Gaim with automatic UbuntuNoob channel)"  then they would be a lot less scared of it.

In fact, an UbuntuNOOB channel would be a fantastic idea if you could man it with people that dont ignore the punters because their question is too basic... sometimes it would be an idea to teach people how to Google properly - I'm sure i could do with that. 

The 'too basic' question thing is definately an issue, although I do appreciate how much work the help channel guys put in - not too mention how greatthey've been for me - their patience has been outstanding - as has their help, knowledge and usefulness - in fact - I stay ubuntu because it has, since the off, been the best computing experience I have had with regards to support. If a noob channel can be on the desktop and easily found and the bot used to point them to google/forum/wiki pages to talk them through stuff they'd gain confidence fast. 

Linux peeps use too much Jargon to encourage noobs in too. Noobs have a fear of breaking - they need their hand held for a while. 

- lack of uniformity accross the linux distributions causes fear and confusion in noobs

- the fact tht it often just doesn't work :( 
ie)   why use totem instead of mplayer when over at least 4 installations on different machines, it just hasn't worked for me. Looking at forums I'm not unique... why not install as fairly minimal & offer choices & recommendations to users? why install a 'non working peice of software?

but I ramble (Sorry - but you did ask!)

### Post by henriquemaia on 2006-05-12
Lack of Drivers/vendors support. 

Not everyone thinks beforehand on a future instalation of Linux in their machine. Sometimes, and because some specific vendor is not fully supported by Linux (by the community or the vendor itself), Linux instalations can be a nightmare. The graphical card doesn't work as expected, the soundcard, etc. One gets the wrong (first) impression of it.

### Post by centered effect on 2006-05-12
[QUOTE=centered effect]I want to bring up another point as well but I wll have to compose it llater.  It involves blogs, CMS, and PHP5/Sqlite.  Sqlite doesn't need mysql database which confuses some who don't understand it.  This can draw users who would not normally think about a web server option into the server/hosting world.  This could possible shed new tools and new ideas to help out the web community.  Not a full idea but a start...[/QUOTE]


Ok let me elaborate.  Alot of people blog, either with bogger, live journal, myspace, etc.  Why because it is free and you don't have to set up anything.  
Why not inlcude a blog/CMS to be uploaded to a web host, within the distro? 

Why? Customization purposes, own domain - [www.joeshmoe.com](www.joeshmoe.com), no restrictions, etc.  How? I have only read about this so it looks good on papaer and in theory.  Php5 includes Sqlite, a faster text db with no configuration, this would help with installers (permissions included!!) and creating tables for mysql that would put off most users (command line anyone?).  

Most web servers still have PHP4.3 and have not updated yet, but mnay are.  Now this may not help the costs of web servers since many would opt for the cheaper solution, but with the numbers, it should level out.  and with those wanting more space and bandwidth,  they will shell out the money.

This could help make people more aware of LAMPand have everyday users possibly want to learn about it.  It can also help developers work on new solutions (last time I checked there were not alot of sqlite based CMS/blogs), inlcuding hosting services (imac and ilife anyone?!?!?!?!?!?!).  As far as I know Windows doesn't have something like this but Mac does (as stated before but not fully).  

Again, just an idea...

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-12
I think there is a large diversity of interests, resources, and motivations within Linux application development.

Some people like to tinker around and just see what they can do. Some people create software solely for themselves ("I'm trying to accomplish a particular task, and I'm going to create software to do it"). Others are paid developers working for Red Hat, Novell, Canonical, or Linspire.

Developers do not have to be slave to end-users, nor do they have to care only about their own needs; though, both situations could arise, depending on the nature of the project.

Ubuntu developers--while not slaves to end-users--do happen to care (as far as I've seen at Malone and Bugzilla) about end-users. I think it's a healthy perspective to realize that Ubuntu developers care about end-users needs, but it's also appropriate to realize that you are using free software. You have no right to demand, only to suggest.

The only things you can do to improve Ubuntu (or any open source software, for that matter) are these (notice that whining on the forums is not one of them):

1. Donate money
2. File bug reports
3. Contribute code
4. Write documentation

If you're not doing those things, **you**'re not helping Ubuntu or its associated applications advance. That's the bottom line.

[QUOTE=aysiu]
The only things you can do to improve Ubuntu (or any open source software, for that matter) are these (notice that whining on the forums is not one of them):

1. Donate money
2. File bug reports
3. Contribute code
4. Write documentation

If you're not doing those things, **you**'re not helping Ubuntu or its associated applications advance. That's the bottom line.[/QUOTE]

I'd like to add #5. Tell your friends.

I've moved people to Ubuntu (at least dual booters). For open source in general, it doesn't hurt to let people know an app exists, many are cross-platform. 

It's not hard to tempt users to switch to firefox, videolan, and openoffice, etc (well less daunting than switching OS's anyway.)

### Post by ThirdWorld on 2006-05-13
[QUOTE=aysiu]I think there is a large diversity of interests, resources, and motivations within Linux application development.

Some people like to tinker around and just see what they can do. Some people create software solely for themselves ("I'm trying to accomplish a particular task, and I'm going to create software to do it"). Others are paid developers working for Red Hat, Novell, Canonical, or Linspire.

Developers do not have to be slave to end-users, nor do they have to care only about their own needs; though, both situations could arise, depending on the nature of the project.

Ubuntu developers--while not slaves to end-users--do happen to care (as far as I've seen at Malone and Bugzilla) about end-users. I think it's a healthy perspective to realize that Ubuntu developers care about end-users needs, but it's also appropriate to realize that you are using free software. You have no right to demand, only to suggest.

The only things you can do to improve Ubuntu (or any open source software, for that matter) are these (notice that whining on the forums is not one of them):

1. Donate money
2. File bug reports
3. Contribute code
4. Write documentation

If you're not doing those things, **you**'re not helping Ubuntu or its associated applications advance. That's the bottom line.[/QUOTE]


well i think that trying to reason with the linux developer community is like try to reason with a coral reef... is just pure chaos, spawning everywhere, for no particular reason, no plan, no purpose...

### Post by ThirdWorld on 2006-05-13
[QUOTE=aysiu]Where have you reasoned with the developer community? Can you point to a few bugs you've filed on Malone or Bugzilla?[/QUOTE]

I have done that, in ubuntu, openoffice and amarok too... its kind of complicated, you have to create an account etc, its not like in windows where if the application crash developers received an automatic report from your system. amarok do that but it fire up the kde mail app and that make the application crash...

but the biggest bug, bug #1 is not windows itself, is developers who dont listen to end users, and honestly dont care about them... I guess thats why as a linux user I need:

amarok to listen to my .mp3 lybraries

streamtuner to listen radio streams, then load those streams back to amarok

easytag to manage and write my mp3 tags

soundjuicer to import music from a CD, then add manually those new files to amarok

gnomebaker to burn a CD from my .mp3 collection, of course i have to dig deep for the files

then i need gnomad2 to transfer my new mp3s to and from my zen micro


and if i have downloaded music i need sound converter to convert diferent audio formats to .mp3s


Or i can just log out from ubuntu, restart my system and log in back to windows and use itunes or windows media player and do all those things with just one application.

Do you guys still dont get my point? or you think im still whinning around?

### Post by richbarna on 2006-05-13
I think the problems with non Linux user are down to stereotypes and past bad experiences.
Linux is seen as a "Geek" operating system by most windows users, and that you need a lot of programming and technical skills just to get it installed.
This stereotype leads to people judging before trying.
I think that the KDE and Gnome desktops should be more widely advertised so that potential Linux users will see that all of the distro's have come a long way since console, text-only environments.
