### Post by janrocks on 2006-05-13
Ok..seeing as this is an open question here goes...

Randomness... uncertainty of operation..
I had to reboot due to an ethernet card failing, so I swapped it for an exactly identical one.. this was about 30 minutes ago.. back up and online..no problem..

Leave everything to pop out for a few minutes, closing firefox as normal..Return and restart firefox to be presented with a create account screen.. The only account is default, so I select that, to be presented with the message "Account is already in use..please choose another"  What other??.. there isn't one.. anyway, give up and restart firefox...to find all my links that took me 3 days to put into a new install have gone...](*,)  Fed up...Too right..and becoming increasingly annoyed at the awkwardness of the whole thing.. I haven't even managed to burn a CD in a week of trying..Where is the <make> command?? I can't find it anywhere in Ubuntu..a real pain. it's stopping me installing applications I NEED!!!..income is suffering and I'm getting to the point of returning to the hated XP because I am starting to feel there isn't an option:-k .. That's why in, my opinion, Linux isn't more accepted.. I know quite a bit about computing but it's all the annoying little (and big) things that are really starting to **** me off ...

Sorry for the rant but I'm an increasingly frustrated user who is starting to consider other options (like being a roadsweeper!):mad:

I haven't even managed to burn a CD in a week of trying..Where is the <make> command?? I suggest posting questions in the beginner section. 
To get make, download the package build-essential from the repos. Have you enabled universe? Average users shouldn't have to build packages from source. There are 18,000 or so packages in the repo ready for install.

What type of CD are you trying to burn?
To burn files, go to places, choose CD/DVD creator, drag and drop the files, click write to disc. If you want to burn an iso, right click it and choose Write to Disc.
If it's an audio CD, use serpentine.

Not sure about the firefox issues (maybe it was changing the ethernet card? They would have unique MAC addresses...), just try making a new profile, continue as normal. I'm not sure how you lost your bookmarks. For now you can go to Bookmarks (in the firefox menu), choose manage, and there is an import/export (so you can keep a backup of your bookmarks)

Don't spend a week banging your head, just ask questions. People here will help you.

### Post by janrocks on 2006-05-13
Yeah cool  thanks, I can't get back to the default account with firefox...never mind..

I got a lot more issues than that.. just letting off some steam about what people go through and why they tend to get a very bad impression very quickly.. 

I do a lot of "technical" type burning of weird formats (xiso on dvd) and the like, from mounted images that I can't find any way to create now..

My other gripe really is 100 different ways of doing basic things across 100 different distros..even adding a utility is a headache..they go in and disappear.. Then everything (well, what I need to use anyway) seems to be only in rpm packages.. and because of the lack of make I can't even install alien to sort them out..Why should I have to download and install what is in reality a basic and essential linux tool? I'd use the fedora distro but redhat is too ram hungry, and comes with too much "junk"...  Losing all my bookmarks for the second time in a week is the clincher.. I can't afford to lose any more work time and income ******* about with it.....shame really..this machine is a lot faster with Ubuntu.. but the old adage "Time is money" comes back and bites... Maybe I'll have a look at slackware 10 tomorrow..maybe not.. It seems ok on another machine in the house.. But I think ubuntu and most of the rest of the linux distro's have a long way to go before they become "user friendly" enough to boot ms out of the game...and I really dislike microsoft...broken os running broken software!!  lol   >rock and hard place<

Well good luck to you. Dunno about RPM's so I didn't pick up any different habits. When I had tried mandrake 7, every rpm I tried to install didn't work. It was then I learned to build from source. :) I was only using debian based (and debian for a bit) until I went to Ubuntu...

Make isn't in there by default, as a lot of people don't need it... Just one of those choices they made to keep ubuntu one CD.

As for doing things differently, that's a perk to Linux. But for stuff you mention being unique to each distro... I think that's what the LSB is for (Linux Standard Base), so hopefully this issue will disappear. For unique things, like the package manager, the friendly front-ends (i.e. synaptic or Add/Remove) should eliminate any sort of steep learning curve.

P.S. Are you sure you've enabled universe? What apps are you looking for that are rpm only? I'm betting they are in synaptic. If you are used to going to a webpage and getting the individual file then installing, that's not how things are normally done in debian based distros... You can do it... but the central package manager makes things easy.

Well sounds like you can figure things out. :)

In your home folder, press ctrl + h (shows hidden files), .mozilla folder
that should have all your firefox stuff.

For the windows stuff, if WINE doesn't work, there is the option of QEMU (run XP in an emulator.)

What printer are you using? I can only vouch that hp printers are easy as pie to get up and running.

EDIT: Sorry if this has become "thread-jacking" if the mods want to move our Q&A to it's own thread, fine by me.

For the printer, mine's a hp deskjet 5740, System -> Administration -> Printing. See if it's detected / choose the model from the list. You should be golden at that point. :) If it doesn't see it, good chance the usb cable isn't in (or the printer is off ;) )

Good luck on getting more use out of the machine.

### Post by KeyboardJockey on 2006-05-14
I don't know i i'm speaking out of turn here but as some who is trying to ditch Windows I think there are several reasons why Linux is not taking off.

There is a general 'user unfriendliness' about the human / computer interface with Linux which to someone who has been brought up on Macs and Win is very difficult to get your head around.

On the corporate side I think that is laziness and the fact that most IT people are trained on Win.  When I suggested at work making a consumer form on disc readable in LInux as well as Win I was laughed out with the words 'no business will use Linux'.

Upgrades break the system.

There is no central point for definitive information.  There are always several different opinions about ways to solve problems not all of them work.

Hardware issues.

If you having big big problems like I am you can't just pick up the desktop take it down to the local PC shop and say heres x amount of money fix it please as the local shops have no idea about Linux.

Lack of books on specific distros - being a learner and having to flip between different opinions in different windows and different fora is causing me a huge amount of problems - I'd really like a book tohold in my hand to guide me through stuff.

No Linux based classes on basic 'how to's and stuff'

---

### Post by kencoe on 2006-05-14
There's been something which I've been watching for a while, and this post makes an excellent point of it.

There is something to be said for looking for usability improvements, which are underway. There is also some room for discussion on selection of default applications. I do see alot of people complaining on the linux boards (like any other) about issues which are of their own doing. I'm sorry if that offends some, but it is true. We need to separate issues with Linux from issues with apps installed. We also have a trolling problem, which I will get to in a minute.

I hear alot of people complaining about specific apps, and small issues as a Linux problem when it is not the OS or the OSs defaults. When you install games and neat downloaded programs from cnet.com on a windows system, you can start to have some really interesting problems. This is not a Linux problem, but we argue back and forth about it like it is. This problem is with bad apps which cause problems, not bad OSs. Microsoft won't give you free support, or send out a repair, because you installed Yahoo Messenger and freaked out anything having to do with online usage (how many of us have been called to fix that?). I would be willing to gamble that most of the people complaining about software and OS instability have spent a lot of time in the Multiverse repository.

Same thing with upgradability. I have been doing Admin work for a long time, and anyone else who has can point out that the upgrade path issue is just as bad with Mac and Windows (anyboy hear remember the first release of SP6 for NT4?).

Personally, I think we should be looking a the usability of things like the File type registration, and the difficulty of a .man for a new user, but I'm sure that many of you have different issues that you would like to see improved. I'm sure our developers would like some input from newer user as to their troubles.

I won't say much on the issue of trolls, but I do have a couple questions. Is it just me, or do we have, porportionately, an much higher percentage of Trolls on the Linux sites? We actually put the Blogs to shame! Am I missing something here? Why are the Linux forums like troll magnets?

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-05-14
[QUOTE=kencoe]
I do see alot of people complaining on the linux boards (like any other) about issues which are of their own doing. I'm sorry if that offends some, but it is true.[/quote] I'll say. About 99% of the "problems" I encountered when I first started with Ubuntu were my own ignorance about how to do things. Now that I'm a year in (and, yes, it took only a year--unlike Windows, which took me well over a decade to master), I don't really have issues. Ubuntu runs just fine and suits my needs. Funny how it's the same operating system running on the same computer. What's the only variable in this experiment? Me.

> 
I hear alot of people complaining about specific apps, and small issues as a Linux problem when it is not the OS or the OSs defaults. When you install games and neat downloaded programs from cnet.com on a windows system, you can start to have some really interesting problems. This is not a Linux problem, but we argue back and forth about it like it is.  Yes and no. I agree that it's often third-party apps, but usually the good are mixed with the bad. If I go to KDE-Look, I'm just as likely to find a high-quality theme that looks great and doesn't bork my user interface as I am to find a theme that looks terrible and makes Konqueror not launch properly any more.

Likewise, there are applications like Gtkpod in the repositories that look like they were thrown together in someone's basement, and then there are applications like OpenOffice that are industrial strength and have corporate backing. Yes, I know Gtkpod is in the Universe repository, but I think the point stands. If you want to contest it, I can dig around the standard repositories to find another thrown-together application.

> 
This problem is with bad apps which cause problems, not bad OSs. Microsoft won't give you free support, or send out a repair, because you installed Yahoo Messenger and freaked out anything having to do with online usage (how many of us have been called to fix that?). I would be willing to gamble that most of the people complaining about software and OS instability have spent a lot of time in the Multiverse repository. At the same time, it's highly unlikely that new users to Ubuntu will not "need" applications from the Multiverse repository.

> 
Same thing with upgradability. I have been doing Admin work for a long time, and anyone else who has can point out that the upgrade path issue is just as bad with Mac and Windows (anyboy hear remember the first release of SP6 for NT4?). You may be interested in this thread: [My wife "dist-upgraded" to Tiger and it messed up her user profile...](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=80341)

> Personally, I think we should be looking a the usability of things like the File type registration, and the difficulty of a .man for a new user I agree. I can't figure out *man* pages to save my life. I can't fathom how the expression read the f'ing manual even came into existence. Those *man*ual pages are more cryptic than the commands they supposedly instruct you on how to use. Most of them don't even include examples. They just look like: ```
command [option] [host] [ -d -x -d -g -r -w] blah blah [other options] [-z -r -F]
``` That makes no sense to me. Sorry. I need an example!

> 
I won't say much on the issue of trolls, but I do have a couple questions. Is it just me, or do we have, porportionately, an much higher percentage of Trolls on the Linux sites? We actually put the Blogs to shame! Am I missing something here? Why are the Linux forums like troll magnets? Linux forums *are* troll magnets. I don't know what it is. Maybe it's the idea of getting "geeks" all riled up that entertains some people. And it doesn't matter how many times people say "Don't feed the trolls." Those trolls are well-fed here. They know where to get the grub.

---

### Post by DevSolar on 2006-05-15
Not having read the thread, just two words: Consistency and documentation.

Having to recompile your drivers on a kernel update. Every distro with its own way to start / stop services or set up things (like, where apache2 keeps its cgi-bin). Helpful posts in another distro's forum not helping you with yours. Several different ways to distribute software, including one distro's packages not working with another. Each distro coming with a different configuration / administration frontent.

And most of all, major parts of the community thinking that this is just fine. Present company hopefully excluded. ;)

---

### Post by kencoe on 2006-05-15
[QUOTE=aysiu] Yes and no. I agree that it's often third-party apps, but usually the good are mixed with the bad...Likewise, there are applications like Gtkpod in the repositories that look like they were thrown together in someone's basement, and then there are applications like OpenOffice that are industrial strength and have corporate backing. Yes, I know Gtkpod is in the Universe repository, but I think the point stands. If you want to contest it, I can dig around the standard repositories to find another thrown-together application.[/quote]

I could say the same with windows. Most users I hear complain about windows crashing have dozens of neat little downloaded apps instaklled on their system. There are several good apps you can install for Windows (Corel, Intuit), but just as many dogs, just like our repositories. Maybe we should look at an application (program) review section in the forums, or the wiki. Something else Windows doesn't do that would always be a nice addition.

[QUOTE=aysiu]At the same time, it's highly unlikely that new users to Ubuntu will not "need" applications from the Multiverse repository.[/quote]

I would probably say that, like cnet and the dozens of system optimization tools that are horrible AND unnecessary fpr windoes system use, users will play in the Multiverse just because it is there.

[QUOTE=aysiu] I agree. I can't figure out *man* pages to save my life. I can't fathom how the expression read the f'ing manual even came into existence. Those *man*ual pages are more cryptic than the commands they supposedly instruct you on how to use. [/quote]

This is another suggestion of mine. Most of us have realized by now that Linux/Unix Gurus have a thing for hokey names (bash, bicycle-repair), and that these can be very confusing to a new user. Maybe we should look toward developing some interface wizards for new users, or to be as helpful as possible, a new user software tutorial similar to what windows does on its 2k and XP systems (please not the 95/98 version). Alot of users don't want to browse the internet for the majority of their answers. For some reason I was OK with bash, but scratched my head forever on vi, don't ask me. 

I would also propose that we need to work on GPL agreements with some third party app vendors so that they can be placed in default install. At this point JAVA is definately a necessity on almost any system accessing the internet. Other people might have universal suggestions, like the writer of Automatix.

I am also glad to hear that it wasn't just me with the TROLLs. I was starting to wonder if I was getting thin skinned...

### Post by BoyOfDestiny on 2006-05-15
[QUOTE=kencoe]
I would also propose that we need to work on GPL agreements with some third party app vendors so that they can be placed in default install. At this point JAVA is definately a necessity on almost any system accessing the internet.[/QUOTE]

Well there was some sort of license change. I can verify that sun java is in the multiverse repos of dapper (both x86 and amd64) A default install of it? I haven't used java since November 2005. There is the GCJ by default, but not everything works... Anyway...

System -> Administration -> Software Properties (for some reason the window title says Software Prefences), has an easy way to enable the other repos, configure the update tool, etc. Users also have the option from synaptic...

As for software choices. The Add/Remove app in Dapper has gotten a lot of love indeed (I forgot who made this comment a while back). It even let's you filter out/by proprietary and unsupported apps. They have summaries, icons, links to the home page, etc. Windows has nothing on that.

### Post by RAV TUX on 2006-05-15
bottom line the masses buy a box they plug it in and they just want it to work. They could careless what OS the computer companies have put in the box.

The problem is more with Microsoft having an unethical monopoly and strangle hold on installation and distribution of  HP, Dell, etc. etc.

Yeah, Yeah I know the old story that some of these companies offer linux now. In reality go to  any one of these sites and windows is the OS of choice.

I just downloaded on my XPsp2 box, windows media player 11, while quite awesome. It seems to be incorporating standards found in Gmusicbrowser.

The biggest set back is

1. primarily on the distribution level

2. we need an OS that is geared towards the masses, music, video, games, multi media based, and throw in the standards, the Gimp, Firefox, Openoffice.org, etc

there are humble attempts at achieving this by new linux distros like:

Dreamlinux Studio Edition
[http://www.dreamlinux.com.br/english/index.html](http://www.dreamlinux.com.br/english/index.html)

&

Musix
[http://www.musix.org.ar/en/index.html](http://www.musix.org.ar/en/index.html)

While honorable and a worthy endeavor we need a major player like Ubuntu to take this as a serious endeavor with utmost urgency.

Burn and take a look at the Dreamlinux Studio Edition live CD, the first thing you get is a beautiful flash presentation with music.

I for one prefer Ubuntu and the multiply customization options but for most people the masses who are not here. Want something that just works and is prebuilt with all the multimedia knock yours socks off presentation and programs by default. What Linux lacks is bling for the masses. Dreamlinux Studio Edition is the best that I have seen to some what accomplish this.

I have to say I love Ubuntu just the way it is, I love gnome. I do believe that the Edgy Eft is the right way for Ubuntu to go, but it appears to late in the game.

Will Ubuntu Studio answer this calling? I don't know? but developers here and users should try Dreamlinux Studio Edition to see where Ubuntu could go, even if as a derivative .

For more intellectual in-depth discourse refer to this thread:
 "Why is the DeadCD Dead? & other existential conundrums& reflections of Linux Distros."

[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=176586](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=176586)

### Post by kencoe on 2006-05-16
[QUOTE=BoyOfDestiny]Especially? I hope I didn't make the app sound confusing. Here are some screencaps.[/QUOTE]
Sorry, BoyofDestiny, old admin habit. I tend to speak cautiously of what I hear about upgrades and new features. Too long working on MS boxes. Thanks for the screen shots! This looks very good! Will go a long way toward newbies, and casual users. I gotta get a box set up for Beta testing... 

I didn't see anything much about server admin referenced in thuis post, and I would mention that Linux can also take the desktop from the other direction. Many people use at home what they use at work. That is how MS Office unseated Corel Wordperfect. Linux could gain a major advantage with its portability and stability with a better Corporate domain front end, such as SSO, Net Interop, and Easier Centralization of settings (i.e. tree-style user admin, roaming profiles, push install). Gnome has made serious headway here,  as well as several third party vendors (RH), and we could see more of it with Novell getting alot more involved with Linux as a desktop. If they are really going to cooperate with us, that would make a BIG difference.

### Post by edmonds on 2006-05-19
i've been trying  to install linux for 6 years - and it is very hard (ie i haven't managed it yet) to get it to work. I  have tried maybe 20 different distros or variants of.

network card
monitor
sound card
numeric pad of keyboard
printer
cd dvd burner

### Post by cleentrax on 2006-05-20
Ubuntu is better than the competition in many ways. It's a great platform on which to use your favorite applications. Even the basics (OpenOffice, Firefox, Evolution) are quite usable. And my hardware is finally fully supported.

But the professional productivity apps are just not there yet. I can't make music in Ubuntu, because Rosegarden, Muse, etc., can't hold a candle to the workflow and features in SONAR. I can't design websites in Ubuntu, because NVU doesn't do scripting, and Bluefish/Quanta don't do WYSIWYG. I can't do imaging because GIMP doesn't do Pantone or CMYK, and the workflow gives me a migraine.

Now that Ubuntu is feature-rich, stable and polished, hopefully the apps will follow.

---

### Post by kamiro1987 on 2006-05-22
I introduced my sister to linux. Her computer had stopped working and she needed to finish a paper. I was working on a old clamshell ibook 333mhz. 192 mb of ram.

She took a break before writing her paper. She found and opened firefox. Her first comment was "why isn't flash working?" i tried to find a ppc flash plugin but i couldn't find one. The absence of flash didnt bother her much. The one thing that REALLY scared her was when she closed the computer and opened it again. as the computer woke up from hibernation there was a moment where some black text scrolled across the screen. She imedietly screamed  "ohmigod. ohmigod where did my paper go?!....oh" the thing that she didnt like was the moments where the pretty ubuntu desktop went away and she saw black scrolling text.

Linux has TOO MUCH choice. 

In Linux you have hundreds, maybe even more Distros to choose from, but when you go for Windows, you have just the one option XP, even XP pro and Media Centre are just XP with extra features. 

So with Windows your sure you made the right chioce, the only choice, but with Linux, you'll never be sure if you've got the best distro. Your average user doesn't want to install 5 or 10 distros to get an idea of which one is best, and even after that you can't be sure there isn't a better one out there.

There have been studies done, which show that people are happier with fewer choices for this reason. The one I know of is based on companies which offer their employees just 2 or 3 options for their pension fund savings VS companies which offer their employees 30 or 40 or more options. The employees of the companies with less choice are much happier because they're pretty sure they've chosen the best one for them, the ones with lots of choice aren't as happy because they think they may be in the wrong scheeme.

I think that for Linux to really take off, the community needs to choose 5 or so distros to back, and drop the rest. 

I know every distro claims to have it's place, but if we really looked hard enough we could see our way to dropping most of them. 
1) A server
2) A Konsole based one(not for the average joe)
3) SUPER user-friendly(no konsole access...linspire type)
4) A middle ground between 2 and 3.

The other advantage of this would be that the developers of the hundreds of distros at the moment would all end up working for 1 of 4 options and so development would go ahead even faster than it does now.

I realise that this is a radical idea, and will never be adopted, so many issues, so many loyalties to spesific distros, which one lives, which one stays. Not to mention the fact that the Open-Source world revolves around the idea of lots of choice, but I honestly believe that it is the solution, perhaps the only solution, if we realy want Linux to become the market leader.

---

People generally *like* variety, as long as the choices aren't confusing. If you don't know about that Zegenie Studios quiz, a list of distros at [DistroWatch](http://www.distrowatch.com) is going to be confusing.

### Post by h2gofast on 2006-05-31
laptop support.  In enterprise you typically have support on staff, unless the business is really small.  This handles a lot of the small issues that are show stoppers.  On the desktop, it's on the brink of being pretty good in all areas.
Laptops are more popular now and for many their laptop is their only computer.  I would install and configure linux on my mother's desktop and be confident she would be less lost than on windows.  But not on her laptop, or anyone else who wasn't comfortable on the command line.  

Anecdotal stories of how linux "just worked on my laptop" aren't indicative of the greater number of laptops where open source solutions aren't there yet.  Linux is the answer, just not completely yet.

### Post by jdbartlett on 2006-06-03
Ubuntu (5.10) was the first Linux distribution I used as my main operating system for more than a month. I used it as my main OS until I got my hands on a second-hand 12" PowerBook.

There are a lot of things right about Ubuntu. Driver support is one of them. Yes, there are problems, but more of my hardware worked "out the box" in Ubuntu than did in Windows XP. That kind of support is fantastic.

Where Ubuntu starts to scare every-day users is the desktop layout that comes with GNOME. I'm not saying GNOME is too complicated. Complicated is not the right word for the problem.

Before I go any further with this, I'd like to point out that of the three desktop environments for Linux I've tried, GNOME is my favorite. It's generally clean and simple, not too much eye candy or cluttering effects (my personal opinion of KDE), but not so sparse and clean as to feel Windows 95 (my personal opinion of Xfce).

Still, there are some things that bother me about GNOME, even in Ubuntu. This is a list, not of priorities, but top-of-mind issues I've had with GNOME and Linux:

1.) Being Told Not To Use GNOME

This one actually is a priority, and it's something we can all help with. Please, please, power users, let us do it in GNOME! I know it's so much easier/quicker for some to work in the command line, giving copy/paste instructions, but GNOME is there for a reason. Many users are intimidated by the command line.

Sometimes, there are problems that can only be fixed in the command line. Sometimes, these are problems so rare / specialized that there's no practical use for a GUI wrapper to the command line tool. Other times, users are just trying to access a wireless network using WPA encryption or even change monitor (when I changed from LCD to CRT I had to manually edit xorg.conf) GUI wrappers for this kind of work would be much appreciated.

2.) Button icons

In fact, overuse of icons in general. Why is this a problem? Before I answer that, I'd like to point something out: the words "OK" and "Cancel" are symbols by themselves. They are so short they are rarely read but immediately identified (most readers read all words as symbols anyway). No picture is needed, especially when that picture is too abstract (the icon for cancel is an arrow turning counterclockwise; as a rule, if an icon is too abstract, it shouldn't be an icon).

Maybe too many icons aren't any help, but why are they bad?

Clutter. Signal to noise. When some things are represented with pictures, it's helpful. When all things are represented with pictures, it's distracting. In OS X, perhaps the worlds most widely praised OS for good looks and user interface, the icons are very effective, partly because there are fewer of them. Applications, folders, and documents are all helpfully represented with unique icons, as with GNOME, but buttons are icon-free.

Practical screen space is another issue. Icons need padding, which means the buttons are bigger. Within reason of about 19px height min, bigger is only friendlier if you have usability concerns (for young ones and handicapped), which is better handled using the special accessibility settings. Other than that, bigger is clunkier and less graceful. "Pretty" is a big factor in adoption. Button icons distract the user, offer useless analogies, and make the desktop environment less attractive.

Having said that, there are some icons in GNOME that are helpful. Menu icons, for example. Some applications go too far with menu icons (again, if it's too abstract, it shouldn't be an icon) but here's a good example: icons in the GIMP's tools menu are helpful because they mirror what the user is already familiar with in the toolbar.

A lot of User Interface design is about learning to juggle, or balance, conflicting demands from users. They want to be helped, but not condescended or overwhelmed with clutter.

3.) 'System' as a main menu option

What, I'm crazy, right? We need that to administer the computer, to change the preferences, to live! Yes, but how often do (should!) we change those settings? How often do we need to nurse our computer, how often do we need to read "Welcome to the GNOME Desktop"? How often do we change our hardware?

Whenever it's not being used (at least 90% of the time), it's clutter. "Preferences" and "Administration" could easily be held in a Control Panel like interface of icons accessed from the places bar. Why?

The fact is, when an every-day user wants to use these options (which is rarely, at least on a stable system), the immediacy of their access isn't the issue. The issue is knowing to click on the right option and setting. This is something no desktop environment handles especially well, but GNOME handles especially badly by keeping everything accessible in a 'Start' menu-like popup menu - great for power users, lousy for everyone else.

Secondly, as far as most users are concerned, "preferences" and "administration" are two words with identical meanings. Few users are able to make the distinction. Keeping both "preferences" and "administration" in one "preferences" pane would be preferable.

So, what I would like to see is a nice big folder in the places bar which, when clicked on, shows big document-sized icons for both preferences and administration tasks (as far as most users are concerned - these two issues are barely separated), possibly grouped by type of setting. Hovering over an icon immediately brings a helpful popups ("Networking configures network devices and connections, such as modems, wireless cards, and computer networks") in a bubble, not a tooltip.

Why so big and shiny, didn't I say not to patronize the user? Yes, but I also said not to overwhelm the user. Reasonably bigger icons in a more permanent (non-popup) display with a 'back' button helps the user realize that if he/she clicks the wrong option, he can always click 'back' and select another.

4.) Updates

Too much information is given when updates are found. These are the things a user needs to know when installing updates:

- There are updates available, do you want to install them now? [This may require a restart.]

- Updates installed successfully. Your system is up to date! [Click 'restart'.]

What the user does not need to know unless the information is asked for:

- The number of updates.

- The names of the updates and their version numbers.

- Filenames and locations.

- That they can choose not to install an update (by unchecking checkboxes).

My proposal is that the update popup contain text like "Updates were found! Click this popup to bring your system up to date." At the bottom, though, is a blue hyperlink for power users: "Inspect updates."

And how about an arrow that hides the updates list in the popup, like with the terminal output for synaptic package manager?

This isn't about "dumbing down" for users, it's about presenting the user with what he/she really needs to know: there are updates, updates are a good thing, install them when you're ready. For the average user, anything else is clutter.

5.) Show the user how to keep his home

"Home" is where the user is, but where is the housekeeping? Windows and OS X generally provide users with a basic folder structure to store "types" of files. "Music", "Movies", "Documents". This promotes good housekeeping and acts as a prompt for users (in Linux, I've seen people start moving OUTSIDE their "home" directory to go elsewhere into the filesystem, looking for a place to save their document!)

This structure doesn't have to be enforced, but the mere availability of such subfolders in the user's "home" is often enough. OS X also puts these folders in the "places" bar on the left hand side of the file explorer for quick access.

6.) 'Places' as a main menu option

Instead, just an application launcher for the default file explorer would be fine. This isn't as much of a problem as the 'System' pane, but I don't think it's necessary, either. Again, it promotes the user moving outside of his or her home folder, which shouldn't be desired. When launched, the file explorer should go to the user's home directory by default and shouldn't go 'up' any further (by clicking the 'up' button) unless the user clicks on 'computer' to access the filesystem. Sometimes, users have slow hard disks and accidentally click 'up' one time too many.

7.) 'Applications' ONLY accessible as a main menu option

It'd be neat if the 'applications' menu were mirrored in the 'places' bar to the left in the file explorer so that applications could be explored without navigating a popup menu. I like the 'Start' menu-like feel of the Applications popup, but it's not good for exploration.

..

All the above issues, aside, I still prefer GNOME to the other Linux desktop environments I've tried. I think more could be done to "pull in" users, though, like the things I've mentioned.

---

### Post by BoyOfDestiny on 2006-06-03
> **jdbartlett]Ubuntu (5.10) was the first Linux distribution I used as my main operating system for more than a month. I used it as my main OS until I got my hands on a second-hand 12" PowerBook.

- The number of updates.

- The names of the updates and their version numbers.

- Filenames and locations.

5.) Show the user how to keep his home

It'd be neat if the 'applications' menu were mirrored in the 'places' bar to the left in the file explorer so that applications could be explored without navigating a popup menu. I like the 'Start' menu-like feel of the Applications popup, but it's not good for exploration.

1. More GUI's are good. As a power user, as long as the terminal is there, it's; all good.

2. Not sure I see the problem... Use a different theme?

3. Preferences & Administration are confusing, I usually have to check both when I'm looking for something.

4. The updates are very straight forward, just click apply. The user has a right to know what is installed. If they see a familiar name like gaim or firefox, it's nice to know a new version is in place. Also important letting the user know if they need to restart the application or the system for the changes to take effect. If they don't need the info, all they have to do is click apply, and that's it. Nothing scary about it. Not to mention some apps don't give shortcuts... So at this point in time it's still nice to check where it's stored if it needs to be run. Don't make a savvy user search or have to use synaptic just to check...

5-6. I think places is great as is. If the user doesn't want access to the rest of the filesystem, that is what the home button is for. Regardless, since they aren't running as root, they can't really do anything by accident. Their first response should be, wtf is this stuff (click home) and back where they should be. Places also gives easy to access to the built in cd/dvd burner, and various devices and folders (if bookmarked)

7. I don't understand. Why would you do this? The apps are just binaries, with most of the settings either in /etc or in /home (as hidden folders)... There is no benefit to that... Unless you mean you want a menu with every app installed... This would be the "debian" menu I guess?

---

### Post by jdbartlett on 2006-06-03
[QUOTE=BoyOfDestiny]1. More GUI's are good. As a power user, as long as the terminal is there, it's; all good.

2. Not sure I see the problem... Use a different theme?

3. Preferences & Administration are confusing, I usually have to check both when I'm looking for something.

4. The updates are very straight forward, just click apply. The user has a right to know what is installed. If they see a familiar name like gaim or firefox, it's nice to know a new version is in place. Also important letting the user know if they need to restart the application or the system for the changes to take effect. If they don't need the info, all they have to do is click apply, and that's it. Nothing scary about it. Not to mention some apps don't give shortcuts... So at this point in time it's still nice to check where it's stored if it needs to be run. Don't make a savvy user search or have to use synaptic just to check...

5-6. I think places is great as is. If the user doesn't want access to the rest of the filesystem, that is what the home button is for. Regardless, since they aren't running as root, they can't really do anything by accident. Their first response should be, wtf is this stuff (click home) and back where they should be. Places also gives easy to access to the built in cd/dvd burner, and various devices and folders (if bookmarked)

7. I don't understand. Why would you do this? The apps are just binaries, with most of the settings either in /etc or in /home (as hidden folders)... There is no benefit to that... Unless you mean you want a menu with every app installed... This would be the "debian" menu I guess?[/QUOTE]Thanks for your reply, Boy of Destiny. A few (actually, technically, since this is Linux, all!) of the things I mentioned could be changed through theme or other customization, but the average mainstream user a) may not realize these things are problems (believe it or not, a lot of user interface happens in the subconscious - people use their intuition whether or not the interface is 'intuitive'), b) may not know how to change the theme, c) shouldn't have to reach for 'themes and customizations' immediately after installing an OS. Any user interface enhancer that doesn't enhance the user interface is clutter. Clutter is bad, especially when it's distracting.

4. I agree, the user has the right to know what is being installed, but most users don't want to know. Try to put yourself in the mind of the average user. You're using a computer for a specific purpose, probably work. You, the average user, have been warned to read every popup carefully before clicking ok. The updates list gives a lot of information, which is good for power user Bob, but frustrates average user Joe because he's spending 5 minutes reading a popup that's distracting him from his work.

Also we aren't removing the user's ability to see what is being installed by hiding that info until they ask to see it (and the power users we're talking about will automatically click the fold-out or 'inspect' options to see what's going on), we're talking about introducing Linux to the mainstream every day users who just want to get the job done. All that information, to them, is clutter that's slowing them down. At worst, they may uncheck some of the updates because they don't recognize them.

5-6. We have to be careful when we hear ourselves using the word "should". "Should" means we are dictating instead of assuming the user's response. After all, people should not download illegal music, but they do. People should think, "Hmm, I don't recognize this, I'd better click that button marked 'home'", but the action I've observed them taking is, "Hmm, I don't recognize this, I'll hunt around some more... nah, I'll just save it here. If it's on the hard disk, it's fine." End result? A few days later, a phone call: "I've lost a document I saved on the hard disk..."

7. I guess I was aiming for a relationship more like the Applications folder and the dock in OS X, but come to think of it, the A La Carte menu editor pretty much solves that problem. The benefit of having application shortcuts in a places folder was mostly for exploration of a new system (remembering which app user has opened last because it's still selected in the folder, and having something more 'solid' on screen than a popup.)

### Post by Tiikeri on 2006-06-06
Hi, 

I'm almost a linux newbie. This is my third linux-installation. All started with SuSe 9.3 when I needed http server, now I'm using Kubutu 6.06 as a desktop PC. 

As a Windows user, I see some advantages that are not fully made us of. SuSe had some nice features with Yast2, that made apache+sql installation way easier than in Windows XP. (yes, tried that). Kubuntu has Adept, which is more like a tool for SysAdmin. It does'n welcome you to browse all the possibilities that linux may offer. 
I have struggled with css-protected dvd-playback. Last week I didn't even know there was such a thing. Adept wasn't much of a help, I installed almost everything with "dvd" labeled. Still, norman dvd:s played fine, but protected didn't.

Finally I heard from a friend of mine about easyubuntu-package that was easy to use and installed some more packages and *bling* things worked out.

Solving simple problems revolve too much around google and forums, when there are quite simple answers sometimes. 

Today I installed my good old Creative Webcam 5. With my unlimited trust to Dapper, I plugged the thig and tried it in Kopete. It was correctly identified, but image was all messed up. 
I lauched Adept and typed webcam. Not really helpfull. After ½h googling, I found out my webcam used pwc-chip, which is no longer supported :(
Forums were instructing about kernelpatches and modules, and I don't even know my kernel number. Shame on me! 

Still, deciding to give it a go, I went to root Konsole and apt-cache searched pwc. It said to be "source code for the pwc kernel module". Whattahell.. lets give it a try. 
# apt-get install pwc-source
Lines start running, and while asking somethig, it suggested to also install 
"build-essential dialog". And so I did. Didn't even bother to see what it is.
After a while it stopped, there were no error messages. 
I had no idea what i just had done, exept installed some source code. 
Ok ok, lets give it a try.

Kopete showed an bw image!?! Hooray. Some tuning with sliders -> OK and back to setting. There were colors! 
Almost an hour of waste for a simple thing like that.

The moral of the story: 
1) The Software-install interface (like adept, yast2) should have enough information about packages and their possibility.
2) Also some information about install/startup instructions if package doesn't automatically give you any Icon to K Menu would be nice.
3) Despite poor descriptions, adept is usable for a engineer student like me, but I bet my mother can't use it.

PS. My kubuntu operates now with full capasity: mp3, dvd's, divx with subtitles, webcam, irc-client, Ksensors, firewall, everything. I <3 Kubuntu ;)

---

### Post by SuperMike on 2006-06-06
* I think there's trouble with enabling proprietary video formats so you can view DVDs and web-based videos or listen to MP3s. Grandma isn't going to know how to install this extra stuff.

* Some websites still require Internet Exploder, although I'm extremely happy to see some websites say stuff like, "This website works best in Firefox." Right back at ya, Microsoft!

* Needs a really well-established Quickbooks (for Business) knockoff. Grisbi is a pretty close option. The problem is that many baby boomers are well-to-do and have businesses. They are frustrated with the malware on Windows and you can often convince them to try something new like Ubuntu. However, they may be disappointed to find nothing very close to what they're used to with Quickbooks.

* Something needs to be done about the Exchange Server adoption in the workplace. People want 100% compatibility with Exchange from Linux, and that isn't going to happen anytime soon. Therefore, the only other option is to convince CEOs that Exchange Server sucks and something from Linux is better.

* Ubuntu needs a better way to enable the universe, multiverse, and backports option without having Grandma edit /etc/apt/sources.list. She needs to do this to grab that one app that she cannot get any other way. Then, she needs to be safely returned to the world where this option is turned off so that she doesn't get upgraded into the universe, multiverse, backports warped space and can stay mainstream with regular updates to her PC.

* Ubuntu needs a better way to download new fonts and install them, even if it's only OpenType or TrueType stuff, not the X fonts or Type 1 fonts. Grandma isn't going to know how to scan her hard drive for the TTF files and drop her stuff there. She'll want a new font and she'll want to find it on the web, download it, and install it easily.

* There needs to be a consistent GUI installer as well as a console-based installer so that Grandma can install stuff easily and so that developers can build apps without thinking about how hard the installer has to be. This also ties into a common Linux base PC that other distros pull from. And if Grandma installs Xubuntu, but installs Abiword (a GNOME app), it needs to automatically get an icon on her Xubuntu menu so that she doesn't have to install it there herself. Also, developers would love to know that they could test their stuff to the standard Linux base and then build their install dependencies around that, thereby increasing the odds that their app will install on many different distros.

* Grandma and Grandpa run Windows because that's what was pre-installed when they bought their eMachines at Wal*Mart. That's about how it is for many people. Linux needs to be so HOT that Grandma and Grandpa hear about it in TIME magazine and actually have a CD in it where they can test it out and see if they want to switch to something more secure and with a low risk of having malware troubles.

* Grandma can't build an iptables firewall. Some firewalls have GUI frontends, but none that I have used don't come with their own set of issues. That's why I have reverted to iptables. However, it's taken me awhile to learn iptables. Grandma isn't going to edit a text file to make a firewall.

* Grandma will not like to know she has to edit her xorg.conf file because her video didn't work after installing Ubuntu. Instead, the installer needs to detect a problem and give her a nice GUI for her to try some configurations out.

---

### Post by BoyOfDestiny on 2006-06-06
[QUOTE=jdbartlett]Thanks for your reply, Boy of Destiny. A few (actually, technically, since this is Linux, all!) of the things I mentioned could be changed through theme or other customization, but the average mainstream user a) may not realize these things are problems (believe it or not, a lot of user interface happens in the subconscious - people use their intuition whether or not the interface is 'intuitive'), b) may not know how to change the theme, c) shouldn't have to reach for 'themes and customizations' immediately after installing an OS. Any user interface enhancer that doesn't enhance the user interface is clutter. Clutter is bad, especially when it's distracting.

4. I agree, the user has the right to know what is being installed, but most users don't want to know. Try to put yourself in the mind of the average user. You're using a computer for a specific purpose, probably work. You, the average user, have been warned to read every popup carefully before clicking ok. The updates list gives a lot of information, which is good for power user Bob, but frustrates average user Joe because he's spending 5 minutes reading a popup that's distracting him from his work.

Also we aren't removing the user's ability to see what is being installed by hiding that info until they ask to see it (and the power users we're talking about will automatically click the fold-out or 'inspect' options to see what's going on), we're talking about introducing Linux to the mainstream every day users who just want to get the job done. All that information, to them, is clutter that's slowing them down. At worst, they may uncheck some of the updates because they don't recognize them.

5-6. We have to be careful when we hear ourselves using the word "should". "Should" means we are dictating instead of assuming the user's response. After all, people should not download illegal music, but they do. People should think, "Hmm, I don't recognize this, I'd better click that button marked 'home'", but the action I've observed them taking is, "Hmm, I don't recognize this, I'll hunt around some more... nah, I'll just save it here. If it's on the hard disk, it's fine." End result? A few days later, a phone call: "I've lost a document I saved on the hard disk..."

7. I guess I was aiming for a relationship more like the Applications folder and the dock in OS X, but come to think of it, the A La Carte menu editor pretty much solves that problem. The benefit of having application shortcuts in a places folder was mostly for exploration of a new system (remembering which app user has opened last because it's still selected in the folder, and having something more 'solid' on screen than a popup.)[/QUOTE]

You're welcome. Thanks for your polite reply.

4. I wouldn't mind a button/expand thing to give extra details. As long as the option isn't buried away and takes several clicks to get to no problem.

5-6. The thing is, correct me if I'm wrong, a regular user cannot write files anywhere but their home folder and /tmp. They would need to launch natilus as root to really "lose" a file... Hopefully 'recent documents' or the built in search (even search by date, if they forgot the name) would help in the case of lost files... 

7. I see. I've never used OS X so I wasn't sure what you had meant.

### Post by forrestcupp on 2006-06-09
The main thing keeping Linux from being widespread is a total lack of standardization.  There are way too many disributions out there.  Programmers can't make easily installable software just for Linux, they then have to make packages for about 50 or 100 different Linuxes.  That's why you can't really get a disk of software that will work on any Linux box.  If there were some effort in standardizing software installation, that would make a huge difference.  Or maybe someday Ubuntu will be the only distro left and we won't have to worry about it.

### Post by Stormy Eyes on 2006-06-09
[QUOTE=aysiu]You can install twenty different programs in Windows with a single command and no reboot?[/QUOTE]

No, that guy's griping about how he can't just download some random package off the net, double-click it, and install it. (which is why the bible was written in latin and not the locale dialect, it keeps sacred knowledge from those who might de-centralise power.)

NSA love that plebs use windows, coz since its closed source it is much easier to stamp out those who accidentally find these back doors.

> Originally Posted by **Sheinar**
                 * If an application screws up in Windows, they blame the application. If an application screws up in Linux, they blame Linux.*
 
its because of the nature of consumption.

those who'd wish most of us would turn into compliant bleeting cosuming bipeds, are constantly trying to funnel our culture into this kind of philosphy : 

when we pay for something, we think that we are buying a garuantee. So when the thing we buy stuffs up, we think that the purchase gives us the right to scream foulplay.

What i have come to realise is that when you purchase something, you are only recompensating the seller for : raw-matierals, time, money. not buying a garuantee of perfection.

notice how many goods offer garuantee for an extra price?

This brings me to the attitude I had with windows.

I was a angry windows user. WAS. I surmise this was becuase of my mis-conception of the nature of a fiscal transaction.

Using ubuntu has made me realise that i was wrong. hence the lack of outrage and flying little bits of plastic mouse cruising at breakneck speeds around my room.

My attitude with windows was like (and kinda still is): "Dammit I paid *good money* for this software. why wont it work. Bloody WORRK!!!!. SLAMM there goes *another keyboard* due to my enraged fist".

But now that I am with ubuntu, I realise that it is free and ergo i am more patient. But  conversly since it does perform just as fast as windows and much more reliably My patience is doubly rewarded. 

By the way, my blood pressure still goes up when i go near a windows machine, and my hand twitches towards my ubuntu live cd. lol

basically to cap it up, since they did not hand over money, they believe that they are not getting qaulity. they have become conditioned by a cynical money culture that dictates rewards only come from sacrifices. but not sacrifices from the elite or your role models, but rather "you the individual" are to blame and that "you the individual" are to be reponsible for paying for the setbacks caused by corporate culture(pollution, theft, drugs, and abuse).

### Post by forrestcupp on 2006-06-10
The problem with linux isn't that it can't do quality games.  Look at America's Army, that is pretty quality, at least it was when it came out.  The problem is that no one is making linux versions of their games.  For a couple of reasons, you can't blame them:  1. too many linux people whining about how they shouldn't have to pay for anything (I love free software and open source, but you have to pay for some things in life)   2. there is no standard of installation for every linux box, so why do they want to invest their time and money into porting to 500 different linuxes.   3. A lot of software companies don't think there is a big enough user base to invest their resources into.

So for the time being we have to either settle for open source games that sometimes are created by people who don't have the resources to make cutting edge games, or shell out another monthly fee to Cedega so that we can play the Windows games that we payed for, as long as we keep paying for Cedega every month.  Or maybe we can be lucky enough to find a bin file to play cd game data for certain games like Metal of Honor.

### Post by nekr0z on 2006-06-10
Well, I'd say Linux is simly too complex for Joe Average to use. It is moving to be more easy-to use with every new release of every distro I know, but now it's too complex. Not too hard, just too complex. And I really hope Linux continues being so.

The main thing that differs Linux from Windows is that in Windows you don't have to know how the system works. You even cannot learn it without getting very deep into details, because Microsoft hides it from the user. In Linux you alway have the door open, always inviting you to get deeper, to know how the things are done and to learn how to use them for your advantage. Windows hides the fact computer is a very complex and flexible machine, while Linux almost shouts about it! That's what I like about linux, and that's what average users dislike.

Average user does not need a computer to be complex and flexible, he/she only needs it to do the job. Joe Average doesn't even want to know about filesystems, he knows that files are stored in folders on a disk, that's enough. He doesn't want to know how to configure X to work with 2 monitors, he doesn't even want to know what the hell X is. All he wants is plug in a second monitor and work. He doesn't want to know that MP3 is a restricted format, he doesn't even want to know what the hell is a format and what differs MP3 from OGG. What he knows is this file with a note on the icon is some music, and computer should play it. That's all...

The average user doesn't want to use a computer as a system, he uses it as another piece of home technics, like a microwave oven, or a TV-set, or a fridge...

And Linux is now trying to work exactly as Joe Average wants. But it doesn't yet succeed too much. And this is good, besause... Well, I don't remember who said this, but it was said very good: "Make a tool that even an idiot could use, and he will be the only one who would want to use it". This is where Windows finally came, and this is where I don't want Linux to come.

---

### Post by ericesque on 2006-06-13
In order:

Driver support
Copyright infringement
Publicity
Microsoft business model
other

Ubuntu doesn't just work.  Not on brand new hardware.  3rd party companies HAVE to release windows drivers with their product.  Linux is typically an afterthought.  As long as people can't get their hardware working, they will shy away from Ubuntu.

Again, Ubuntu doesn't just work... because I have to install codecs to make mp3s work.  People expect to be able to play their music-- and not necessarily from cd.

Publicity comes in 3rd, because even if everyone knew about Ubuntu, they'd say to hell with it: it doesn't support my hardware or my media.

Microsoft is about on par with publicity.  The monopolistic business model that microsoft perpetrates leaves most people with no reason to ask if there's an alternative.  

other: Ubuntu-- and linux distros in general-- assume that the end user is willing to work at getting their computer to do what they expect it to do.  Maybe this should be first.  Maybe it's last because it is somewhat all encompassing.  Mac has it right at the moment.  People want simplicity.  They aren't interested in learning.  They just want it to do what they want it to do and they want it to do it now.  I want some instant pudding.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-13
[QUOTE=ericesque]Mac has it right at the moment.  People want simplicity.  They aren't interested in learning.  They just want it to do what they want it to do and they want it to do it now.  I want some instant pudding.[/QUOTE] Maybe Mac has it right because it marries the hardware and software--you don't have to install it on a blank box and hope it works.

That's why more people should support businesses like [System 76](http://www.system76.com). If people don't like to install and configure Linux operating systems, they should buy their computers with Linux operating systems preinstalled (as they already do with Mac and Windows).

If video, USB, audio, bluetooth, CD-ROM, etc. all work out-of-the-box, getting MP3s working is a trivial task. In fact, the more popular Ubuntu becomes, the more I see distros like Mepis (that have no qualms about including proprietary codecs in the default installation) picking up the slack.

After all, the upcoming version of Mepis will be based on Ubuntu.

### Post by _Samson_ on 2006-06-13
Being pretty new to Ubuntu and Linux in general, here's my personal list -

1. Drivers - Whilst most stuff works straight out of the tin, I gave up trying to install a Belkin wireless PCMCIA card in Breezy 5.10. Last straw was when I found a page that suggested compiling the latest version of ndiswrapper. Ermm, no thanks. About to install 6.06, so hopefully it'll work in that.

2. Terminal - Due to #1 I spent quite a lot of time in the terminal trying to get the belkin/realtec drivers to work and got pretty confused at first. Not enough GUI and to much typing text can put some users off, especially if they aren't tech minded.

3. To many flavours - Kubuntu, edubuntu, dapper, breezy, that's just this flavour of Linux, there's hundreds more. Even I found it really confusing as to what I needed at first, made worse by searching the forums here, and I kinda knew what I wanted!

4. Games and Application support - Been mentioned several times, really don't see how it's going to change in the short term. Long term it needs more machines coming with Linux pre-installed, but then you have the above problem - what flavour?!?!? If I can get any game that comes out for Windows running in Ubunut, my windows box is gone. Nobody mention consoles to me, hate the damn things, most of the games I play are online and consoles still aren't there yet.

I think overall people see Linux as being more complicated than it actually is, as a realm for geeks who spend all their time in the digital world. That's perpetuated by the huge variety of Linux distros. 

For the average Joe, Windows is easy, it's either 98, ME, 2000 or XP. 

Most would take 4 choices over 400.

### Post by MonkeyBoy on 2006-06-13
Naglfari is right about the publicity.  You never see Linux advertised really because of the major differences between commercial and open source development.  M$ charge a lot and can then spend a lot of their profits on making sure we know about windows.  

The windows tax is heavily responsible too.  I have never seen a PC in a shop without windows being sold ready installed.  If PC buyers were told when buying a PC that they had a choice of XP (£70-£100 more. No office software included.), Ubuntu (No extra charge, but more complicated to buy compatible new parts.  Has openoffice.), and maybe a few other choices, I think they would seriously consider using Ubuntu.  Also, if this were to happen, the manafacturers could add the correct drivers to the other distros the way they do with XP. Lets face it that a lot of casual home users are not confident enough to install software or new hardware anyway.  

Of course this would reduce the profit that the shops get from selling windows and mean that the staff of places like PC World would need proper training.:p 

It's not gonna happen for the forseeable future...

---

### Post by Mr Wrath on 2006-06-13
In my opinion, the thing that is holding Linux back is not 'Linux' itself, but the people in general.  People/humans are creatures of habit.  They get their first family computer (which is usually Windows) and as they grow up they just continue to depend, and suffer, with Windows because it is "user" friendly, and well, the only thing they grew up with.  There are not many people willing to learn something new that wasn't taught in high school or college...but, time changes everything.  The only things we as 'Linux' users can continue to do is contribute, spread the word, and teach others.

I had only heard about Linux a few years ago, and that was only because of a friend using LINDOWS (which was a joke).  My actual using Linux (Debian Sarge) didn't start happening until I was forced because my company's servers being:
(1) Debian Sarge -File Server
(2) Ubuntu Breezy -Archive Server
(3) Debian Sarge -Music Server

I became the IT Manager and had to learn very quickly how to work with 'Linux'.  It was somewhat difficult at first, only because of using Windows for 13 years, but needless to say...I broke the habit, and for the better.  'Linux' in my opinion is far better than Micro$oft.

### Post by Ubunted on 2006-06-13
1: Lack of major OEMs offering preinstalled systems to everyday customers, as in on store shelves and options right next to Windows in the customize section, not buried in twenty webpages.

2: Drivers, drivers, drivers.

Many proprietary codecs (.rm, .mov) don't even play in Windows without installing RealPlayer and Quicktime. Same goes for Java and Flash, so I don't really see this as being a weak point, especially since with the right repositories preconfigured, installing them is just a few keystrokes away.

I work ISP support all day - as long as you tell people what to type, they're not afraid of a command line.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-21
[QUOTE=clparker]In Linux, few things are point and click easy[/QUOTE] Really? These are the things I can do point-and-click easy:

Launch any graphical program
Install from the repositories.
Enable extra repositories.
Install .deb files.
Organize, rename, delete files.
Log out, log in.
Create new users.
Change desktop environments.
Change themes.
VNC
VMWare
Automount external media
Play music
Edit photos

These are things Ubuntu needs terminal for but other distros can do point-and-click:
Configure X
Mount partitions with proper permissions

These are things I have to use the terminal for:
I can't think of any

### Post by usfour on 2006-06-21
I think what is keeping Linux from getting a larger market share is not the lack of hardware driver (this takes time to develop, we're seeing much quicker availability then any other time in the history of Linux), or MS dominance, or copyright infringements, etc...  It's the lack of respect for the general user who hasn't gone to technical school and understand all the tech stuff.  Before the Ubuntu distro, the other distros were catered to the tech fanatics.  Not by design but the product just wasn't ready.  Only the hardcore techs really understood what it would take to get things to work.  Who builds the opensource products like OpenOffice or Evolution or Gimp?  The techs who want to offer a choice.  But the choice was not to be limited to the hardcore techie.  It was meant to an alternative.  Perhaps a better alternative.  I understand the intent of this thread was to find out what we can do better to get more adoption.  Calling Windows user 'idiots' is not going to gain increased adoption. Understanding what Windows has done right will increase adoption.  If you don't agree with some of their philosophies or methods, don't do them.  Do what is right.  Do we not want more adoption?

I've been a Redhat user since Rel 7.2.  If you weren't a technie with an unlimited amount of patience, you couldn't use it.  I'm really excited with Ubuntu because it's really ready for prime time.  It's a beautiful desktop with a suite of terrific products.  Not to mention the LTS of Dapper Drake.  We finally have a winner that we can bring to my 68 year old Grandma so we can exchange email.  This is called evolution and product maturity.  Don't fight it.  It's here to stay.

---

### Post by usfour on 2006-06-21
[QUOTE=aysiu]Really? Mepis and Linspire don't really fit into this revisionist distro history of yours. Neither does Xandros, SuSE, or Mandriva. Frankly, I don't know what you're talking about.

Of all the "user-friendly" distros out there, Ubuntu is the one that's still most reliant on the command-line.[/QUOTE]

This is quite interesting.  I don't understand the anxiety my post has caused.  How stable were these distros you identified above.  I fully agree that they're all great distros.  I've tried Mandrake and Suse (very Window'ish) or even Lindows.  Who are we trying to kid?  Perhaps it's my lack of experience with these other distros.  Who has the time to try every distro out there.  With each update, something breaks.  There is no argument that the server products are second to none.  Apache is top notch. I love Samba.  The list goes on.  Can you actually use Openoffice before 2.0 if you need to exchange files with a windows user?  Your resume will look really great with unpredictable formatting.  Have you seen all the clutter that comes with a standard Redhat desktop install.  Tell the average home user that they're going to have to upgrade every six months because there is another release coming and no one really knows what will break in the upgrade.  I actually had Fedora 5 installed on this box.  Did an update and it told me that I'm missing several packages for Evolution.  I've added no other package other then what was on the standard install.  What's with that?  

I've stuck by Redhat without straying until now.  I've checked out the other distros from time to time, but I've always gone back.  I run a Centos 4 desktop and it is rock solid.  Now, I'm really excited and encouraged by Ubuntu because this seems to be quite a solid distro.  

I was just at Futureshop looking at some cameras.  This well dressed educated looking guy was asking the sales lady what a megapixel is and how many photos will fit in a standard memory that comes with the camera.  Give him Linux, do you think he has heard of it or will want to try it?

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-21
> How stable were these distros you identified above.  Those distros were just as stable as Ubuntu, if not moreso.

I was addressing your false assertion that, before Ubuntu, other distros catered to tech fanatics.

Please explain to me how Mepis caters to tech fanatics and Ubuntu doesn't. Provide specific examples please. Same for Linspire.

> This is quite interesting. I don't understand the anxiety my post has caused. Your post hasn't caused anxiety any more than mine has. I'm simply not understanding this idea you have that Ubuntu is any more non-tech-fanatic-friendly than other distros.

Let's see:

**Mount a Windows or Linux partition...**
Mepis: click on it
Ubuntu: edit the /etc/fstab or paste some terminal commands in

**Restore Grub to the MBR...**
Mepis: Click on OS Center. Click on "restore Grub." Follow prompts.
Ubuntu: Type obscure commands into a terminal

**Play proprietary formats...**
Mepis: Just play them
Ubuntu: Install them yourself or use some third-party tool like Automatix to install them for you

**Graphical installer on a live CD...**
Mepis: Around for at least two years if not longer
Ubuntu: Just came out three weeks ago

**Double-click a .deb to install it...**
Mepis: Around since last year at least
Ubuntu: Just came out three weeks ago

I mentioned this in another post but just to reiterate.  I tried Suse and Mandrake, they're both very nice.  However, there were just too many clicks to get to a feature.  Again, I'm lazy, so I bailed and headed back to Redhat.

Interestingly, when we say "preventing Linux to widespread", do we mean within the Linux user world or those outside.  Because, I can't imagine general users with one PC at home to want to configure the X server, mount a partition or adjust the default Grub.

I'm committed to CentOS (after playing with previous Redhat pre-Enterprise releases) because of their ability to provide stable updates (Redhat rebuild).  I'm now enthused by Ubuntu's Dapper Drake because of their extensive testing and LTS.  Not to mention their simplicity.  Most distros are bloated with stuff to impress.  I just want the basics to get me started and then I will add as I need it.  The Linux desktop is supposed to provide me with service.  Not for me to provide service for it.  All the time.

And to respect the other mainstream distros, I'm sure they're all equally capable and robust.  I've just not had the experience to attest to it.

### Post by emperor on 2006-06-23

BTW, the ATI remote works great with MythTV!

### Post by bonzodog on 2006-06-23

At least I had the monitors native resolution from starting X. 
(windows was absolutely convinced my system could not do more than 1024 x 768- until I installed the monitor driver.) 
Nah, I came back to linux after finding a new install of windows just too hard, and I won't troubleshoot others windows systems, as it's too complicated, and there are far too many things that can go wrong in windows, with no clue where to start looking. At least with linux, the root tree design means that in the event of a problem, you know *exactly* where to start.

======================================
Selling price: 1000 cookies
where 800 for the computer manufacturer and 200 cookies for the happy Bill... 
======================================

This is the first paragraph of the Windows XP EULA certificate that can be found in all the PC with Windows installed (file: EULA.txt): 

> 
Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition 

END-USER LICENSE AGREEMENT 

IMPORTANT-READ CAREFULLY: This End-User
License Agreement ("EULA") is a legal agreement between you
(either an individual or a single entity) and Microsoft
Corporation for the Microsoft software product identified above,
which includes computer software and may include associated
media, printed materials, "online" or electronic documentation,
and Internet-based services ("Product").    An amendment or
addendum to this EULA may accompany the Product.  YOU AGREE TO BE
BOUND BY THE TERMS OF THIS EULA BY
INSTALLING, COPYING, OR OTHERWISE USING THE
PRODUCT. IF YOU DO NOT AGREE, DO NOT INSTALL
OR USE THE PRODUCT; YOU MAY RETURN IT TO YOUR
PLACE OF PURCHASE FOR A FULL REFUND.

>

### Post by Coomkeen on 2006-07-02
Yes, a unified deb. But also advertising. And ease of use, particularly installing apps and drivers.

MapTech: "We have no plans ...etc" (Marine charting and navigation software)
Both of these I need to run, so I'm forced into using MSWindows.
There are no Linux equivalents, and believe me, I've searched!
Olympus P11 printer - I just found a driver, after quite a search, for Linux.
But NO instructions as to what to do with the file after I'd downloaded it, and I still can't get the printer to run.
With MSWindows it was dead easy.
This is all really a great shame.
I might add... I hate MSWindows.
Geting too close to the problem, I mean using Linux in 'technical' mode, can mean we, as a group of people, don't see it from the normal consumers point of view.
All they want is something they can use - simply.
Ron

### Post by the_blue_pill on 2006-07-02
Drivers, drivers, drivers. If we had Dells and HPs preinstalled with linux, with a nice customized distro CD, people would have little to no incentive to prefer XP or OSX. 

The way things are now is a loop:

Users don't want linux because they are (in my opinion rightly) intimidated by setup/configuration/compatibility issues.

Linux developers cannot improve the PnP efficiency of their distros because they are not assisted by the actual hardware producers. They always lag behind, and features well established in win boxes like direct rendering, wireless and acpi configuration become an unpredictable mess in linux.

Big vendors like Dell or HP aren't interested in making linux-friendly  machines or at least helping the oss developers because there aren't many people demanding it. Geeks build their own boxes, and average computer literates simply don't know/ don't want linux.

And so it goes. I don't anticipate people spontaneously flocking in droves to linux anytime soon, or the oss community to become do better than what they're already doing, so I'm hoping the cycle will be broken at some point by a vendor willing to risk throwing its weight behind a linux distro, marketing home user laptops/desktops with linux preloaded.

### Post by kline on 2006-07-02
[FONT="Times New Roman"][SIZE="3"]Publicity is a large part of it.  I use FreeBSD on my DNS server, and virtually zero non-geeks have ever heard of Berkeley Unix.  More have heard about Linux, but assume that it's "too hard" or that it's harder than M$.  But it keeps getting incrasingly stable, and much easier to use.  But the main reason the *open* computer systems aren't growing faster is that nobody really understands all the facts.

Sheer, brute economics means that HP andtheir affliates are not going to the bother (major $$$,$$$) to port stuff for their All-in-one printers that color-print, copy, scan and OCR [and do your dishes and clean your floor].  

The reality of the MIT $100 laptop with Redhat for the hundreds of millions of poor kids may be  one thing that starts to tip things.[/SIZE][/FONT]

### Post by Dr. C on 2006-07-03
Software piracy particularly of Microsoft software. What many forget is that the rate of software piracy ranges from about 30% (in Canada and the United States) to over 90% in many parts of the world. This “free” pirated version of Windows is the single biggest competitor to Linux and Open Source. After all what is the advantage of a free / libre software license over a restrictive commercial software license if the user, a pirate, has no intention of following either license? 

There are bright prospects here in the future. Microsoft is doing something about piracy with product activation and WGA (Windows Genuine Advantage) and if they find a way to control those rouge corporate keys (promised for Vista)  it will have an impact on piracy. If only a small percentage of the pirates of windows see the light and move to Linux it will be a huge boost to Linux on the desktop. There is a real advantage in Microsoft's WGA and similar programs for Linux and open source. 

At least on Open Source project has seen the negative impact that the piracy of commercial software has on FLOSS 

[http://why.openoffice.org/getlegal.html]("http://why.openoffice.org/getlegal.html")
