### Post by Cephus on 2006-07-04
[QUOTE=earobinson]Where is the option to say that windows is just better? Or to say linux just needs time, or that linux is not designed to be used by the advrage user.

i know that these are all populare reasons for why linux is not wide spread[/QUOTE]

I consider myself a pretty advanced computer user, though I have primarily used Windows and DOS throughout my computing career.  I did give Slackware a try back in the mid 90's but that had been the extent of my Linux experience.

I took the plunge and installed Ubuntu 6.06 on my Sony laptop.  For the life of me, I could not get my wireless card properly configured, though Ubuntu recognized it, showed the SSIDs in my neighborhood, etc.  I simply COULD NOT get a DHCP address.  Googling around, I see other people with the same problem that reverted back to Hoary.  I Installed Ubuntu Hoary, and immediately my wireless came to life.  So much for the latest and greatest.

Hoary came with an older version of Open Office, so I uninstalled it, and installed Open Office 2.0.  This was a major learning experience figuring out how to handle the RPMs from the command line.  I managed the install, and then the applications would not run.  I had *heard* of the CHMOD command, but I didn't expect to have to apply it to a newly installed application.

I now see that with Hoary, I can't even view (yet) the contents of my NTFS partition.  I do realize that I won't be able to write, but I did expect to be able to view files, copy from NTFS to the Linux drive, etc.  6.06 allowed me to view and copy.

I'm not frustrated - it's been a great learning experience.  However, while great strides have been made since I first tried Linux in the mid 90s, I still don't yet see Linux as a mainstream Windows replacement simply due to the problems I encountered.

I'm curious now how it will be to upgrade in the future.  Reformatting my Linux partition again will not be acceptable.  Any application or operating system upgrade should be moderately painless.    One thing I am thankful for is GRUB.  Throughout all my reinstalls, my NTFS Windows partition remained intact, and I always had dual-boot capability.  Also due to above issues, I cannot yet completely delete my Windows partition.

Most people will pirate that anyways, it's mostly big companies that buy things like that.

### Post by forrestcupp on 2006-07-10
> **koshari said:**
> "- hard to install apps or get apps for that matter"

synaptic is far easier than anything on windows.

try dowloading a windows app then going to run it, and get, you need .net framework, or sunfin like that, 

synaptic fetches all your dependencies for you!

Synaptic is amazingly easy.  Much easier than installing software on windows.  But if you want something that's not in the repositories, a lot of times it's a royal pain in the backside to install.  Take Cinerella, for instance.  It's the only mature free Linux software available that does professional video editing.  Don't even try to tell me about Kino, etc.  They are a waste of time, unless all you want to do is paste some clips together.  So why in the world hasn't Cinerella made it to the repos?  Anyway, I finally got it installed, using alien and having to manually track down the extensive list of dependencies.  But it was really a pain to get it installed and working because it's not in the repos.  Just face it, it is definitely harder to install things that aren't in your Synaptic list than it is to install things in Windows.  Other than the few .net programs and the very few Visual Basic runtime programs, everything else for Windows either doesn't need dependencies, or it comes with what it needs in the automatic installer.  When I installed the great "free software" called the Gimp for Windows, I had to download the GTK runtime environment to get it to work.  And that is a Linux port.

### Post by Adamant1988 on 2006-07-10
Actually, I'm thinking the biggest setback for linux isn't necissarily linux.  I'm pointing my finger at the hardware companies on this one. 

It just takes one "favorite gadget" to stop someone from switching, even if they have help with the switch.  

No wireless you say? No linux for me.

Linux doesn't come preinstalled on PC's 9/10 times and the major hardware vendors don't support it really. Dell even goes so far to say that they won't service their machines if you put Linux on them. 

The only other problem with linux is the lack of a universal package format that's commonly implimented.  (although I do LOVE autopackage which is what Xara came in).  if all of the distros had a common package format they used then life would be GREAT for new users... we could look on the web and find things in .package or .deb or .rpm or whatever and just download and install them (with the installer program on our computer handeling dependancies).

(go autopackage )

### Post by themainliner on 2006-07-13
Other - user reluctance.  

Linux is outside of the average user's comfort zone.  They buy a PC and it works (after a fashion) and they try to get used to it.  This inevitably means getting used to Windows (XP these days) and lets face it XP is pretty good as OS's go...the average user wonders what exactly is the point of changing?  

Also they do not understand the delinations between hardware, firmware, Operating System, API and software.  If they want the PC to do something they 'install something into it'.  Linux looks pretty, or at least different, but what is the point of moving from something that works to anything else.  We can shout until blue in the face about our platform being better, but to get these benefits appears to be difficult to the novice and their PC works so why mess with it?

### Post by mech7 on 2006-07-13
I think it is a combination of..

- Poor GUI, there are even many graphic control that need to be launched with a command line, you can't even right click files or dirs to open them as root ](*,)
- Not enough software support
- Software installs in 3 different places, no standard point for install.
- Poor menu editor, when installing software it usually does not show up in the menu but you have to find out where it is yourself, also the edit option in the menu editor are just wel broken. Also it is extremly hard to find where the software installed as the search only works in the home directory?
- Not stable.. especially with XGL it crashes many times :-k 
- No standarized executables for setup files.
- Not enough hardware support
- Disorganized device manager
- Extremly hard to install correct drivers for example videocard or wlan cards.

I think if all of the issues have been resolved and each time something is added to the core it is integrated in the gui asswell ubuntu has a damn good chance of taking over many pc's but at it's current state it is lacking on some points.

### Post by OneSeventeen on 2006-07-13
I'll throw my opinion in, just for the fun of it:

Windows Installs on more x86 hardware without problems than Ubuntu (sorry, I use ubuntu at work and at home, yet I still can't figure out how to install it on my friend's 2nd hard drive...  well, it installs, but it won't boot, even when I manually boot that drive)

So:
[list]
[*]ease of install still goes in Windows' favor, since it works on more x86 hardware.
[*]Proprietary media types (MP3/DVD/WMA)
[*]Proprietary Software Availability (MS Office, Adobe, etc)
[*]Marketing (geeks ranting is negative publicity, so we've got a lot to make up for)
[/list]

To clarify: I have not had problems when installing Ubuntu as the only OS, it is only when trying to install it in a dual-boot environment that gives me troubles.  It works on my laptop, but not on any modern Dell desktop that I've tried.

I still don't know why we can't make it easy to install MP3/DVD playback (can't someone get a closed-source DVD player authorized by the DVD companies and give it away, free as in Beer?)
Could we also add an easy-to-purchase Win32Codec package that is legal?

I don't like MS Office, but I have to admit, if I want to make a table in a document where one cell spans multiple rows, OpenOffice.org just can't...  And we use Access at work, which I can't connect to in Linux.  Remember, switching OSes also relies on the OS being able to deal with existing work.

And finally, we need some commercials.  Why not get the blender foundation to work on an amazing ubuntu commercial.  I'm sure they'd be willing to compile it on Ubuntu servers and design it on Ubuntu desktops.

(don't argue with these points with how bad Microsoft is at stuff, I could have written 10 times as much information as to why Windows isn't ready to be widespread either...:p but please let me know if I'm wrong otherwise, so I can better explain stuff to non-linux users)

---

### Post by SonicSteve on 2006-07-14
I'm very new to linux and ubuntu so take this for what its worth.
I should however say that I'm not new to computers, I have extensive experience in hardware and windows.

Things I see holding back linux are from most important to least;

1.a. **too many versions of linux**, they're all different, from slight to very. Which one should the public or the business community pick? It took me weeks and months to find Ubuntu. This is the second time I've played with it and finally enough experience from the first adventure stuck to allow me to break through and figure some things out. 

1.b.**THERE ARE TOO MANY CHIEFS IN THE LINUX COMMUNITY AND NOT ENOUGH INDIANS**. Sorry if that phrase offends I don't mean anything racial. **If all the increadible programmers in the worldwide Linux community could ever come together they would easilly rival Microsoft. But instead they are all split into factions of which Ubuntu is one of.**

2. Linux is very intimidating, not always intuitive to carry out simple tasks like adding a second hard drive or simple file sharing between two lan computers, just to name a few. These kinds of things required me to do hours of searching and tinkering. 

3. New concepts to learn, such as root, and sudo. Why are such things needed? I'm sure they are but to the beginning user they are just enormous pains in the butt. I know that windows has similar technology but it's more hidden. 

4. Too much is still done from text files like smb.conf and others like it. This kind of tasks need applets for the novice user to accomplish anything. If I didn't have experience with Autoexec.bat and config.sys this kind of configuration would have been near impossible for me. There are kinds of users and novice/new users of computers just couldn't handle Linux in it's current state. They can hardly handle windows and for the most part everything there has become gui configurable.

Argue if you must but from what I've seen I'm not wrong. Sorry if that sounds arrogant.

### Post by sim085 on 2006-07-14
Games!

In my opinion games are the reason behind what makes an operating system successfull or not.

I do not know how many of you people here remember the Amiga.  Well ask yourself, why was Amiga so successfull?  Why every child wanted one?  Well the answer for that question is that Amiga has a good graphics system that maked games much nicer, and it also had a lot of games!!

Why did Amiga have a sudden dead?  Because all the game manufecturers started doing games for the PC rather then Amiga and that meant that no one needed an Amiga any more!

Why is MAC less used then Windows? Because Windows has more games supported on it then MAC!

Basically what you need is in OS that can run games, and somehow make companies that make games start developing games for linux.  That in turn will start making people use Linux ;) easy right :P

However that is just my opinion,

regards,
sim085

### Post by XVampireX on 2006-07-14
Hmm, there are few things:

Standardization:
----------------
Maybe everyone talks about all kinds of standards, such as Open Document Standard and so on... There really isn't a true standard. For example, GNU/Linux has several Sound Systems... Many developers develop in either one of these, and to tell you the truth, both suck. OSS hogs up the sound card and ALSA is too complicated. Also, it seems GNU/Linux has quite a bit of choice... again... For example, Distributions... Too many of them! "What's the best distro for me?" is something people just can't answer, without having to go to philosophical debate (I.E: "Use what you feel is right to you"). So my point: Choice is good. Not too much choice.

Brainwash!:
----------
Ok, so as we all know, humans are by nature resistant to change. And as we all know, Windows is a standard (I.E: Everyone sells it, together with the PC, and most everyone uses it...). So my point here is that we have to brainwash people into thinking/believing that linux is MUCH better than windows and that switching is a MUST (Or else...). That's basically what Microsoft does to hold the market in their name.

Optional - Monopoly:
-------------------
Must start holding a monopoly over linux and doing microsoft tactics :D Though that would be silly...

---

### Post by blastus on 2006-07-14
I think that as Microsoft continues to jack up the price of Windows (and Office) and continues to rake in monopoly profits, more and more people will be reluctant to continue using Windows (and Office). Eventually the system will break down because the cost of Windows will inevitably overshadow the cost of the hardware that runs it--especially on lower-end systems. Some people won't be able to or won't want to pay such a high price for software that should have been commoditized a long time ago, but wasn't because one company held a monopoly in those software markets. 

But before that happens, I predict that Microsoft will start offering Windows/Office on an optional subscription basis, where you rent the software. After that, it would just be a matter of time before the subscription model became mandatory and perpetual licences for Windows/Office are no longer sold. Microsoft could very easily market the benefits of a subscription model by making it initially cheaper to rent to a Windows/Office license rather than buy a perpetual one. I can just see the headlines; "Subscribe to Windows today and you'll never have to pay to upgrade ever again!" It's going to happen; it's just a matter of time before the software industry shifts to a service-orientated industry.

---

### Post by Stormy Eyes on 2006-07-14
> **SonicSteve said:**
> Stormy keep in mind that apple isn't hardly in the same league (marketshare) as microsoft.

No, Apple doesn't have anywhere near the marketshare that Microsoft has. On the other hand, Apple seems to cater to a few "niche" markets: creative types, education types, people who want to get shit done and look good doing it, and a couple of others. It appears to do a decent business catering to the artsy types, the teachers, and those geeks who find themselves in possession of a sense of fashion and enough cash to indulge their need for stylish gear. I'd say that Apple is neutral towards games and gamers. It won't get in the developers' way, but I don't see Steve Jobs courting the average gamer.

Do we really need more than one Microsoft, more than one vendor that tries to be all things to all people and ends up making a commodity of mediocrity? IMO, Microsoft makes mediocre software for mediocre people; that is the secret of its success.

### Post by fogster74 on 2006-07-15
I'm a 31 year old guy who's been using computers since I was seven. I hate Microsoft but, and here's the clincher, despite crashes, security concerns etc, the fact is that I don't have to spend three days on Microsoft Windows trying to get my Dell laptop wireless drivers to work, then over a week of trawling forums trying to learn to edit an xorg.conf file to make my external monitor work. I've searched the forums, I've posted posts for help - nothing. It's all very hit and miss, I'm determine to crack Linux and get it working - but the average user would have given up long ago. I still can;t my HP Deskjet printer to instal, I have no idea why, again the forums have, so far, proved fruitless.

So I would say that, for me as someone studying a postgraduate MSc in Computing at UCL in London, having programmed in everything from Cobol to BBC Basic Assembler to Java and PHP, if it takes me this damn long to get Linux up ad running, what hope has the average Joe?! I appreciate that Kubuntu is one of the best distros out there and, for many, it will work straight out the box (a great step forward); furthermore, I know that WindowsXP is flawed in so many ways but those flaws tend to be more predictable and easier to fix.

I take my hat off to everyone who uses Linux and, like you Master Barristas scoffing as I sip down my first cup of the strong stuff, I aspire to gain knowledge and become a user and expert.

However - therein lies the other problem. As a community we have to accept that Linux is flawed and not give ironic and sarcastic replies. If we accept the issues of adopting Linux and all actively support new users then, and only then, will the desktop adoption of Linux take off. New users such as my mum or my uncle are not going to want to have to become Linux experts - it should plug in, it should switch on, it should work. Until that day comes, we've all got a problem...

---

### Post by marcip on 2006-07-15
Lack of attention to the dial-up Internet connection via modem. 

The Ubuntu team naively assumes that every user will have broadband, just as they have; so they put no noticable effort into modem configuration tools, and make it next to impossible (for a novice) to perform system maintenance for a computer that is not on-line or is on-line only occasionally via a slow modem connection. Well, I have news for them: **this is how most of the world operates**. It is apsurd to force the user who can perform most of what is the "real use" of his computer (primarily e-mail, some low bandwidth browsing) via modem into broadband just so he cen maintain his system! Under circumstances, why does Ubuntu even bother mailing install CD's? (otherwse a laudable gesture).

Other than that, I'd say it is the best desktop Linux, bar none.
marcip

### Post by Shadowfire on 2006-07-15
Its not that business or the other items of the desktop are less significant. It is that people expect it just to work.  I think there are some minor adjustments in polishing for this, but IMHO it is simple as this -  When you have major game makers natively coding Game releases on the Linux desktop with out emulation, wine and cedega the you will gain the attention of the world and the masses more then you have up till now.

I know you wouldn't think that it is that big of a deal, but think about how much money is invested everyday on entertainment.  Sometimes the obvious is right there and you just have to turn off the back ground noise to hear it.

I think it was best put in Hewy Lewis and the News song "All I want is a couple days off!"

:)

DirectX is no better then any other game coding that allows game makers to put games on the desktop.  It's just Microsoft has DirectX to their advantage, close source and doesn't port to other OS easily, and you have to pay fees to Microsoft to be able to build in DirectX enviroment.  It keeps games on the Windows desktop, and untill people break out of the DirectX stinking thinking linux will never grow to its full potential. Alot of savy people play games, and would definately take the plunge if the Big Games were on the Linux desktop natively!

I can't tell you how many times I have heard it.  The first thing I hear is "I won't switch till I can play BF2 or COD2" or "I've tried it and Wine was to complicatied" or "I did the dual boot but I went back to Windows because it was easier".

There needs to be a major change with native gaming for linux, we need major game makers to natively support linux with their games and it really should have already happened!  This has been talked about and talked about but is yet to be done.

On another note - it would also be nice to see companies like Intuit make Quikbooks Pro for Linux.  I know GNU Cash will work for Quicken, but I don't know how it's features are compared to Quickbooks and Quickbooks Pro.

### Post by Stormy Eyes on 2006-07-15
> **Shadowfire said:**
> I know you wouldn't think that it is that big of a deal, but think about how much money is invested everyday on entertainment.  Sometimes the obvious is right there and you just have to turn off the back ground noise to hear it.

I spend a fair amount on entertainment myself. However, unlike many of the "Linux needs this or that to succeed crowd", I'm not arrogant enough to suggest that developers who work on Linux voluntarily put aside what they want and focus on what I want. I'm not paying them, so I have no right to complain. 

Nor am I willing to bitch about how game developers aren't willing to bother with Linux ports. Windows is where the money is, and development houses have to turn a profit in order to survive, and must frequently answer to both publishers and stockholders. If it isn't economical to develop for Linux, then the game developers won't bother with Linux or treat Linux as more than an afterthought, Id Software and its generosity in providing Linux binaries notwithstanding.

Linux isn't Burger King. If you want to have it your way, you'd better be willing and able to either do it yourself or pay others to do it for you.

> **Shadowfire said:**
> I can't tell you how many times I have heard it.  The first thing I hear is "I won't switch till I can play BF2 or COD2" or "I've tried it and Wine was to complicatied" or "I did the dual boot but I went back to Windows because it was easier".

And I can't tell you how many times I've responded to such complaints with, "Go back to Windows, then, if that makes you happy. You're welcome to use Linux, but Linux doesn't need you." Linux doesn't really need the desktop market, IMHO, not when Linux can be used in handheld devices, cell phones, servers, and embedded computers. There's more to personal computing than desktop machines and laptops.

> **Shadowfire said:**
> There needs to be a major change with native gaming for linux, we need major game makers to natively support linux with their games and it really should have already happened!  This has been talked about and talked about but is yet to be done.

Now, that sounds pretty arrogant to me. Is it really your place or mine to say that such and such "needs to happen"? Sure, you'd like to see more game development done on Linux. I can understand the sentiment, even though I don't agree and think that the gamers can go f--- themselves.

> **Shadowfire said:**
> On another note - it would also be nice to see companies like Intuit make Quikbooks Pro for Linux.

Well, don't talk about it here; write a snailmail letter to Intuit's marketing department and encourage others to do the same. Try buying a copy of th Windows version and send proof of purchase with your request for a Linux version so that the marketing people will see that you're a paying customer.

Money talks when it comes to proprietary software, and BS walks. Show 'em the money.

As far as Ubuntu is concerned I think it's one of the versions worth investing in. The trouble is that egos get in the way (on many levels) and I'm not one who matters in this case. I'll get off my soapbox now.

I'll talk to all later.

### Post by Cinquero on 2006-07-17
Well, I have some experience with those non-Linux users.

What you need is:

1.) It must run out-of-the-box.
2.) Upgrades must be seamless and fully automatic.
3.) There may not be any necessary user-configuration except for the first time.
4.) Seamless backup solutions.

How to solve it:

1.) Pre-install it. Sell explicitly supported and well-tested hardware with it. Put hardware recommendations online. Just look like Apple did it. Tell the user which DV-Cams (for example) he/she may use without problems. Give out free versions to the testers, give out non-free versions (in form of, maybe, a monthly subscription) of tested versions. Just make yourself clear that you cannot support EVERY hardware out there. Therefore make RELIABLE recommendations and/or sell it along with the OS.

2.) There are different possibilities on how to do that. First: takes some gigs of additional harddisk space, but just update an off-line installation and switch to it on next reboot. The second possibility is slower: download in background and tell the user to reboot for doing system updates. Either way, it would be MUCH better than what MS does with Windows -- install and configure once and never again.

3.) How is that handled today? Is there an application-independent configuration storage format used? If not, do it. Each upgrade must then have scripts that transform those configs into app configs.

4.) Backup is important. If a user has at least two PCs, he should be able to use them for doing network backups (in the background via a GUI, and not just rsync). Improve data integrity by providing services that check the user's data on a regular basis, taking machine failure (RAM, disk, CPU, everything) into account by letting the hosts check each other.

### Post by kornelix on 2006-07-19
Linux cannot spread beyond the geeks and zealots as long as installations remain complex, distributions are mutually incompatible, and software remains buggy and user hostile.

specifics: 

screwing around forever with multimedia apps from various unofficial sources that don't install easily, or just don't work

400 Linux distributions, each a little different (directory names, library locations, desktop and windowing systems, security management, preferred or default applications).

error messages that don't exist, are hidden from the user, or are incomprehensible to anyone but the developer

3rd party developers have to make their stuff work and be supportable on too many Linux variations - so many just refuse to deal with Linux

 The lack of vendor support for printers, wireless adaptors, etc. is partly explained by the prior point.

For the general market, standardization is more important than technical capability or the creative freedom of developers. Windows does not have to be nearly as good as Linux to keep 95% of the market. The horrible security problems in Windows have not caused the world to move to Linux, and for understandable reasons.

### Post by Lin-X on 2006-07-19
> **trash said:**
> I do installations for people, 90% of the time when I am dealing with a potential client I hear (replace XXXX with brother/sister/computer friend and the list goes on and on....

 "my XXXX said that linux is too over my head, too hard to learn....."

For some reason people who may have tried linux 2-5 years ago can't fathom the idea that linux evolves the same way windows does...(even better in my opinion)

Well, I guess no one will ever read this because the original is so old, but, anyway: You are so-o-o-o right. The average user of computers is way-way- way
behind times, even those who consider themselves "power users" are mostly not up to snuff. Here is an example: In 2001 I chose as a class project (1) installing a dual boot Linux; (2) configuring one hardware item; (3) installing one item of software not included with the distro. I was amazed at the reaction. People slid up to me outside of class and murmmered ominously, "Be careful! You might ruin your computer beyond repair!"  My instuctor, supposedly an accomplished and informed computer specialist, told me, "Are you sure you wouldn't like to ammend this? Perhaps some other project would be more appropriate to your level?" And, of course, I heard, over and over and OVER again --- until I wanted to throw up --- the ever popular and eternal "Linux is not ready for the desktop." 
     I was able to give a presentation of the installation of Xandros desktop (the fastest installation of any Linux os --- and I have tried a dozen at least!) that was concise, detailed, and absolutely successful. I installed, successfully, Corel WordPerfect Office Suite; I configured my laser printer and printer, and (Gasp!) printed from it right there ... in the very classroom! GOSH!
     You might think this made a big difference in the attitude toward Linux. Dream on, my sweet children! After the presentation, I was congratulated on my "bravery"; I was actually stared at! (Well, come on! This was a class where one student got an A for a presentation of choosing and installing MSWord!)
     As we used to say, back in the old, old, old days, Linux has "a bad reputation"... you know, hangs out on street corners, goes for a ride with strange men and doesn't come home til 4 am. I don't know what to do a about it, but it's out there and that's a BIG reason people don't even consider Linux as  an OS.

### Post by SkyNet2029 on 2006-07-31
IMHO: Windows has placed the end-user in such a nightmarish hell of daily security updates, massive applications downloads, most of which are not free; the majority of the 'freeware' -and when I say freeware I am meaning for windows - is simply an application to load up more binaries and DLL's that copied to its very own folder, thus increasing the interweaving of a file system structure that relies on so many other 'integrated' application, that this is what they have come to expect. 
It took me about a week to realize (my wife pointed this out actulally) that I don't cuss at my machines anymore. Well, of not, there is nothing to fix.

### Post by peabody on 2006-08-07
The two greatest hurdles to Linux adoption by the masses is a lack of 3rd party support for hardware drivers and software patents.  Linux can't (legally in the US anyway) use patented software algorithms for playback of media.

### Post by tbrminsanity on 2006-08-19
In my mind there are three things holding back Ubuntu (and Linux in general) from being the number one OS in the market.
1. Ease of use vs other OS like Windows and MacOS
What can I say, it is easier for my grandfather to use windows then it is to use Linux.
2. Initial use
Most people (around 65-85%) will continue to use the first OS they are presented as it is the first they get use to.  The only thing that stops this is if thier OS no longer exists (AmigaOS, DOS, BeOS, etc) or their old OS is just a piece of crap (Win 3.1).
3. Puplicity
Face it, Microsuck owns 75-90% of the OS market and as such can force Windows down anyones throught.  There is only a handful of people that have used computers and not used Windows.  As a result if some one does switch off their OS they will tend to go towards the biggest loudest OS available to them (ie Windows).

---

### Post by Paul_UK on 2006-08-19
Hardware support.  This has improved considerably, but there are still issues with certain graphics cards, wireless networks etc.  This isn't helped by the manufacturers (usual first place to look for drivers in Windows) as they often don't make drivers available for Linux.

Lack of support for MP3 files etc (at least not built-in).  Thinks like EasyUbuntu do make it easy to set this up, but the information isn't obvious to newbies.

Difficulty finding answers to questions and solutions to problems.  Things have improved a lot over the last year or so though, especially in these forums.  But newbies from the world of Windows don't expect to have to search around forums and try things - they expect to press F1 and get an answer.  But of course Microsoft only release a new version of Windows every 3-4 years whereas there's a new Ubuntu every six months, and documentation probably tends to be lower prioriy than making it work properly.

Things are really moving in the right direction with Ubuntu though.  I know of a number of Windows users giving Ubuntu a serious try, though some are being frustrated by problems they can't get answers for.

I think Vista could be good for Ubuntu.  Windows users will try it, may not like it, and will be looking for an alternative.  

Vista will also bring a lot of good used PCs onto eBay etc because they can't run Vista properly.  They will be cheap and will run Linux fine.

### Post by bdalzell on 2006-08-19
It think that misinformation or commercial ties play a part. we have a local computer store which sells used systems. Many older laptops are not going to be able to handle winXp much less vista. I got a Pentium III laptop recently for only $200 from them because of that. Ubuntu loads up and runs great from the Live CD - found drivers for everything first time (all I had to do was add memory). I suggested to the owner that they could get sales for these older laptops that are sitting around by putting GNU/Linux on them (I left them a Ubuntu Live disk). "Oh no" says their chief technician. "the support hassles would be a nightmare".

Now I know from hanging around the store that most of their support income seems to be from taking people's virus and malware ridden systems in and getting rid of the malware for a fee (and it is actually a pretty reasonable fee around $100). If the systems had Ubuntu I suspect there would be far fewer systems coming in needing a clean up. In addition I suspect the chief tech is not very Linux savvy, although he really does know how to subdue a windows machine.

### Post by iggyboy on 2006-08-22
This is my first thread, although i have been actively using this forum for past 3 month to solve and get answered to most of my linux problems and questions. 

I have been using M$ product since the days of DOS (15 years back), utill last two months ago when i encounter Kubuntu. Previously I have tried many linux distros but none has last more then a week in my PC box or worked out for my daily use.

The biggest setback that REALLY preventing Linux to widespread is SOFTWARE PIRACY (well atleast in my country, Malaysia). In my place, anyone can get ANY software known to man for the price of a cup noodle. Being an engineering student my system used to have all kind of full blown high end softwares that's equivalent to hundreds of thousand of dollars, ofcourse if i purchased it legally. But the truth is it only cost me less then 100 Ringgit Malaysia (about USD40.00).

Although there are many reason that I see which draws back linux from widespread use, things like insufficient speciality software like CAD/CAM, not enough games, no hardware drivers etc... In my opinion it's all comes to one simple reason SOFTWARE PIRACY... it's the real culprit that cause all that "not-enough or not-available" problem.

Almost every home computer user in South East Asia are using pirated M$ software that cost less than USD 10.00 dollars. Why would one go to unfamiliar operating system (linux in this case) whereby one can get familiar operating system for almost free. If every one are to pay for software then I believe more people will be willing to use unfamiliar software (linux), simply because it can be obtain free or at much cheaper price.

Average software vendors reluctant to invest in Linux, simply because less linux user meaning less possible profit compare to vast M$ based user. Generally a businessman will only invest where he find more customers.

If sofware piracy is totally eliminated then I believe more people will be drive to use linux and increases linux user. This will give more confident to businessman to invest in linux (more software) and also hardware vendors will force to provide thier support to linux (more harware compatibility).

p/s: I have been using Kubuntu linux full time for past three months or more and never touch M$ Window$ (although it still exist in my PC) since then. As a proud Linux user I have demonstrated to my college and friends the reallity and simplicity of Linux to them, but the feed-back is "M$ Window$ DVD only cost RM15.00 dollars and M$ 0ffice Enterprise Edition always included in the DVD. Why bother Linux when M$ Window$ are cup noodle price"

Just my two cent :)

### Post by bobbonomo on 2006-08-22
I think the bottom line is ease of use. The competition is Microsoft and no matter what you think or say of them they are slick.

Put aside the tired "security issue" that is always mentioned. When the moms and pops of the world will find it as easy as XP or 2K then it will be widespread. And it's FREE. Can't find a better price the $0.0

Slibuntu say "linux is almost as easy to use as Windows. ALMOST. The fact that you are here and have tried something different says that you are not the general user. 

Most peeple just want to use computer and not have a degree in CS. What it does under the hood to get done what needs to be done is irrelevant. They don't want to know. And with reason. What do you know about your car? Turn the key and it starts. Press the pedal and it goes. Press the other pedal and it stops. You need to know about the steering wheel too:)

The command line is a big issue and people just don't want to be bothered. Let the programmers and computer figure out all the underlying commands to get the job done. That's its job. Simplfy life and do the mundane.

I grabbed this at random using Google. Look at this list of instruction for an upgrade of SiS drivers. 
Look at those names and the depth of those directories. uppercase, lowercase, dashes, dots, underscores.
Mom and pop just want to download an upgrade and install. 

I unpacked the downloaded file in my 'matt' user's home directory:
$ cd ~matt
$ tar zxvf sis_drv.o_4.3.0_gcc2_300803-1.tar.gz
Finally it is just a case of copying the unpacked file over the top of the SiS driver included in the XFree86 default installation. The following needs to be done as the root user:
# cp ~matt/sis_drv.o /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/drivers

My background is computing since the mid 60's. Command line was all I knew until useful GUI's became common in the mid 90's.

### Post by AndyWise on 2006-08-23
Hi 

I have seen some realy interesting treds (didnt read 'em all - too many, so i hope i dont repeat someons words) here and some interesting points of view BUT most of all i must agree with AYSIU and his post "[Is Ubuntu for You?]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=63315")" becouse its so damn true: Linux is not Windows and you cant expect it working like Windows. So thats why you cant just install Linux and complain how it sux and all.
First of all, installation:
I cant see nothing easyer then to open Synaptic and choose the program i want. If the setback is not knowing the programs you have [this forum]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org") where you can find out what programs are for what. I have never installed a single thing without someone explaining how its done, and you must admit its not too hard to copy things into Terminal. If you understand why did you copy something then its easy to learn and you can do it on your own some day.
Second, needs:
What does a regular user need? He/she needs programs and to know how to work with them. And thats truly it. I've installed Ubuntu to a friend (on computer where Windows constantly crashes - hardwear probles, and its a very old computer - but still didnt have to manualy set-up any hardwear), set it up properly, and showed him how to use programs. Sure he has some questions how to do stuff but nothing that would need Terminal. And I must say I'm quite amazed that so many people dont even know Linux exists.
Third, updating: 
Very much like Windows. You press the icon when it pops-up and start updating. And a regular user doesnt update hardwear.
I could continue but I'm too long already...

If people would grow up with linux like they did with windows there would be better support and more users.

p.s. i know my english sux

### Post by Catewilliams on 2006-08-25
I'd say the knowledge you need to know and should know to use Linux is one of the setbacks to it being used widespread. You need to know how to download things, use the command line and so on. 

Also, games, cool web applications and other trivial things can't be used automatically in Linux. You need to downlown emulators and so on to make those run, those require time. In windows, it takes a few clicks to get a game running correctly and you're set. 

Also, I run Linux on my laptop. When I go places, a lot of people ask me what I'm doing on my laptop and so on. I explain and they get this deer-in-headlights look and claim I'm using something that's way over my age level. So it's discouragement like that among other things that set people back. 

Teens make up a lot of computer users, obviously. So running games is improtant, web based flash games are important. Using a webcam is important. Things like that need to be set up properly in Linux. 

Just my two cents, being a teenager, It's rather hard to do everything in Linux, but once I get the hang of it, I could never go back to the security blanket that was once windows for me. :rolleyes:

### Post by MïLÖ_The_Ñµmß on 2006-08-25
MS Office is certainly a big one.  It very well may be the golden goose for Microsoft.  Accounts for 30% of their income. Make it run on Linux out of the box and it further evens the playing field in the business environment. That environment drives the software market.  More business movement to Linux means more software written/ported to Linux.  Like a snowball after that.  Office is a wide road in - 90% of the business office suite space belongs to Microsoft.  In the business world the number one reason for not going to Linux is Office on Windows (I have to have that so why run something else - just complicates things).  

You can't fight this with alternative office products.  They fall short of seamless compatibility (or so they are percieved as such regardless) and if an alternative starts to make inroads that really damages MS bottom line, they can just start dropping the price to keep their space on the desktop (at least until any other offering becomes a spec of dust again).  

Instead, you must embrace MS Office - that's how you beat them. 

Must replace or tie into the Outlook PIM too - eliminate the need for Exchange and work with office, then you're getting somewhere.  Combine this with TCO and CHOMP!  You get a big bite out of MS.

### Post by Joer4x4 on 2006-08-27
When it becomes competitve with MS and the applications that run on MS.

Average user want to sit down and get their work done with little fanfare. The shortest distance between two points is a straight line.

I know of no average user who wants or is willing to download a distro, then have to figure out (get technical) how to get there hardware working. A good example is a modem. While MS took away the hardware modem and converted it to a soft-modem, average user doesn't know the difference (or cares). He just wants to work and work now. Proprietary excuses won't fly with average user.

Applications need to be near 100% compatable with MS apps along with choice of apps. As another example... look at Abiword. Average user (at home) uses MS-Word. Do you really think they want to take a step backward? When something is started, it must be finished...and done quicky. The argument can be made that Abiword is great for most users. But that's not how MS sees it. Its Word for everyone and it's easier to convince the populas when it's bloated up! And when it's bloated, it makes it harder to be compatable with.

Linux need to be preloaded on machines in a massive way just like MS is.

From my point of view, when Linux is ready to compete head to head with MS, then Linux will be prime time. Linux needs to rethink itsself and solve a few issues for out of the box perfection. Linux must better MS, commit resources for doing it "today", and deliver high quality products that work the way the users want. In other words...compete like a business...not an alternative. It's all about expectations and marketing, but you have to deliver too. Abiword just might be better for the world, but you'll have to convince the world of that!

### Post by Burnspot on 2006-08-27
Sorry, I'm not going to peruse all 94 pages, so I'll just dump my $.02 based on the last couple of pages. :D

MS Office and drivers would be the two biggest obstacles in our office. I oversee around 30 Windows boxes, but we do utilize RHEL and CentOS servers to run some databases and websites. Nearly everyone in the office is firmly entrenched in Office and when they see OpenOffice, they fail to be impressed for whatever reason. That and knowing the silly problems our Windows users (salespeople) get themselves into on a daily basis, I just don't see Linux as a viable alternative for them right now. Few have ever seen a command line and if the need ever came up for them to use it in Linux, they'd be lost. I could see complete chaos ensuing when trying to do something as simple as bumping up their screen resolution from whatever Xorg picked out. Our design team might be easier to swing as they have more computer knowledge in general, but pulling them from Dreamweaver, FrontPage, Flash, and Photoshop would be a tough sell.

A lot of our users have "gadgets" like PDA's, cameras, and other things they actively use in their work. They plug them in and expect them to work, which generally happens 100% of the time in Windows...Linux is a bit more challenging in this regard.

Personlly, I've used Linux off and on in the past, but never committed to it until now - have to learn it now in order to take over management of our RHEL servers from the 3rd party company that's bleeding our company dry, lol. I'd consider myself an advanced Windows user, but I've found Linux challenging in spots. I can only imagine the experience some of our users would have..yikes!

I've offered to put Linux on anyone's desktop who wants to try it out, but I've gotten no takers so far. Probably the one factor holding that back is our required use of some online applications that work in Internet Explorer only.

I think once those bumps are smoothed out, Linux will have a lot easier time getting itself into the minds of the everyday average Windows person.

---

### Post by nardis_Miles1 on 2006-08-27
Games. Also, the lack of games is a difficulty. Oh, yes. My children can't play their games on linux. The remedy would be, at least, three fold. 

First, WINE would need a much more intense effort. 

Second, working with the game vendors would be smart. Could the community give tutorials about X-graphics?

Third, better access to graphics drivers. To do this, members of the Linux community would have to be willing to sign and honor non-disclosure agreements. Of course, this would mean that the games would be distributed in binary, and that they would not be free.

### Post by mdsmedia on 2006-08-27
I've started reading this thread so many times and got so sick of the same old responses that I thought I'd just go to the last page and see if there was anything new.

The thing which is stopping Linux from becoming widespread is that it's not Windows.

Windows is on "every" box that comes out of the shop. When you buy a new PC it has Windows on it.

What Linux needs is to become a standard OS on SCHOOL PCs and workstations. Schools need PCs which provide tools to students wanting to get projects completed. Linux has all the tools that any of them need. If kids are getting their first exposure to computers at school, and they use Linux at school, then Windows will no longer be the ONLY OS.

Almost all the responses talk about not having Office or Powerpoint or THIS or THAT. They are PACKAGES which have been created for WINDOWS. If people got used to say OpenOffice or Gimp (just for example) BEFORE they were exposed to MS Monopolyware, and bear in mind that OOo and Gimp are FREE, the mindsets wouldn't need to be changed.

Schools need to be educated in Linux and OpenSource software, so that they realise what's avaialable and they can use their tight budgets in far more responsible economic ways.

I agree most with this idea..

> **Joer4x4 said:**
> ...From my point of view, when Linux is ready to compete head to head with MS, then Linux will be prime time. Linux needs to rethink itsself and solve a few issues for out of the box perfection.

Unfortunately, we need hardware copatibility (which is coming along nicely) and we need to market (which we are not). I have seen amazing progress in Linux, and I am extremely impressed with the improvements in the latest release we have today, but I will point out that we need numbwers to get results.

Microsoft pushed through its "designed for Windows 95" campaign by showing hardware manufacturers its installed base numbers. It got these numberrs by being familiar. Most users I talk to don't like windows. They use wiondows because they have it at work. As our friend said abaove, We need a business-type approach. Not a "for profit approach", but a business marketing approach. Novell may be helping us alot with the new suse. Mr Shuttleworth is another man to continue this push (hope you're listening Mr. S). Technicians need to follow suit, as well. We know the TCO numbers. We can show how much cheaper our solution is.

If you want hardware support, and you want commercial support, you need a market. Free or not, you need numbers to get this. Business desktops, particularly large business, is an excellent way to get the numbers.

### Post by boz on 2006-08-27
> **earobinson said:**
> Where is the option to say that windows is just better? Or to say linux just needs time, or that linux is not designed to be used by the advrage user.

i know that these are all populare reasons for why linux is not wide spread

I wouldn't say that Windows is better.  For all the money consumers pump into their OS's they can still get viruses, registry errors, and lack of security updates and support (if a previous version becomes "obsolete").  However, Windows does offer ease-of-use, the ability to run very powerful applications, and very wide driver support (among other things). 

I still don't believe this justifies the price we have to pay ($300 - $400 for a single PC) just to install the current OS.  If we're going to pay that much, tools such as virus protection and registry cleaning/repair should come free (at the very least).

In essence, for what consumers pay, they attain a comparatively unstable OS.  Linux has far more potential, IMHO, to be a better Operating System.

### Post by Carrots171 on 2006-08-28
The most popular option in the poll is "3rd party support", and a whole lot of posts here say the reason why Linux isn't "widespread" is because it doesn't have as many games/apps/hardware drivers as Windows. But one of the main reasons why Linux doesn't have as many games/apps/hardware drivers is because it isn't as popular as Windows. There's a chicken-and-egg problem here if the people who say that Linux isn't as popular because of 3rd party support are right: Linux doesn't have as many games/apps/drivers because it isn't as popular, but Linux won't get as popular without games/apps/drivers. The lack of 3rd party support is definitely slowing down Linux adoption in the desktop market, but in my opinion it isn't what's preventing it. Linux is getting bigger and bigger and we're already seeing more and more hardware and software vendors provide support for Linux. As more and more hardware and software vendors provide support, Linux grows faster and faster and causes even more hardware and software vendors to provide support. In my opinion, however, this alone will not cause Linux to become mainstream on the desktop - pre-loading is the deciding factor.

### Post by NoTiG on 2006-09-03
I think theres 3 big reasons.

One is MS office. there are alot of apps that only work in microsoft, but im picking this one because its the most important. Open office is too slow, and doesnt synch with MS office properly at the moment.

The second is the fact that MS already has the largest market share and they use that as leverage through licensing, preventing OEM's from including anything else.

And the next big one is DirectX. with directx 10, game companies now are going to be programming with it for games years into the future. you might not realize it but games are a huge attraction. Just count up the posts through any "why havent you switched 100% thread?" and the majority is always games. When games work as well on linux, and there is an ms office equivalent.... the second reason i listed will hopefully shatter and there wont be anything left.

### Post by ago on 2006-09-03
The fact that ubuntu does not come preintsalled on common retailers (online or not) is IMHO the real show stopper. 

In the PC market most people do not really "choose" the OS, the same way they choose the color of their car... The PC market is like a car market where you can either buy a blue ford or a black BMW. If you want any other color you need to buy one of the two cars and repaint it yourself. How many are going to do that?

In such a market if you claimed that blue was the color of "choice" you would not be correct. Wouldn't you? If you were asked what OS you want on your machine, the same way you are asked what color you want your car, then and only then you could talk about choice. Withot choice the other items in the poll become less relevant. 

So you need to fix that first, either by making retailers "ask the color question" (difficult), or at least by introducing a new red car and hope that people will like the color and start asking for it also in other models.

Add to it that if linux was preinstalled, hardware compatibility would be far less relevant (only compatibility with USB devices would be important).

The niche market ubuntu could target is the low end computers, on machines costing half of the Vista ones because of cheaper software and lower hardware requirements. Once we have Xgl & co working out of the box, try to place ubuntu close to vista on the same shelf, with better eye candy, lots of apps preinstalled, and half the price tag... I bet there will be buyers for the new red car...

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-03
ago is right on about the preinstallation bit. I can count on one hand the number of people I know who do not work in technology (sys admin, network admin, webmaster, help desk, etc.) and who have installed their own operating systems.

Also, people make way too big a deal about gaming. Check out [this article from April 2006](http://www.clickz.com/showPage.html?page=3596631): > Adult gamers favor games on PCs rather than consoles and spend more time playing each week than do teenage gamers, according to the 2006 Gaming Technology Study released by the Consumer Electronics Association (CEA).

Different age groups exhibit different gaming preferences and behavior. **One-third of adults spend 10 hours or more playing games each week, compared with 11 percent of teens.** Teens in the 12 to 14 age group spend more time playing games than teens aged 15 to 17. The CEA speculates older teens have less free time or they're playing on wireless handsets. Eighty-one percent of teens aged 15 to 17 own or use a wireless phone. **Handheld systems such as the Sony PlayStation Portable (PSP) and Nintendo Dual Screen (DS) are used more often by teens.** Seventy-seven percent of teens who have a handheld system have used it in the last six months, compared to 25 percent of adult handheld owners.

Adults prefer playing games on PCs over consoles. Teens choose to play on consoles more often than computers. **Fifty-eight percent of households owning both a PC and a console system consider the console the dominant gaming platform.**

Teens tend to be more social when gaming. Sixty-four percent of adult gamers usually play console games alone, though 55 percent play console games online with other players. Teens are five times as likely to play a multiplayer game on a console with their opponents. Teenage girls comprise a large portion of multiplayer gamers. While teens do play opponents in person they're likely to play games online. Seventy-eight percent of male teens play online, compared to 58 percent of female teens.

Among adults, female gamers dominate in the 25 to 34 year-old age group. The saturation is due to free, online games played on sites like Yahoo Games. The 25 to 34 year-old cohort represents the largest concentration among overall female gamers (29 percent).

The study is comprised of data from an online survey of 1,767 adult video game households in December of 2005. Teens were surveyed via telephone interviews to a random national sample of 500 teens, 247 males and 253 females age 12 to 17. The teen survey was conducted in November, 2005. The parts to pay attention to are in bold. No, PC gaming is not what's even close to the "biggest setback that is preventing Linux from widespread adoption."

### Post by NoTiG on 2006-09-03
First of all, lets define a true setback. You see.... a real setback in my eyes is something that Windows can do, that linux cannot do. It would be easy to bypass the problem of not being installed on OEM's, simply if it was. What real advantage does microsoft have? One is that it has microsoft office, which delivers a superior experience (or not) to many of its customers. What else can windows do that linux can't? Sure it can play some codecs easier, but that doesnt mean they are better either. take mp3 vs ogg for instance. ogg is better. Well... narrowing it down, pretty much the only real advantage windows has is DirectX . Why else would microsoft spend so much of their budget developing it ? (ie. before it was also integrated into the window manager)  It has been called inferior in the past but thats really changed.

And what good is a statistic that compares the ratio of console vs pc players ? alot of people play both, what then? What we really need is a statistic that compares people who own modern computers that game, and those that dont. But even if only 10% of those actually game, while it may seem insignificant its actually a critical number because they are the ones that push hardware sales.

So lets look into the future shall we? Further down the road, linux is only going to improve. Gnome, and kde and whatever other DE is only going to get better. Already open office is good enough for the average home user. Hell, even Abiword is. So 5 years into the future from now you will see all these improvements, the interface will become easier to use, it will look nicer. Everything will mature. Except for one... and that is gaming. because its built on direct X, no amountof hacking will ever be able to keep linux on par with windows because its a moving target. And the game will always work maybe 3 months after its windows release or something.

### Post by Taskr36 on 2006-09-08
Quite simply, the biggest thing holding Linux back is that it things don't just work out of the box. I'm great with computers and I'm good at learning new things, but for the past 12 hours I've been fighting to get my wireless USB adapter to work with Ubuntu. 

I've installed every update, hunted and scoured the web for drivers, posted on forums asking how to install drivers, found drivers that I THINK are the right ones, installed the prerequisite software for the drivers, double clicked and seemingly installed the drivers, and I'm still getting absolutely nothing from my wireless adapter. That's 12 hours of hard work and I'm good with computers.

On a Windows XP computer it would have taken me less than five minutes. I would either use the install CD, download and install drivers from the manufacturers website, or gone to the windows update website. 

The average user wants to plug in a computer and use it. They don't want to spend their entire day off trying to make it work.

### Post by BLTicklemonster on 2006-09-09
I think Linux's inherent breakability plus the fact that those of you who can help are so far over the average beginner's heads when you try to help add up to disaster for the majority of newcomers. Not your fault that most people don't get it. You have been doing this for a long time, and don't relize that most of the time you need to post a full explanation in every thread you reply to because THESE ARE THE THREADS NEWBS SEE WHEN THEY SEEK SOLUTIONS. Sure you may be replying to a seasoned veteran's problem, but a lot of problems newbs run into could be easily taken care of if the solution were more comprehensive.

Remember, "we can see what you're doing" on a forum. Everyone who looks for a solution sees every post you make. No matter how redundant you are, you are doing the community a favor.

And a dummy button that restores x to it's primitive state with no hassle would be a plus. You know, the first boot, when you get to desktop, those settings ought to be put somewhere where they can not be altered, and a /restore command ought to be used to bring the machine to functionality so people who bork out their machines can get back to the desktop. 


These two simple steps would make a major difference in how linux is percieved, trust me. I think the restore (just to get back to the desktop, and put root and permissions back, too, btw) would be a quantum leap towards acceptability. Hard core users will scoff, but they don't have to use it (though I bet at some time they will, and be glad it's there). Oh, and the first thing a person should see when they get to the desktop on first boot is a failsafe restore message telling them that if all else fails, "do this simple thing" such as log in from that black screen with them white letters, and type "sudo /restore" (pathing is not right, I know, but I'm not the propellor head here, you all are), and tell them to write that on the back of their hand or something.

Peace ya'll.

One "ordinary user"'s experience.

### Post by SpectralDesign on 2006-09-10
okay, so it's an ancient (contextually anyway) thread, and I see that most of the original posts are not much more than snarkiness and trolling, but seriously -- more widespread adoption is important to some, so:

Given the options on the poll, I'd say 3rd party drivers are a real issue, but that's a self-correcting problem;  get more people on-board, and more 3rd parties will begin to create or open up their drivers for linux.

So, tackle the core issues first, and that will follow, IMO.  And, here's a big issue:

ipchains, and any other firewall tech for linux is far from user-friendly.  Make a front-end that *truely* simplifies port filtering and service controls, and you'd suddenly be a much more attractive option to people without the time, inclination, education, or whatever to deal with the minutia of having a linux PC.

### Post by Kansas on 2006-09-12
Linux tends to be fragile, buggy, non-intuitive . . . isn't this obvious? All of the power and configurability is lost on most users because of the first issues which render the potential benefits useless.

Five years ago I installed Mandrake on a PC that I had just built. In addition to the driver issues, the poor documentation, the ommitted or unintelligible steps in the install instructions, there was the frustration of having the system spontaneously abort for no apparent reason. After some months and multiple attempts to get it to work, I gave up. 

I am here, so obviously, I am trying Linux once more. This time I have a Mac. Macs are about as predictable as computers come, so this time I was expecting to experience no hardware compatibility issues. I have also heard that Ubuntu is as simple as Linux gets. Well, five years later, I am having the same types of problems that I had 5 years ago. I have been back to the forums multiple times and discovered that some bugs had been fixed in the very latest release that were designed to eliminate the very symptoms that I see. I have now downloaded the software twice from different sites. I have burned the boot CDs in various configurations (which have resulted in different sets of bugs becoming apparent). 

It is weird that the Linux community wishes to share, free of charge, something that I desire. The end result of the exchange, however, is frustration that turns to anger directed toward those who are offering me the gift. I feel as though I  have been lied to once again and that as a result I have wasted more of my time on something useless and stupid. Many open source projects produce brilliant products (Camino and WordPress come to mind), so my frustration is not directed toward open source, but rather toward Linux specifically. 

The answer to the poll is clearly "other". The fact that Microsoft or Apple have a lock on the market is not to blame. I would gladly pay $130 for the latest version of OS X because it is a brilliant piece of engineering. The "eye candy" that Linux users sometimes deride is actually an outward manifestation of the  care put into the design of every aspect of Apple products. Perhaps viewing the human-computer interface as eye candy encapsulates the entire Linux dilemma. I am not a violent man, but if I see a small flightless, well-dressed bird in town tomorrow, I will choke it within an inch of its life. Am I frustrated? Yes. Is five years enough time to sort out installation bugs? I would have thought so.

---

### Post by terminatingzero on 2006-09-12

### Post by majesticturkey on 2006-09-12
I think the problem with 3rd party support is because we're run on a capitalist society, where if you build a better mousetrap, then you expect people to come to you. So you put the mousetrap in a black box and don't tell people how it works, or they could copy it and get more people themselves.

The problem is that Linux and the FOSS movement is more of a communist/socialist thing. (American prejudice aside, please.) If you build a better mousetrap, you share it with everyone else so that someone could build a better one sooner, and share that.

People won't support Linux because it's seen as esoteric and peripheral. They won't let Linux support themselves because it means a bunch of hackers who don't really care get all that secret information that they won't use to threaten them. So Linuxers get stuck reverse engineering things, or spit-and-superglue together other options.

The thing is that Linux is not for everyone. It requires you to know about a computer. Not all that much, but more than someone who's always used a computer just to read email. And it requires you to have at least some common sense, to have the ability to figure things out yourself.

There are Joe and Joanne Averages out there who can use Ubuntu just fine. My mom is one. She loves open-source software because it gives her everything she wants without having to pay for it, and the ability to find modifications. She loves the extensibility. But unlike most of her generation, she's not a technophobe. She realized that she, too, can figure out how these boxes work like her students - too many people just go "Oh, magic box, whatever." For them, Linux will never be an option.

Woo, rambling.

---

### Post by mssever on 2006-09-12
> **jason.b.c said:**
> In my opinion , Ubuntu should come with everything nesessary already pre-installed so user's like me ( on extremely sloooww dial-up ) ( **and newcomer's** ) can actually use the system without the need _to screw around and screw around and screw around_ just to get something ( a package or program ) to freakin work...!!

I think ubuntu should come with all packages and such installed by default ( **Even if [SIZE=3]"YOU"[/SIZE] don't think you'll need them or if it's not a good idea.!** ) I do happen to think it's a good idea.!

And it could easily be done, Just include "two"(2) install CD's instead of the one , ( one for the base install and one for the extra stuff. ) That way newcomers and other's like myself wouldn't be having such a hard time doing normal things ....  Like trying to install a package that requires some other kind of package to make it work., And you don't know how to/what to get...

Like for example, I've been trying to install **Eagle** ( look it up in synaptic ), But see thats the thing, I can't install it because of some unresolved issues because i need something else ( another package to make it work ) wich i can't seem to get for any reason..!, Now if that thing was included already in the base system it wouldn't have been a problem, Now Would It.?

Even if other types of packages may never be needed by someone dosen't mean they shouldn't be included, Who knows, You ( or someone unknowing ) might just need it someday.

I know i'll problably catch hell for this.! , I don't care..!How much disk space do you want to require? Eagle is specialized software, so if Ubuntu included it, probably many more people would complain. And you don't want too many install options. Try Debian and you'll see what I mean. Ubuntu includes everything that a typical user uses out of the box with the exception of certain codecs. And installing additional software is simply a matter of searching synaptic. It couldn't get easier. It's certainly easier than Googling for Windows software, downloading a setup file, then runing the setup utility--especially for novices.

> **woodlandjustin said:**
> But surely it ***IS*** too complicated for the average user! I installed Ubuntu maybe 1 and a half months ago. I spend many days, hours and hours and hours, coming too and fro on this forum and trying and trying to get this Ubuntu to work on my laptop. Gradually things started to work better.Having Linux come pre-installed on computers would totally eliminate this problem. Perhaps you haven't installed Windows recently? And, by the way, Ubuntu has installed perfectly on all my hardware, with everything except wireless working out of the box, so your mileage may vary. Again, when was the last time Windows worked out of the box? You have to install all kinds of drivers just to get a usable system.

> **bobbybobington said:**
> hide system folders by default. Put a message saying that going here may mess up your comp. Then have 2 buttons, Show anyways; do not hide in the future. Linux already does this. Your config files have names that are prefixed with a dot; these are hidden by default. System files require you to enter your password before you can change them. This is actually a whole lot more protection than Windows has--except that Windows system files are binary so you can't directly edit them even if you have a good reason to do so.

Also, all the advanced Windows users I know--myself included--disable most of Windows' protection features anyway. And no computer novice would go tinkering with that kind of stuff. So I don't think that a warning would do much. Those who want to tinker, will.

> **Kansas said:**
> Linux tends to be fragile, buggy, non-intuitive . . . isn't this obvious? All of the power and configurability is lost on most users because of the first issues which render the potential benefits useless.What Linux system have you used? Linux is the most stable OS I've ever used. It certainly beats Windows (I don't know a lot about MacOSX, but MacOS <= 9 was junk). If it weren't for the occasional power outage, my desktop would have months of uninterrupted uptime. And when I do reboot, it's because I want to, not because I have to. Do Linux programs ever crash? Occasionally, but no more than Windows apps. Does a crash ever take the whole system with it? Never.

When it comes to intuitiveness, that all depends on what you're used to. I find Linux to be much more intuitive and user-friendly than Windows, but I've been using Linux for awhile. And just because some people are more used to the Windows way doesn't necessarily mean that it's the best way.

### Post by Kansas on 2006-09-12
msserver,

I have tried Mandrake many years ago and now Ubuntu. I extrapolated from there to the rest of Linux as these two were meant to be the most fool proof. I realise that once you get things set up and stable on a particular system, or if your setup just happens to work perfectly out-of-box, Linux is probably very good. The poll question asked what limits wider adoption of the platform. I think that if a system works perfectly for 90% of the people who try it, it will develop a bad reputation outside of the circle of users and adoption will be limited. Add in even a few critical bugs, the requirement for some specialised knowledge and sketchy documentation and limited adoption is guaranteed.

I wonder if the business model on which Linux has been developed - give the product away and sell support - perpetuates the very problems that limit wider adoption. On a Drupal forum one time, I read a post that basically said, 'we earn a living implementing and customising this software for customers. If you are expecting us to make it easy to implement - Piss off!'. I do not get that sense from these forums at all, but I wonder if there are some subtle undertones in the Linux world in that regard.

Thanks for your reply to my original post.

### Post by bastupungen on 2006-09-12
I think Linux is NOT ready, at all, because it's not even half as easy to use as, say, Windows XP. Examples(seen through the perspective of a average joe):
Hardware: Sure most things work, but too what price. Most often you  have to configure the hardware in linux to get it too work. This is most often done through a CLI, not a GUI like, say, windows uses. Some real pains in the *** are: WLan, Sound Card, Grafics Card Some times even an new HDD can screw things upp major.

kernel: Stable as anything else. But the kernel stability is not really necessary for the average user. If you compare to, say, windows or os x it is more stable. But, even Windows XP kernel is stable enough to get an "Joe" through the day. So good!, but not really a major point for the average user.

GUI(gnome KDE): Good. But still a little too inadequate and not thought through enough. Me, an experienced user, sometimes can't get easy things done through the gui's. In Windows i can do mostly everything i wan't through a gui. In XP or OS X(some exeptions here) i don't think i have had to access an CLI too configure some part of the built in functions.

Software: There are many options to the default programs in windows XP and OS X under linux. But for some of these programs (mostly multimedia and word programs) they suffer from the inadequacy like the GUI's they work under. Example: VLC under windows is an wonder too use, its stable and can play EVERYTHING. Under Linux I have found, sadly, this is NOT the case. VLC (remember just an example) is not as stable and fast and definatly doesn't play "everything" (I KNOW there are some legal issues for this but that is not what we are discussing really. Keep those comments in another thread)

User-base: Most users of Linux are really nice and try too help. The only problem is that for many, not ALL, they have a problem with empathy. Many times you get an quick answer that is really not comprehenceable, not ever for me (then try too think from a perspective of an average "joe"). This is a really BIG set-back for many newbie users. That completly turns them away from the subject of Linux, sad but according to me true.

Note: I am NOT a windows/os X fanatic!!! I use it at home and for a server. But, i think, that Windows as totaly underestimated by many of the linux user out there. There is a kind of dogma that says that windows sux, no matter what, and that linux is the solution. Even this is a major problem for linux reaching the desktop market. An attitude like that is not convincing to the people around you, that you may be trying to convince of help use your software.

Sorry for the long post. I have had this growing inside of me for some time now and got the opportunity to get it out of my system. You may of may not agree with me. Its up too you. But please don't discard me as an non free OS fanatic, beleave me i'm not.

Also sorry for sometimes crappy english:wink:

### Post by Beamerboy on 2006-09-12
The thing that is mainly holding back linux as a desktop system is directx support.  Cedega is getting there but it still has a long way to go.  The day the average person can insert the cd for their new game into the drive and play it in linux with true directx support, is the day you will see many people switch to linux.

Most windows applications are not an issue with VMWare server now (office etc all run great through vmware server and now vmware server is free ther eis no reason not to run it). I am a Cedega subscriber but there are still many games I have to play on my laptop because they will not work under Cedega.

I know literally thousands of people the majority of which use computers for games, email, instant messengers and web browsing.  Most of them also hate windows and would switch to Linux in an instant if they were able to play their favourite games in linux.

The linux development community should be taking advantage of the disfavour WGA has earned Microsoft, not to mention DRM, and work really hard to develope a fully working directx environment to pull all those windows haters away from windows and into Linux.

(On a less urgent note, DVD playback needs a lot of work, certainly in the UK now, most dvds that are released will not play in Linux regardless of whether or not you have the CSS libs installed and configured - I recently addresed this issue in my blog and with the European Commission [http://blog.paladine.org.uk/#item7136](http://blog.paladine.org.uk/#item7136) )

So yes, linux is getting there and the difference between linux now and 10 years ago are vast, improvements all round, but still a ways to go.

Beamerboy

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-13
> **nanbudh said:**
> the apt-get system is also terminal interface based. again one needs to first find out the right package before he can download and install I'm afraid you're misinformed there, buddy. The *apt-get* system has several graphical frontends by default in Ubuntu--Adept Package Manager, Synaptic Package Manager, and Add/Remove Programs.

All of them are point-and-click and easy to use--just search for the software you're looking for, mark it for installation, and apply the changes.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-13
No, I don't think I did. I specifically quoted the fourth point, which was about *apt-get*--it has nothing to do with file and printer sharing. It had everything to do with *apt-get* being terminal-based.

I was correcting nanbudh--*apt-get* has GUI frontends. You're talking about something completely different.

As for knowing what programs to download, you can search by "name and description" in Synaptic. I don't see how it can get any easier than that. I have a much easier time searching for software in Synaptic (knowing that every result *is* software and easily installable software, at that) than searching for software on Google (where the results could be non-software results, software with spyware, software that's difficult to install, software that costs money, software with undesirable licensing agreements).
