I realise that once you get things set up and stable on a particular system, or if your setup just happens to work perfectly out-of-box, Linux is probably very good. The poll question asked what limits wider adoption of the platform. I think that if a system works perfectly for 90% of the people who try it, it will develop a bad reputation outside of the circle of users and adoption will be limited. Add in even a few critical bugs, the requirement for some specialised knowledge and sketchy documentation and limited adoption is guaranteed.System instability is due to two things, mainly:[LIST=1]
[*]Hardware. This is difficult to solve currently because of hardware manufacturers. However, if Linux were to come pre-installed on Joe Average's computer, hardware compatibility would be a non-issue and he'd have a completely stable system. Remember, the only reason that Windows works with your hardware is that the computer manufacturer includes all the appropriate drivers that *don't* come with Windows.
[*]Messing with system files. If you don't know what something does, don't mess with it--you could break something. This same thing holds true for Windows, as well. This is something experienced users are much more likely to do than Joe Average.[/LIST]> I wonder if the business model on which Linux has been developed - give the product away and sell support - perpetuates the very problems that limit wider adoption. On a Drupal forum one time, I read a post that basically said, 'we earn a living implementing and customising this software for customers. If you are expecting us to make it easy to implement - Piss off!'. I do not get that sense from these forums at all, but I wonder if there are some subtle undertones in the Linux world in that regard. Remember that Linux is mostly developed by volunteers who benefit directly from their contributions in terms of better software. They don't make ant more or less money if someone decides to sell support.

> **Beamerboy said:**
> The thing that is mainly holding back linux as a desktop system is directx support.  Cedega is getting there but it still has a long way to go.  The day the average person can insert the cd for their new game into the drive and play it in linux with true directx support, is the day you will see many people switch to linux.
Be careful that you don't assume that some missing feature is holding Linux back on a wide scale just because *you* want it. I'm scratching my head wondering why on earth I would want DirectX on my system. I don't need it (I don't have--or know of--any software that needs it) and I certainly don't need software with so many security holes. If it's necessary for some games, then maybe Cedega should include it. Remember, not everyone is a gamer. In fact, judging from the specs of computers in a typical store, most people aren't hard-core gamers for whom DirectX would be a make-or-break deal.

By the way, does MacOS support DirectX? I ask because I know that an awful lot of Windows software won't run on OSX, yet nobody criticizes it.

> I know literally thousands of people the majority of which use computers for games, email, instant messengers and web browsing.  Most of them also hate windows and would switch to Linux in an instant if they were able to play their favourite games in linux. You really know *that* many people?

> **bastupungen said:**

Then i don't think it is likly too be an good substitute for most users. Some maybe willing to earn a couple of dollars but lose maybe 1/4 of their computers hardware and compability. I know most of my friends are not. Remember that you, as a semi-experienced user, are trying to do a whole lot more than Joe Average would be interested in doing. Once Joe's system is set up--either by the OEM or by a friend--he'll be able to use it without dificulty. And, if he uses Add/Remove Programs, he can install software very easily and never have any problems installing it. Try that one on Windows. I know a number of people for whom installing Windows software is very daunting.

### Post by Shaamaan on 2006-09-16
Well, being a Windows user, I've recently decided to try out Linux Ubuntu out of sheer curiosity. Needless to say, I'm a newbie when it comes to Linux, but I think I know quite a lot about moving around in Windows (Windows, DOS, partitions, problem solving... I've done all that... so I'm NOT a 'computer newbie' who can't do ANYTHING)

So, here's a short story of how I tried. I think it should show you what are some of the problems with Linux in general; why doesn't it appeal to the masses.

It all started with someone posting (on another forum) a YouTube movie about someone using Linux with XGL and Compiz. Eyecandy! I asked around, and got a link to Ubuntu, as well as links to the HowTo-s to install display drivers drivers, XGL and Compiz.

Being expirienced with partitions and using more OSs on one computer, I've quickly set up some extra space, and booted from the Ubuntu CD (Note: a "typical newbie user" will probably either have a lot of trouble at this point, if he/she want's to have a dual-boot system, or it won't concern him/her, if Linux is going to be the ONLY OS; but I think the same goes for any OS). The installation is very smooth, apart from the point where one has to pick the mounting points. Ubuntu manages to pick the default options well, but a newbie user like myself was quite confused at this point.

Needless to say, the system was installed, and after a reboot I had a nice Ubuntu loading screen.

Now, the differences in desktop looks between Windows and Gnome are... well, they aren't really the point of this. Both are good. I was more used to Windows, but I can't say a bad word about Gnome as well. ESPECIALLY that one can move the items around as much as one wants.

Well, since my goal at that stage was to install Compiz to have some fun with the eyecandy, I proceeded to install the display drivers.

And here comes the first problem. The drivers are screwed, and after following everything, X crashed. Well, it's the PCI address that somehow gets 'detected'. A bit of frustration and panic, some attempts at console commands (I noticed there was a backup made when I installed the drivers... now where the hell was it?), and I managed to get X working again... still, no drivers. After some googling and toying around, I found the cause, and managed to get the drivers working. Thank God I used DOS a lot, so I didn't panic out completely when the console showed up, and I managed to find my way... took a bit of trial and error, but I got there.
STOP! At this point, any 'typical' Windows user would have quit, and returned the OS back to the store (yes, Linux is free, but he could have returned it to the store where he got the computer from... assuming he got the Ubuntu CD from that place as well :p ), fuming with anger most likely. So, here's problem A...
Suprisingly, installing XGL and Compiz wasn't that hard, when I followed the guides. BUT, if I'm to whine (and a 'typical' Windows user WILL whine a lot)... quite a lot of manual file editing was needed. The Synaptic Package thingy is a VERY good idea, but it's still missing a lot. A typical user will want to have an installer do everything FOR him, rather than have to edit all the files manually.
Well, needless to say, I got Compiz working, and the eyecandy was looking nice. I've had some trouble setting up Compiz to how I like it most, but I guess I can forgive this, as it's still beta (a final version should have a well working configuration tool; when I was setting up compiz, I had to use the Gnome Configuration Editor, and the current, new Compiz config tool still has a long journey ahead, if it wants to be really good).
OK, so eyecandy's there, but what else can I do on this thing? Since this whole thing was more or less as an experiment, I decided I might as well see where it goes.
The already installed Open Office is a good idea. A free OS for office use with default, FREE, MS-Office-like applications is indeed a nice solution. But I'm at home, I wasn't really looking for an office solution. So, first thing I decided to try out was multimedia. I have a lot of audio and video files on my PC, so I wanted to test this out.
And behold, a BIG problem. Multimedia support in Ubuntu, I'm sorry to say this, but it SUCKS! I followed quite a few guides, but I still had trouble running some video files. Finally, a solution presented itself in the form of Automatix, which managed to install all the needed stuff for me. But it should be there by default. (OK, I know. Some of these codecs I've got installed are probably illegal right now. But still... if you want to get a Windows user to switch to Linux, this HAS to be sorted out.)
Well, so I finally got multimedia working. So now what?
Well, being a gaming fan, I started looking around at the options that I have for running games under Linux. And unfortunetly, I haven't been able to solve the problem thus far. Cedega IS an option, just like Wine. But Wine can run only a few games, and for Cedega one has to pay. Sure, there are a few games that have Linux support: Americas Army for example. But I want to be able to play the games I already have, or new ones, or demos!
So, this is a problem. I suspect the exact same thing goes for other software that's been developed 'for Windows only'.

All in all, I can see GREAT potential in Linux. However, it still has a long way to go, to be REALLY user friendly and to be a serious threat to MS and it's Windows. Right now, it's main plus is that it's FREE, and that it's a lot more secure than Windows. Oh! And it uses a HELL of a lot less resources than Windows. ;)

Quick EDIT: I should also note: so far MOST of the hardware drivers work. The only thing that doesn't is my webcam, but since I don't have a MSN-compatible tool (well, there's aMSN, but it looks so ugly, I couldn't stand it) I really don't care. If I were to vote on the poll in this thread, it would HAVE to be 3rd party support... but ADD SOFTWARE IN TEHRE AS WELL!

Second EDIT:
Also, I still can't get used to the way files are organized in Linux. Everything seems to be everywhere. Someone had to point out to me, that files are sorted out according to their type. So you get all .DLLs together, all image / graphic files together, and so forth. I've been thinking hard, and I really don't see any point of this. Is this somehow better? OK, so Windows isn't the king of the hill when it comes to managing files, but I'd much rather have all files from an application in one place, rather than all over the place...

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2006-09-16
Please don't assume that you're the first person to complain...

1. Codecs which are illegal in America should be included anyway. Or, somehow, license fees should be paid so it's legal to bundle these codecs... but who pays for it if the user doesn't? Once again, if OEMs from other countries preloaded Linux onto their computers, the codecs would also be preloaded.

2. Fair point about the drivers, but if the proprietry drivers suck it's not our fault. OEMs install the drivers for you on Windows machines, and they do the same for Linux machines.

3. Since you've been using Compiz for only a very short while, you obviously haven't seen the massive internal changes that have occurred in the project recently. Installing Compiz is vastly different now than it was when I first did it. If I'd downloaded a script to do it, and then waited a little while before running it, chances were that I'd end off with a broken system due to the changes.

I suspect another reason why there's no automatic script to do it is because it's unstable, BETA technology that is not recommended for new users. In the Windows world, you wouldn't even see a piece of software before it hit Version 1, anyway!

4. Yes, commercial game support isn't here in Linux. But that's why people still have Windows on their computers. I'm sure nobody ever told you that Linux was great at *everything* you want to do, just as nobody here will tell you that Windows is great at everything.

Having said that, are commercial games really that much better than their open-source counterparts? Open-source game development is directed by gamers, not by studios, and they have endless breadth due to their ability to be expanded.

5. There are very good reasons why there are "files all over the place" - different files mean different things according to which user(s) are running them. But really, there are only a small number of files that need to be hand-edited. Even then, they often don't need to be hand-edited, as GUI frontends are available. The big ones are:

/etc/fstab
/etc/apt/sources.list
/etc/X11/xorg.conf
wherever the grub configuration file is

Fstab only needs to be used once. Sources.list can be edited through a GUI built-into Ubuntu, but it's traditionally hand-editor. Xorg.conf is almost always hand-edited, but if you need to do it you only need to do it once. There's a GUI for Grub, but you only need to edit it once every couple of kernel updates.

Windows users who come to Linux often complain about the filesystem organisation, that things aren't grouped together in "Program Files". But when it comes down to it, I can't think of a time when you'd need to go looking around for a program's files. Synaptic tells you where a particular program's files are, and if you've compiled from source, the man pages will tell you the configurable ones.

And besides, if you can't handle the thought of editing text files for a program, then don't edit them. If you can't handle the thought of touching a command-line, then don't try to batch-encode files in Windows Media Encoder... er, then use Linspire :-)

I'm not having a go at you, I'm just explaining things that will become clearer to you as you continue using Linux. I had many of the same complaints when I first started out, but over time I've appreciated why things are the way they are.

### Post by Salim on 2006-09-16
I think the many different Distributions are not good for linux, and preventing it to widespread. Many Distributions are also doing stuff for their own. The Distributions are somehow fighting against each other instead of helping each other or growing together - at least in my opinion.
The same is for applications. Why are there many applications which are for the same one thing, but none of them can do it right. Why not work together on ONE thing which is really good?
Especially the GUI Stuff and such things should be "standardized". Why do applications use many different stuff. GTK, Qt, Tcl/Tk, WxWidgets, ...
Why are there different sound "modules"? Arts, Alsa, Oss, ...
For the user, and especially for the developers it would be easiest (and prevent apps from strange Bugs) if such things would be "standardized".

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-16
> **Salim said:**
> 
For the user, and especially for the developers it would be easiest (and prevent apps from strange Bugs) if such things would be "standardized". They're working on it:
[http://www.freestandards.org/en/LSB](http://www.freestandards.org/en/LSB)
[http://portland.freedesktop.org/wiki/](http://portland.freedesktop.org/wiki/)

Your perception that distros are fighting each other is way off. More now than ever, they're actually working together, and the fact that most software written for Linux is open source allows people to build off (and not start from scratch--"reinvent the wheel") the work of others.

As for the choice in distros, I don't see what the big deal is. Do you want one hamburger, one cheese, one car, once telephone company, one kind of household pet, one television station? Then, why would you want only one operating system to choose from?

The confusion arises from a lack of good **information** about those choices, not the existence of the choices themselves. I think all new users confused about what distro to pick should just take this quiz:
[http://www.zegeniestudios.net/ldc/index.php](http://www.zegeniestudios.net/ldc/index.php)

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-17
I'm not sure why.

From [this 25 April, 2006 article](http://www.freestandards.org/wordpress/?p=174): > The Free Standards Group also announced today that Novell, Red Hat, Ubuntu and others are all certifying their versions of their operating systems to the LSB, delivering true world-wide coverage of LSB certified distributions. > "LSB-compliance is very important for Ubuntu," said Mark Shuttleworth, Ubuntu founder and chief developer. &#8220;We believe that Linux offers the world freedom of choice, freedom to innovate and freedom to localize. The Linux Standard Base is a crucial enabler of those freedoms, creating confidence in the standardization of the core platform while still preserving the ability of the platform to evolve and improve." > The latest versions of these Linux distributions will be achieving LSB compliance in the following months:

    * Asianux
    * Linspire
    * Mandriva
    * Novell
    * Red Hat
    * Turbolinux
    * Ubuntu
    * Xandros

Yeah, they're a reasonably large barrier...I think it kind of goes along with the hardware barrier. Games require expensive graphics cards (most of them), which sometimes end up not being fully supported.

mostly the biggest barrier in general is the low-level profile that it has. They just need to be convinced that there's a profit to be made with linux, and they'll come scurrying.

### Post by ekuliak on 2006-09-17
Most people I know play commercial video games on their computer.  However, I realize that is because those are the types of people I tend to get along with.  

A lot of gamers don't even play computer games, many of them will just play console games.

Most computer users aren't interested in games, technology, etc.  They'll just go to the store and buy a pre-built computer much like they would a toaster, microwave, or DVD player.

So I'd say the biggest barrier to desktop Linux is that Linux is rarely on pre-built computers at stores.  Most people probably won't care what Operating System they are using as long as it works and their computer was cheap.  I saw Lindows on a computer at my local Fry's once, but it's gone now.

Here's something to think about.  Guess what 7 out of 10 people I see in my school's computer lab are doing?  

Nope, certainly not reading forums or news on games, computers, etc like me.  Most of them aren't even typing up their schoolwork, most of them are using Myspace.

Kind of makes me wonder what the computer lab would be like if they blocked access to myspace...

ID have been one of the few companies who champion OpenGl over DirectX AFAIK. Unforunately, they ARE one of the few.

On this machine, I can't really play games (integrated GPU), so it's really not an issue for me.

On our desktop machine, it boots XP- the kids use it mainly for playing games. It has an X600 graphics card, which would run most games very happily 18 months ago. Now it struggles with new games. Oblivion (one of the most anticipated games of the last few years) runs like a dog on even medium settings. To get it running well would require spending mucho$ on a new graphics card. Or we could play games on consoles, which is pretty much what I do, personally.

The majority of gamers play games on consoles. Just as the majority of PC owners aren't hardcore pc gamers. Ubuntu on a hardcore gamers will play games thru cedega as well as if not better than most store bought, non-upgraded pc's will play the same game.

Yes, gaming is possibly holding back linux from gaining popularity, but a bigger barrier for intemediate users is the 'what if X hardware doesn't work'- my biggest issue at first. But it all works. Fear of change is a big incentive to stick with what's comfortable, evn if the fear is ungrounded.

For basic pc users, who bought a cheap pc from their local walmart for surfing the internet and reading email, the biggest barrier is ignorance. I'm not saying they're stupid, I'm saying they just don't KNOW there are viable alternatives the the big M. And as long as it works for them (kinda), they'll be happy.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-18
I still have yet to hear anything but anecdotal evidence from the "games are the biggest barrier" crowd.

[Here's yet another article (from May 2006)](http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/12686020/) showing that games are not as popular as some people here seem to think.

A few relevant quotations: > 40 percent of American adults play games on a computer or a console. That's right--40% (less than half), and that includes consoles. Not all of the 40% play PC games. > Casual games like board or card games were the most popular, followed by strategy games, action sports, adventure, first-person shooters and simulations, the poll found.  That's right--casual games are the most popular, and Linux has plenty of casual games. > More than a fourth of gamers say they spent nothing on that hobby last year and slightly more, 31 percent, spent $100 or less.

Only 11 percent spent more than $500 last year. That's right--it's not a major part of most people's existences, just a hobby.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-18
> **missmoondog said:**
> personally, games are the least needed things in linux or windows. what a total waste of a computer. #-o
You're not alone in that thought.

I think we have to be clear about our language.

If we're talking about **the group of people who are most likely to install and use desktop Linux on their own**, then, yes, games are probably a huge deal. A lot of people on these forums dual-boot just for games.

If we're talking about **the general populace** (most of whom would never try Linux on their own, most of whom have never even heard of desktop Linux), then games don't really matter--preinstallation and the overcoming of fear of the unknown are what matter most.

Here's another one--Wikipedia citing The NPD Group: [quote=Wikipedia]The NPD Group tracks computer and video game sales in the United States. It reported that as of 2004:

    * Console and portable software sales: $6.2 billion, up 8% from 2003 [3]
    * Console and portable hardware and accessory sales: $3.7 billion, down 35% from 2003 [3]
    * PC game sales: $1.1 billion, down 2% from 2003 [4]

These figures are sales in dollars, not units; unit shipments for each category were higher than the dollar sales numbers indicate, as more software and hardware was sold at reduced prices compared to 2003.

Retail PC game sales have been declining slightly each year since about 1998, but this fact should be taken with a grain of salt: the retail sales numbers from NPD do not include sales from online downloads, nor subscription revenue for games like MMORPGs.[/quote] Yes, we should take it with a grain of salt, but we're talking over five times the sales here--console games to PC games!

### Post by ekuliak on 2006-09-18
> **aysiu said:**
> 
If we're talking about **the group of people who are most likely to install and use desktop Linux on their own**, then, yes, games are probably a huge deal. A lot of people on these forums dual-boot just for games.


I was going to say the exact same thing until I read your post.


Games are a (the?) biggest barrier to the current potential Linux user who is willing to download Linux and install it themselves.

It doesn't matter how easy it is to install _______ Linux, the average computer user isn't interested **at all** in doing so.  They want it done for them.  

I'm building my dad a new computer soon.  My dad is what I would call the typical computer user.  He surfs the internet, sends e-mail, types up documents.  I'm going to set up Ubuntu for him because it is faster to install than Windows and easier to install than Windows (in my opnion).  Ubuntu should fit his needs just fine.

You might want to note the key words I used in the last sentence.  
> 
I'm going to set up Ubuntu for him

He isn't going to install it himself.  To him, it's going to be no different than going down to Fry's or Best Buy and buying a pre-built computer (with the exceptions of it being a lot cheaper, there won't be any shovelware installed on his computer, and he's getting free technical support (from me)).

In my opinion the biggest barrier to "desktop Linux" is that it isn't readily available to consumers the way they want (pre-installed on pre-built computers).  If Dell or HP (or whatever the big-name companies are now) sell Linux computers and market it well enough, then games will be the biggest barrier.

### Post by NoTiG on 2006-09-27
Heres an interesting tidbit from the latest Wine conference :

Direct3D10, which will ship with Windows Vista in a few months, doesn't seem to be a large cause for concern. At first glance it appears to be more of an evolutionary change rather than revolutionary. New shader support will be needed, but extending ours once OpenGL supports it should be pretty easy. Stefan mentioned Microsoft is currently offering a lot of incentives for Windows developers who develop D3D10-only games since they'll only be usable on Vista - there's no plan to backport D3D10 to XP. Dan Kegel asked if that means we should port Wine's forthcoming D3D10 implementation to Windows, which would be relatively easy when we switch to WGL.

### Post by mr_niceguy on 2006-09-27
> Why does MS put time in making Directx, it because a very big market is using games.

Indeed that and because they can effectively make DirectX a proprietary technology. It's all about creating or extending monopolies with that company. (although some recent signs there are a few flexible minded folks making noise within the company)

I took a long look into becoming a game developer and one thing I soon realized was that a flexible OpenGL based engine can make the end user's particular platform a much less relevant detail. In fact Mac users are a decent market for smaller companies to target since they are not as cheap as us Linux folks and are fiercely loyal. If you are a company selling titles for the Playstation or Gamecube, you're already working without DirectX.

Don't want to concern yourself with any compatibility issues? [Bytonic was able to port the Quake 2 engine to Java]("http://www.bytonic.de/html/jake2.html"). While not bleeding edge it does show full-on high action 3d graphics running great on most systems via a platform agnostic technology.

So the issue of compatibility boils down to whether the development studios care to develop games for linux. If studios can't even be bothered to make titles for paying Macintosh consumers they certainly won't trouble themselves for a community used to free software.

Finally, as a Linux user do I really want proprietary software running on the same system I store my data on? How do I know what's in it? Look at the heavy-handed DRM in something like Steam. That's something else I can live without.

I would much rather use a console for commercial games and keep proprietary software separate from my own data, thank you very much.

### Post by camilluk on 2006-11-27
Linux is friendly enough for open-minded users and for intermediate-to-advanced users. But that's not the point...

For Linux to become a real alternative to Windows, we need to migrate large networks from Windows to Linux, and here's why:

The majority of people I know can hardly start MS Word in Windows XP, and anyway they do this in a very brainless way. One thing goes wrong, like an icon out of place and they're stuck. Let alone configuring xorg.conf and other 'weird' stuff...

The paradox is that it would be easier to roll out linux on a large network, where user actions are restricted, rather than to home users. In a network you can configure the users' machines and ensure all the apps they need work 100%. Linux security does the rest to make sure they don't screw things up.

Home users are used to buy a pc/laptop, turn it on and watch the pc (windows) that tells them "click here to set up an internet connection", "found USB Media Storage" and other very user-friendly messages and wizards. In short you don't need to know much about computers to get a standard windows machine up and running. The same can't honestly be said about linux, unless you have very ordinary hardware, no bluetooth, no sophisticated graphics cards, no mobile phones, no media centre and so on. As soon as you want to do something just a tiny bit trickier and you're faced with more how-to's than you can read.

Conclusion: get the large networks to use Linux for their workstations. People will become familiar with it, they'll want at home the same thing they see while at the office. Large networks also mean a lot of pc to configure for linux, which will get the manufacturers to support linux out of the box. This will make life easier for grandma to watch her grandson's pictures on her brand new pc.

For every computer literate, there are hundreds of people who have no idea of what the words "file, boot, install" mean... so there's no point in advertising Linux to end users. Advertising should target companies, PA's and HW manufacturers. Basically, it's about politics.

---

### Post by hawk684 on 2006-11-27
The biggest setback in my opinion would be and I hate to say this but...Windows "just works".  People go and buy a computer with Windows installed, they bring it home and it let's them get on the Internet.  They can go to any store and buy a new game or program, then install it without having to know anything beyond "CD go in drive, click next a lot".  If they get in trouble odds are there's somebody at a party who'll accidently introduce themselves as "working with computers" so that they can say, "Oh, you're a computer guy?  Any idea why my computer [incredibly vague problem]?"

I know this isn't giving people much credit, but being an onsite technician for years and now being an IT manager I've just seen too many people without a basic grasp of the logic behind computers.  They don't know how to use them, they know that when they click a certain button it gets them a certain result.  If anything breaks the pattern they're used to they just freeze up (the users, not the computers) and try to start over repeating exactly the same thing.  They don't know how to read error messages no matter how simple they are (I can't tell you how many people just click OK without looking).

Linux is not suited to this, the reason I use Linux is choice.  Your average computer user doesn't want choice.  They want someone to tell them how to do what they want.  Nothing else, they don't want to know how or why it works.  They don't want to know that free software is better ideologically.  They want to play games, visit sites like youtube and ytmnd, and chat/email with friends.  Linux comes with better basic games than windows, but it won't play commercial titles like, "Generic card game superpack X" or "Latest snappy FPS/RTS/MMO" without enormous effort.  There are free alternatives to some, but without a shiny display box and CD how will people install it?  Synaptic is nice for you and me, but grandma?  Youtube needs flash of course and that's somewhat problematic, and I just spent 3 hours trying to get YTMND working (it's great at parties on my Mythbox).  It took reading all kinds of forums and seeing that a huge number of people also have problems with Firefox playing embedded media.  I eventually stumbled over a post that linked to a beta version (4.0) of mplayerplug-in and *poof* magically fixed.  Popular websites are an important out of the box experience.  Chat/Email is solved...though I might make it more obvious how to do it for new users (put GAIM with Firefox and Evolution on the default install perhaps).

Now of course this is just my own complaining.  What might I do to fix it?  Linux needs driver support (Video and Wifi mainly, everything else works usually).  People need to be able to buy any piece of hardware and have it "just work".  Average users are *never* going to read a list of compatible hardware.  They barely know what a megahertz or a gigabyte is.  They're going to buy what's on sale at Best Buy or at Dell.  Linux needs something along the lines of Autoplay so common users can install software.  This is of course could cause quite a few problems, but I think it's necessary for your common user.  Alternatively a shiny friendly version of synaptic that's customized for the everyday user.  Add/Remove is really close (and honestly I hadn't checked it since Hoary), but I'd say it needs (and I know how anti-Linux this is) less choice.  Who's going to use EtherApe that doesn't use Synaptic or apt-get already?  It needs eye candy.  Ubuntu's already pretty strong here.  I always get comments on how nice the theme is, and if I've got Beryl/Compiz running it gets even better.  This impresses people and makes the computing experience more "fun" for your common user (sadly not everyone loves the CLI as much as me).  Lastly and most importantly, it needs promotion.  It needs trusted people saying how great Linux is.  It needs the tech columnists in the local rag saying they've switched to Linux and everything is sunshine and candy.  It needs to become "cool" to use Linux and "uncool" to still be on Windows.  We need a charismatic public spokesperson to tell the world how "Amazingly Great" Linux is.  Linux inspires devotion in us (nerds), why shouldn't it inspire devotion in other people?

And that's just getting it widespread on the desktop.  Servers (small-business specifically) is another post entirely and I think more likely to happen than desktop use.

---

Publicity; As soon as I learned about Ubuntu I installed it and are trying to learn it. Linux is a mess when it comes to “publicity” compared to Windows. ### Post by Ireclan on 2006-12-03
I havn't been using Linux for long, but here's generally what irks me the most:

*It is still too command-line oriented. I feel that the command line should only be used as a VERY LAST resort to accomplish things. I forsee this problem becoming less with time. 

*Drivers. Most of my immediate problems are with hardware not working out of the box. I don't foresee this getting better on its own, because we have no way to MAKE hardware manufacturers release their hardware specifications, whereas microsoft does. If we could somehow force(here "force" being taken in the sense that we make a very unfavorable outcome for the company if they don't) them to release their hardware specifications, I'm sure we would have plenty of people who would be willing to write drivers for them in their spare time. Perhaps all Linux and open-source supporters should organize into a "union" of sorts. That way, if a company refuses to release their hardware specs or write a good open-source or free driver themselves, we just begin a mass boycott of their products and buy something else (thus leading to that "unfavorable outcome I was talking about). This might not work, however, if the leading industry giants organize themselves and ALL refuse to write OS/free drivers or release specs.

*Documentation. Simply put, the help documentation for Ubuntu isn't helpful ENOUGH. It doesn't even tell me how I can add more data to a used CD or erase a CD, all of which are common enough operations that they should be included in the system help.

### Post by theOtherMarino on 2006-12-04
Hello,

Suse user for 2 years then Ubuntu Dapper Drake user for 6 months now.

Linux is a cool thing. I don't see what I can't do with it that I could do with XP. It's just not as easy, and organised differently.

Often, apps comes with broken links, are discontinued. Packages in Ubuntu are desperately obsolete (take openobex, for instance).

On windows, apps are centralised in the "program files" directory. On Linux, when I install an app... I can't even figure where it's going. It's somehow scatterd into different directories. I've never unsderstood the difference between /usr/local and /usr/bin ... It seems that you can indistictively combine any level of "local", "bin", "share" subdirectories in /usr ... There must be a reason to this. Things go smoothly when using synaptic, apt, etc. but not as smoothly when using the "./configure make make install" procedure. I know win's registry is a crap, but I never had a problem with it, even if I'm quite sure it's messy. Broken dependencies, uncleanly removed apps may make a linux sys go havoc.

Due to a problem with udev, I can't use some usb devices. My usb key is regarded as a read-only peripheral.

When I add a disk, nothing pops up at reboot time to offer me to mont it in the file system. I've to do it myself. Why would I add a disk if not to have it available?

The elitist behavior of some is really bothersome. I read posts that are really abusive. Ingeneers are taught they are some kind of superior being or what? I have no time to learn PERL or SHELL. I don't want to use things as crappy as VI or EMACS. I'm a user, not a sysadm. I want my webcam work now, and a three clicks procedure to get it work. I know, that's demanding, but XP can do it.

And Hell, GIMP squad is... sorry... Photoshop is THE standard. You have to change your funky GUI. Each time I use GIMP, I will curse you and your twice cursed obstination. Your reasons will nevers be your users', but, yes, you are free to stick to your design.

The fact Linux is free do no prevent any contributor to ask people about usability, accessibility, relevance, and to take users'observation into account.

I tried to install a debian sarge on an old salvaged computer... Sound card undetected, graphic card undetected, screen undetected... Result : no X server. Debian managers *may* have a reason for that. As a user, I can see none. Such a system, from my point of view (a user), just *don't work*, and people that ask me to know the brand, the model, the amount of memory of my graphic card are out of their mind. How would I know it? Do you think there's a label attached to it? To know all this, I'd have to install XP, write down all the characteristics and then wipe it and install Debian?

And I even don't give a damn of what hardware I use as long as it works fine. This is a geek thing. Did you ever wonderred what brand of carburettor you had in your car? Did you ever upgrade your fridge? Did you ever flashed the BIOS of your TV set? Geek things.

Linux has been a geek-oriented system from the beginning. The "average user" has been silenced with loud and terminal "RTFMs". No, I'll never read a manual if it's longer than some pages, and I'll only read it once. No one has time to lose with a 100 pages manual. Don't dream. And if your app need a 100 page manual to be used, it may just point it's badly designed. I know; I'm a a bit excessive, but I'll open a manual only when I can't find how to do a particular thing, not to know how to open a file. The Microsoft Word manual is about 500 pages thick. I guess the Open Office one is about the same. I've seldon opened them.

The command line atavism is a burden. I use it, but with awe. I reckon it's powerful but... Let's take an example. I've got a son*/Eric**** v600i phone. No soft for Linux (thanks, phone makers). I have to use obexftp to retrieve pics from my phone... That gives commands like this :

marino@marino-desktop:~$ obexftp -t /dev/ttyACM0 -c /Images/camera_semc/100MSDCF -G DSC00003.JPG DSC00004.JPG DSC00005.JPG DSC00006.JPG

Nice, isn't it? Show this to an average XP user... But I know openobex and obexftp are really powerful tools.

But, wait, there's a GUI for obexftp; it's called obextool 0.33 ([http://www.tech-edv.co.at/programmierung/obextool/en/detail.html)](http://www.tech-edv.co.at/programmierung/obextool/en/detail.html)). I've spent 2 hours to install it (it has many funky depedencies). It's free software. It comes with an awfull graphic interface (looks like a 6 years old redhat), and will not work - a permission problem I can't solve. That means I'm stuck with a command line obexftp.

And I do not blame anybody. Son*/Eric**** are not legally bound to provide soft for Linux. The obex people have done a great work. The obextool people too. But the whole is hardly usable.

Another thing I've noticed is the fact many apps can't exchange data through a cut an paste. Just try to cut and paste between inkscape and openoffice or between Dia (diagram editor) and inscape. Technical issues are none of my business. I can exchange data between Illustrator and word.

And here again, who am I to complain? Everything comes for free. If I don't like it, I may not use it, is it? It's free, so why people would bother with such a thing as end-user satisfaction, or even "tech support".

So, I use Linux -well, I try- and shut the f*** up, for it's free. Soon, I'll **BUY** a Stronghold OS for my Sun RaQ4 server. I'll get a tech support with it, and not stay silent should there be a problem.

And again, again, and again, I don't blame anybody. Linux comes for free, is made and maintained by people who give their time to it. I would never nor use another system to run a web server neither as a dayly workstation. It' some kind of miracle that so many people have gathered their knowledge for so long FOR FREE!

And, oh, I'm an accessibility expert. Linux is still not usable for a blind (except in console mode) or a visually impaired people. Too many different graphical libraries, I guess, to make it possible for a speech engine to read anything, or for a magnifier to magnify correctly any window. Assisitive techs are really, really expensives on Win****, about several hundreds of dollars. It would have been a good way out... Gnopernicus is dead. Festival is some kind of lab creature still wired to the slab. *Sigh* There are many universitary labs that have been working on this topics for decades, and all they have been able to provide are unfinished protos. Great efforts again, but when you are blind, you need something that work immediately, not after hours (day?) of dependencies resolution, conflicts resolution, configuration, etc.

The $100 laptop ([http://laptop.org/](http://laptop.org/)) is designed for children and third world. It uses Linux.

### Post by purplearcanist on 2006-12-04
There are several reasons why Linux is not popular, and Windows is:

1. Linux is unknown by lots of computer users.  Windows is known by almost every computer userm
2. Windows is preloaded on many computers.  Linux usually is not preloaded.
3. Linux is commonly viewed as a "complex" operating system, for computer geeks.  Windows is commonly viewed as an "easy" operating system.
4. Most people are reluctant to change the OS on their computer, or even install a new one.  Installing Linux usually requires one of those two options, without something like VMWare.
5. Microsoft has a huge fanbase.  Linux has a small one.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-12-04
Cost is a tricky thing.

You'll note in my last post about Mac, I mentioned *perceived* cost because sometimes, even if Mac prices are comparable to or cheaper than certain Windows PC prices, a lot of people still *think* of Mac as being expensive--and what you think, regardless of reality, will sometimes hinder you from migrating.

Also, there is getting your money's worth versus strict cost. Oftentimes I hear arguments like, "Well, if you get a Dell that has exactly the same features, it actually costs more," but sometimes getting exactly the same features isn't what people are interested in. Sometimes it's strictly price.

Someone may actually want to get a $500 laptop with an Intel Celeron processor, 512 MB of RAM, a CD burner, and a cheap wireless card. You just can't get a $500 new Mac laptop or a $300 new Mac desktop, regardless of what features they have or do not have or whether it's "worth it."

In summary, cost as a factor in computer purchasing comes in at least three varieties (I'll add a fourth, too):

1. **Perceived cost difference** - totally disregarding actual numbers... just what someone *thinks* is the difference

2. **Value for the price** - comparing product prices once features have been adjusted to be comparable

3. **Actual price that's available** - seeing what's the cheapest available, not taking into account features or quality of hardware

4. **Associated costs** - you don't technically have to be locked into Apple's products once you go Mac, but you do feel a bit of a pull if you don't get locked in. My wife, for example, thought she could go cheaper by getting a Gateway external monitor for her Powerbook, but she noticed the colors weren't "as good" in that as on her Powerbook, and as a graphic designer, she cared a lot about color consistency, so she got an Apple Cinema Display, which was considerably more expensive but integrated better.

She also considered getting a non-Apple MP3 player (a Samsung) but wasn't sure which ones were supported on Mac as drop-and-drop players and actually preferred an interface for managing music instead of using folders and drag-and-drop, so she has an iPod.

If you feel an urge to get Apple peripherals because of having a Mac, there are associated costs with having a Mac, not just the Mac itself.

And dual-booting I've always thought of as a lame option; Mac migration, Linux migration--it doesn't matter which. Parallels sounds like a much better option. Seems more like VMWare or Mac-on-Linux.

### Post by jerrylamos on 2006-12-05
I'm a retired general user now, on PC's and main frames going back to 1982.
As far as I can tell, many if not most people could do everything on Linux (esp. Ubuntu or variants) EXCEPT many versions of internet video.  RealPlayer and Macromedia Flash beta 9 help, but there appear to be many videos on common sites like ABC News that will use some proprietary format such as ActiveX.  

Ubuntu CD Live helps, persistent helps more (whir whir whir), USB Live would be better yet, no whir, portability.  I can get USB Live onto a 1G flash memory EXCEPT FOR GRUB.  I can install XUBUNTU on a 2G flash drive EXCEPT FOR GRUB. (Just put a bug report on Launchpad on that.)

If you look on the forums you will see many people struggling with mbr onto USB drives, and grub onto USB drives.  SuperGrub gives some more options but no solutions.  I've tussled with Linux in the past, Red Hat 7, SUSE, Turbo Linux, you name it, and as far as "grub" is concerned, ubuntu is back into the dark ages where a few experts could do things with linux line mode and the general user was left to go back to Windows.

I just checked their site, and it seems they're no longer doing it. No clue when they stopped.

Oh dear, I'm rambling. 

It really does warm my cold, black heart to see companies selling boxes with distros ready to go, but I have see them having very little appeal to anyone except those of us already familiar with -and fond of- Linux. I might buy one for myself out of sheer laziness/desire to have hardware that was guarenteed to work, and I might buy one to gift somebody with, but I can't see the average computer user (who is totally unfamiliar with Linux) being willingly to plunk down the cash for something that they know isnt the standard. The majority of first time PC buyers (man it blows my mind that they still exist!) seem to have some measure of familiarity with Windows, and they'll stick with it because its "safe".

You know what I would love to see? The community at large taking a page from the play book of the folks at AOL. I mean, I cant go to Wal-Mart to buy my son diapers without running into eleventy million "FREE! THREE MILLION HOURS! TAKE ONE!" CDs. I see them at gas stations, supermarkets, and McDonald's. I've had them fall out of cereal boxes and into my breakfast ](*,) 

How cool would it be to have a project that does the same thing with live distros? Granted, we'd do it in moderation... but I wonder how many people would grab ubuntu or slax or knoppix if we could just put it out there where the public eye would fall on it (in reasonable places - libraries would be cool, maybe a few at stores selling computer related things, but keep them out of my cap`n crunch, thankies).

OH. And before I shut up (sometimes, I do that!), I think that one of the things really keeping people away from Linux are **some** of the Linux users, themselves. I'd elaborate but this is long enough as is :P

### Post by DarkOx on 2006-12-25
Hardware. Patents. That's what the marketshare arguement comes down to, 'cause that's the only thing that's really going to solve these problems.

In a worst-case scenario, both of these could be used to bring down Linux entirely. DRM could make it uninstallable, while patents could paralyize development. In a best-case scenario, they'll be inconvinient.

So here's the two questions to ask: first, how bad do you think the issue of hardware support for Linux and the issue of patents being used against Linux will be? Second, can you live with it if things do get that bad? If the answer's that it'll get bad and you couldn't use Linux because of it, then the choice of freedom's gone. In that case, market share matters.

Of course, the answer could equally be that it won't be that bad, and you could easily still use Linux to do everything you need a computer to do. So in that case market share wouldn't matter really at all. 

It may not really be about "winning", it could just be that even a 10% - 20% marketshare is enough to convince companies to release free drivers, or to put pressure to reform patent law.

### Post by ZylGadis on 2006-12-25
This is a very good and wonderfully laid-out article. All of it is true AFAIK, and I have been thinking along the same lines for some time now (OS X will replace Windows, etc). However, the article makes one fundamental assumption that does not hold true for me.

The assumption is: in order to achieve universal freedom, we need to dominate the world. In order to dominate the world, we need to sacrifice a lot of the freedom we already have.

The solution Eric Raymond proposes to the scenario is that we gather around a single distribution and help it gain as much market share as possible at the cost of sacrificing freedom. Then, after the market share is gained, that distribution (run by a single entity - person or corporation) should willingly go back to the GNU ideals. Barring the obvious ideological problems with that, I see a very important issue with that solution: trust.

There is no person on earth who can convince me that he / she will take power and then let go of it willingly. No Linus Torvalds (Torvalds following GNU principles is a joke anyway), no Eric Raymond. Heck, not even Richard Stallman. What the article proposes is a non-solution to any sane person, I think.

To top it all, I see no problem whatsoever with GNU/Linux being a fringe OS, made by geeks for geeks. I have the technical expertise to develop it, configure it, and use it. I am not the only one. Everyone who uses GNU/Linux only because Windows is malware-ridden and Macs are expensive should flock back to whatever the current dominant OS is. GNU/Linux is not for them.

This may sound elitist, but it is true. GNU/Linux is not ready for the desktop of the masses, and in my opinion it should never be.

The article also makes it clear that GNU/Linux can dictate the rules of the market at the one place where it matters: servers. That is very logical. The people running servers (I mean the people actually configuring, administering, and maintaining servers, as opposed to a CEO who has never written a single LoC) are highly technical-minded and do not suffer from the misconceptions of the masses about technology. Because of the already dominant position of GNU/Linux on servers (without sacrificing any freedom), DRM should not be a threat to the hardware that matters.

### Post by prizrak on 2006-12-26
> **ZylGadis said:**
> This is a very good and wonderfully laid-out article. All of it is true AFAIK, and I have been thinking along the same lines for some time now (OS X will replace Windows, etc). However, the article makes one fundamental assumption that does not hold true for me.

The assumption is: in order to achieve universal freedom, we need to dominate the world. In order to dominate the world, we need to sacrifice a lot of the freedom we already have.

The solution Eric Raymond proposes to the scenario is that we gather around a single distribution and help it gain as much market share as possible at the cost of sacrificing freedom. Then, after the market share is gained, that distribution (run by a single entity - person or corporation) should willingly go back to the GNU ideals. Barring the obvious ideological problems with that, I see a very important issue with that solution: trust.

There is no person on earth who can convince me that he / she will take power and then let go of it willingly. No Linus Torvalds (Torvalds following GNU principles is a joke anyway), no Eric Raymond. Heck, not even Richard Stallman. What the article proposes is a non-solution to any sane person, I think.

To top it all, I see no problem whatsoever with GNU/Linux being a fringe OS, made by geeks for geeks. I have the technical expertise to develop it, configure it, and use it. I am not the only one. Everyone who uses GNU/Linux only because Windows is malware-ridden and Macs are expensive should flock back to whatever the current dominant OS is. GNU/Linux is not for them.

This may sound elitist, but it is true. GNU/Linux is not ready for the desktop of the masses, and in my opinion it should never be.

The article also makes it clear that GNU/Linux can dictate the rules of the market at the one place where it matters: servers. That is very logical. The people running servers (I mean the people actually configuring, administering, and maintaining servers, as opposed to a CEO who has never written a single LoC) are highly technical-minded and do not suffer from the misconceptions of the masses about technology. Because of the already dominant position of GNU/Linux on servers (without sacrificing any freedom), DRM should not be a threat to the hardware that matters.

It doesn't have to be one person it could be an entity. Establish a foundation that has representatives from all the major distributions (free and commercial ones) to make sure that there is certain balance of power. There are ways of doing it without having to worry about usurping. 

GNU/Linux is more than ready for the mass desktop from a technical standpoint. Ubuntu works just fine out of the box on most hardware I have tried it on and all the multimedia stuff also works perfectly. It just happens to be legally dubious to ship it with the distro. 

What makes you think that Linux is completely free on the server market? If you read LKLM discussion on banning binary modules from the kernel you will see that there is alot of binary code that gets thrown into Linux that has nothing to do with the desktop. Embedded systems and servers include more than enough proprietary software to make Linux "non-free" on them. 

Yes sometimes it is necessary to sacrifice something in the short term in order to succeed in the long term. If you don't believe me then look at all the people who go to college, they are sacrificing quite a bit of their free time and money for education in order to achieve something. They could have been working during that time and making money but instead they chose to get an education and make more money/have more fulfilling careers. 

As far as market share goes Linux has no need to be the dominant OS it just has to have enough market share to be known as a choice. It's the Firefox effect, while IE is still the dominant browser people are aware of FF and are at the very least willing to give it a try to see if it fits their needs. My girlfriend for one has switched to FF because she likes it better. It would be nice if Linux based OS's were at the very least known enough to prompt the user to try. You might want to be elitist (devil knows I am about things) and keep Linux as a geek OS but I would like Linux to be a viable competitor to Windows if for no other reason than to not get mass amounts of spam from botnets. I don't really care if Linux is on 95% of desktops as long as it provides enough pressure on others to make their OS's secure enough for someone to need real talent to create malware for them. I also want to be able to play Toca Race Driver3 on Ubuntu and have software that lets me setup my tablet the way I want to and use all those nifty buttons that do cool stuff (like turn on damn Bluetooth that happens to be Broadcom).

As far as the article goes though, Raymond is a very smart man who knows what he is talking about. However he is overlooking one critical thing, even though Vista is not true 64bit it will still be able to use the entire address space (it's been possible for a while now) and as such will be percieved as 64bit by the end user. He also touches on being able to run Windows software being something that could help and how it's a bad thing but I don't think he is connecting it enough. If we are able to run Windows software with no fuss then no one will bother with Linux native versions. Sure we won't emulate 64bit software but as Vista is 32bit (according to him) there is no incentive for software makers to do 64bit versions for a completely different OS that has 300 variants to test on and package for anyway.

### Post by will_in_wi on 2006-12-26
It seems to me that there are two mentalities in the replies to this thread, and these seem to reflect the rest of the community. The first group are those who want linux as a major player in the software industry. These people are willing to do what it takes to get that market share, even if it means using software that is not open source.
The second group are those who want free software with no compromises. This group ranges from no binary drivers and firmware, to no binary drivers, firmware and codecs or IP/Software Patents, to all software should be free and the only companies making money should be selling services.
As with all generalizations there are exceptions. However I think that this includes most of the people in the community.

My view is that of the first group. I would like to see linux on every desktop, and I am willing to pay $20 a pop for codecs and use binary drivers, until that can be fixed.
Many from the second group will say that if we compromise now, we will never regain freedom. I say that if we don't, we will never gain that freedom, and we will probably lose any that we currently have. If this article is right (and I think that it is) then if we lose this 64 bit transition, either Vista or OSX will gain majority marketshare.
Software vendors generally try to develop for the fewest possible platforms to save costs. If we had a three way tie coming after 2008, then the easiest platform to develop on will quickly dominate because of the developers who do not have the resources to develop for all three. I don't think it very likely that we will have a three way tie, and if people want choice, even to use OSX or Vista, Linux is the best way to get it. Which would be easier, for Linux to develop wine using complete reverse engineering, or for vista to develop a Linux layer using published specs? The result would be freedom of choice because if you develop for linux your software would run on osx and vista.
If Vista dominates, then the controls that MS is trying to put into hardware will make it many times harder to reverse engineer new hardware, and nearly impossible for vendors to release specs. Also, it will become not only legally impossible for vendors to release the codecs free as in speech, it will also become technically impossible for the community to reverse engineer them. Think cracking AES 256. See this link for details: [http://www.cs.auckland.ac.nz/~pgut001/pubs/vista_cost.txt](http://www.cs.auckland.ac.nz/~pgut001/pubs/vista_cost.txt)
If OSX dominates, then we can just imagine what Apple's partnering with the music industry will involve in the next versions of their OSs. With a market share of close to 100%, will they still use OS software? Apple could either allow linux easy use on mac platforms, or kill it. We don't know which.
If Linux dominates, then the next itunes will have to be released on linux. The next iPod will be released on linux too. Imagine vendor pickup for embedded devices. Also the free software group will have their leverage to get open drivers, codecs, etc..

As I see it, if we lose, we will either, in best case, continue to be a fringe OS, or in worst case, it will be impossible to use linux for anything other than a dev platform on old hardware that is rapidly disappearing hoping for a miracle.

### Post by saulgoode on 2006-12-26
It is wonderful that all of you people are willing to pay $$$ to be able to use proprietary codecs and plugins in Linux but realize that the programmers who write the software and release it under the GPL do so precisely because they don't really wish you to do that (I am not saying that they forbid you to do that, if you are not distributing). They *want* it to be difficult for you to use their Freely-released software with "proprietary" software. As the authors of the software (more precisely, the owners of the copyright), they have the right to make it difficult -- indeed, they have the right to make it impossible if they were to so choose.

Prizrak earlier stated "Yes sometimes it is necessary to sacrifice something in the short term in order to succeed in the long term."  Well, the people who release their software under the GPL have already made just such a decision. The sacrifice that *they* are willing to make is the short-term support of the latest and greatest hardware and, perhaps even, the latest multimedia compression and distribution algorithms. They make this short term sacrifice because over the long term, they think it will result in producing the best software available.

If you do not agree with the goals of the GPL contingent and their williness to sacrifice short term capabilities with a long-term result, fine! But then why the blue blazes do you insist that they change their licensing to accomodate you? The rights which you seek to eradicate from GPL software are precisely those rights which make MIT/BSD/MPL licenses unacceptable to the GPL crowd. If you wish to permit proprietary software to avail itself of non-proprietary software, switch to using BSD software.

"But...", you say, there is more GNU/Linux software available. INDEED! And why might that be? I would submit that it is owing to there being more incentive for a programmer to write software when he knows that someone else won't leech off it to further their own ends; releasing derivations that even he, the original author, is forbidden to use. 

If you want to create an operating system that can gain a greater market share or "unseat Windows", by all means do so. But please don't twist the means of GNU/Linux to attain your ends; the people who have created GNU/Linux have higher aspirations. Whether or not you feel their methods are wrong, you are not the holder of the copyright and you are not authorized to change the terms of the GPL.

---

### Post by prizrak on 2006-12-26
> **lyceum said:**
> Right, I can see that, I just don't see how MS or Mac can compete with free, as in price. 

That being said, I do agree that is always more we can do. I think we do need to get more of the market share to keep freedom alive. I just hate that we fight amongst ourselves. I have read too many arguments about "free" and "too free" and not "free enough". We just need to get the job done. If that means we need to add drivers for now, I agree with you, lets add them. If that means that later we can do with out them, even better. We just need to get the job done and let the market take care of itself. "If we build it, they will come." This is my point about the my "third way of looking at it". Hope that clear things up. :)
Price doesn't actually enter into the competition. If you look at prices from Dell, System76 and Apple you will see that Dell is actually cheaper (on average than the other two). While the OS is free the system is still more expensive and it is obvious that there is a number of people out there willing to pay a premium for what they percieve to be a better product. Think of the iPod for instance it has less features per price than the SandDisk's Sansa players, yet Sansa is 6% of the market (second largest) and iPod is 65%. 

saulgoode,
The GPL does nothing to forbid proprietary applications from running or being installed. In fact the GPL is quite clear that the only thing that you are not allowed to do is distribute derivative work. It is perfectly legal for me to take Linux code, change it around so that it fits my park of machines and never ever let anyone see the code to my changes. I am not allowed to give it to other people without the source though. 

The GPL is also very clear on the status of output, this would include system calls to the Linux kernel from non-GPL software. Derivative works are legally grey area since it's very hard to set constraints on what is actually a derivative software as opposed to one that simply makes system calls. This is exactly why there are GPL shims around that will be loaded by the kernel and act as middlewear between it and the application/driver that is using it. 

No one here said that we should give up the GPL or that it should be changed, in fact I think that GPLv2 is great the way it is. Distributing codecs is not actually against the GPL it might be against the copyright/patent of the entity that holds the rights to them. The problem is that alot of purists are against anything proprietary even if it does not violate the GPL. 

Kernel hackers are against binary blobs in the kernel mostly because of the issues they create with maintainance since they can't fix them if something goes wrong but still get blamed for it (perfectly reasonable IMO).

### Post by equal on 2006-12-26
I just never understand this whole *How can we become the dominant force?* mentality. It's like religious zealots. "It's not enough that we believe in it, everyone else must too!" Forget that. The one thing this article forgets is, Linux is, and will always be, free. The reason people didn't jump from Windows to Mac OS X in droves is because they didn't want to pay $1200 for a new computer. When Linux gets to the point that it is just as easy to use as Windows/OSX, people will start moving over, because there's no cost involved. As soon as it is demonstrably better to be on Linux, and all people have to do is pop in a CD and point and click a few options, Windows will fade away.

And, to curb the screams of "but Linux isn't Windows! Stop expecting it to be!", I'll say right now: No one who comes in here saying "why doesn't it work like it does in Windows" wants Linux to be a Windows clone. They just don't want to have to spend an hour installing codecs, altering their xorg.conf files, and compiling their own wireless drivers. The advantage that we have as Linux users is that even if Linux one day gets to the point that we install it, and it works even for Aunt Betsy, we'll always be able to load up the Terminal prompt and get down to the nitty-gritty. The problem with Windows and OSX is not that they work well, it's that they don't allow you to change *how* they work. If Linux was as easy to use as OSX *and* was as configurable as Gentoo, everyone on Earth would be satisfied.

The primary point of ease of use is to have a piece of software, or a distro that will work on the world's computers.

### Post by prizrak on 2006-12-27
> As soon as it is demonstrably better to be on Linux, and all people have to do is pop in a CD and point and click a few options, Windows will fade away.

You are simply wrong, for one you can run Ubuntu and many other distro's as LiveCD's where you literally just pop the CD in and do nothing more and it works. For two if you think Windows is easy to install try doing that on a System76 machine where you don't have a nifty repair disk that contains all the drivers or at the very least all the driver disks. I'll tell you installing non-OEM XP (don't worry it was a legal copy) on my dad's machine (because OEM sux) was a huge pain. The network (wired) wasn't recognized and it had no internet access to download the drivers. If it weren't for having an extra machine and a flash drive there would be a big brick sitting under the table. Don't think that Aunt Jeanie will bother installing a different OS either she will use w/e comes with the comp it can be BeOS for all she cares. 


I must say that while the article is great it is a bit misguided. I'm actually surprised at Raymond for not catching on to it especially since he was studying historical trends. He is right Linux has to come preinstalled to succeed, however it has to be an organizational desktop. The reason MS is where it is, is because they marketed heavily to the organizational user that buys in bulk. 

People tend to take work home and to use the same OS at home as they do at work to avoid having to learn more than necessary. If Linux is on their desktop at work they are very likely to start asking $BIGCOMPUTERRETAILER about it. 

If you are Dell and out of 1million customers 500thousand ask you about Linux you gonna want to install it for them before HP does. 

Marketing to the organizational buyer will also take care of the proprietary software issues. For one there is no need for any kind of multimedia stuff in most workplaces (a/v production is an exception but that is normally Mac dominated either way). For two when Dell, HP, whoever else decides to start preloading Linux is likely to choose hardware components that have open drivers available mostly because they won't have to deal with maintenance the community will take care of it. Of course there is also the consideration that org desktops tend to run integrated video, which for the most part has open drivers since there is no need for 3D. 

As far as home users go as soon as the demand increases enough for OEM's to start selling preloaded systems they will be the ones who will make deals with multimedia companies for distribution of their products. It will work just as it does in Windows OEM's preload either customized software (like Dell's Jukebox crap) or just the necessary codecs. They will also be likely to go with commercial distros such as SLED so no one will have an issue with "ruining" Linux.

### Post by Johnsie on 2006-12-27
Here's what I think Linux needs to win:

-Quality hardware support. The ability to work with drivers that already exist. That would require some kind of crossover application which understands the windows drivers that are released with most hardware. Ndiswrapper does that but only for wifi. Native linux divers should be used when Linux becomes more popular and has a bigger axe to weild at companies who dont comply.

-The ability to run people's current software... the software they already have, know, and like using. This includes but is not restricted to games, office software and design software. Wine is the basis for that and must be improved to a point where it works will nearly all existing Windows software.

-Killer Linux apps, not just cloning of Windows software. Yes we should be able to use Windows software but we must also have our own high quality range of software.

-Education. Windows has a lot of courses at colleges and stuff.  Linux has a few but nowhere near as many. We needmore Linux in schools and colleges.

-Linux cd's should be everywhere. Shuttleworth has done a good job at that but he cannot do it alone. There should be much more investment in the distrobution of Linux cd's so that every household has at least one professionally printed disk.

-More people need to start selling Linux-ready pc's

### Post by angelic venus on 2006-12-31
I think that it is futile trying to track down individual explanations behind Linux's lack of widespread implementation. The reasons are a mixture of the above: publicity, people being stuck in their ways, daunted by the "complexity", daunted by the complexity (note lack of " marks... some of the Linux setup really is complicated), Microsoft's somewhat violent business model, lack of third party support (do we really want a tonne of companies selling closed source and potentially bug ridden software for Linux, anyway?).

I think there are more interesting questions, like: Do we really -want- Linux to become used everywhere, by everyone? Would that not effect Linux's integrity as people start coding shoddy applications, and having viruses written to exploit it's weaknesses (like with Windows). I am not saying that that will happen, but it might.

I think that we should not worry so much about wedging Linux down other's throats, but let it slowly sway those who come across it (or not, if the case may be).

Adam.

---

On the user end...

The simple fact that it gets neglected over every other O.S. out there.

For some strange reason... Well! Strange for tech people, anyway.
People find great confort in having those "Windows Ready" stickers on things.


[COLOR="DarkOrange"]**For example!**
My sister just bought a MacBook.
She is a small time gamer.

She craves a game, but because she doesn't see the "Mac CD/DVD" on the boxes, she won't buy them.

She is also a lazy bum, so she won't even look online to see if she can buy a game that doesn't say anything about Macs on the box.[/COLOR]

Most people are lazy.
Most people like being stupid, and ignorant.
Most people don't like putting their money on something that doesn't seem to offer any conventional security.

What most people want is something a brain-dead infant could use.
At least from my perspective.
So they loose control...
So what?!

This is what they want.


On the other hand we have us.
The few that like control, power and a sense that "this is mine!".
A level of control that is hard to define and the power that comes from it.
The search for the knowledge might be hard, but we don't care.
As long as the end product is worth it.

Of course just working is fine as well! I like it!
I rather not have to move myself into searching threw tomes of knowledge to find how to do something.

What I'm trying to say is...

You've been doing a good job and we thank you for it!

Most linux lacks right now is marketing, and support.
I mean real support.

Kind of...
There is a sticker on the box with the product they purchase.
They got home, installed and it didn't work.
They just wen't back to the store and got another one.
They put the cd in, not even reinstallation needed, and it worked.
The product did a correction to broken files on cd boot and just finished the rest of the instalation, and started.

I'm talking about...
You put in a USB drive.
The computer asks you if you would like to put your "/HOME/USER/.*" folders in there to make your computing experience the same in any computer you use that has Ubuntu.

Elephant tusks are bigger, but pearls are still searched for more often...:D

### Post by msimon1960 on 2007-01-11
Too many reasons to list -- Here's my top ten!

1. sketchy documentation  -- most of the time I've wasted in Linux has been in fruitless searches for incomplete documentation.

2. 4,932,345 different distros -- I get a headache just trying to keep up with the cutesy names and version numbers.  One linux -- and only one linux, anything more and we're all just wasting our time -- Microsoft wins.  When it comes to an OS individuality will be our undoing (a little 7-of-9 paraphrasing for the Trekkies out there!).

3. If it ain't in the depository ferget about it -- I still don't have t a clue how to install software under linux.  I've seen some apps I'd like to try but can't figure out how to install them.  Linux REALLY needs a common installer.

4. terrible networking -- GUI urgently needed.  Worked on setting up simple file sharing for for weeks and got nowhere near 'simple file sharing'.  Eventually the whole installation collapsed under pressure of endless tweaks and suggestions.  This was a real shocker for me -- I was under the impression that Unix/Linux was strong in this area.  

5. lack of commercial applications.  I love the 'free thing' but really -- think about trying to get anything done in Windows if the only apps were freeware.  I like the idea of an open source OS that is committed to speed, stability, and ease of use but there should be tons of competition on developing first class apps.  Microsoft only became the great satan when it entered into a conflict of interest by developing applications.

6. Can't imagine why, or how, a software developer would be able to release an app in Linux world -- with so many distros I imagine support would be a nightmare.

7. blah, blah, blah from linux geeks that say 'you just have to invest the time'.  It's a lame argument to excuse the simple fact that Linux OS's are grossly immature.  If a fraction of the time developing 'really neat stuff' was put into standardized documentation, proper GUI's, and providing Linux equivalent apps for popular Windows software Linux would pound Windoze into the ground.  Let's lose the text interface -- it's time to grow up and get some GUI's in place.

8.   Open Office is a great start but Linux needs an Outlook equivalent (Evolution ain't even close).  NVU is no Frontpage and I doubt it ever will be.   And the myriad of linux database systems are no replacement for Foxpro, Access (ugh), or dBase.  I've yet to find a decent customer database package (e.g. ACT!, Goldmine), or a Linux replacement for Quicken, Quickbooks, or Simply Accounting.  Linux for small business people like me is DOA until there are comparable substitutes for these essential applications.

9.  Driver nightmares -- I don't expect drivers to be written for every cruddy piece of hardware out there.  Really, I don't.  I fully expect to buy new hardware on occassion.  It's just silly to expect otherwise.  However, Linux needs a universal driver standard and a universal installer.

10.  Linux is dominated by a priesthood that takes masochistic pride in their mastery of arcane technobabble.  I started out in Assembler -- sure I could do anything with it and it was efficient and elegant and powerful and all that but who wants to spend several days programming the the processor to move bits and flip gates?  Ease of use and the ability to do things with the absolute minimum of knowledge should be the holy grail -- any other goal is just dumb.

If it sounds like I'm down on Linux -- I'm not.  I love many parts of it but it's not ready for primetime and never will be until these common complaints are overcome.  I'll continue to support it when I can, use where it is practical to do so, and rail against Windows-world everytime I'm forced to use it.

M.
