### Post by macogw on 2007-01-12
3rd party support.  For every person who is told "your hardware won't work.  you're SOL" they'll tell other people that nothing works with Linux.  We don't need to have people hearing "I'm thinking about trying Linux..." and then popping up going "ugh, stay away from Linux.  It's terrible.  Nothing works with it.  If you want to find something that does, you have to search for hours and return stuff constantly.  You practically have to build your own computer for anything to work."  And really, they're right that you often have to search online for hours to get a good list of options and that the best chance is a build-from-scratch.  The idea that it's hard to find any hardware that will work is very widespread.  I mean, I have 2 guidelines in my head: no ATi, always use HP printers.  Aside from that, I'm asking Google.  I know hardware support is great for there not being driver discs, but it has to be better.

### Post by macogw on 2007-01-12
> **msimon1960 said:**
> 
3. If it ain't in the depository ferget about it -- I still don't have t a clue how to install software under linux.  I've seen some apps I'd like to try but can't figure out how to install them.  Linux REALLY needs a common installer.
There is one.  It's called [autopackage](http://autopackage.org) and it creates packages which can be installed on any distro with a nice neat installer that checks stuff off as it goes and doesn't require hitting "next" 30 times.  I'm going to go suggest that autopackage support (the first time you use it, it downloads a file off the internet to install autopackage so that the thing you're installing can be installed) is included in Feisty by default (so you don't have to download that file).

> 6. Can't imagine why, or how, a software developer would be able to release an app in Linux world -- with so many distros I imagine support would be a nightmare.
See the part about autopackage.  Aside from that if you release an .rpm, a .deb, and the source, you're covered.  Just source and one of the two binaries will work fine even thanks to alien.

> 7. blah, blah, blah from linux geeks that say 'you just have to invest the time'.  It's a lame argument to excuse the simple fact that Linux OS's are grossly immature.  If a fraction of the time developing 'really neat stuff' was put into standardized documentation, proper GUI's, and providing Linux equivalent apps for popular Windows software Linux would pound Windoze into the ground.  Let's lose the text interface -- it's time to grow up and get some GUI's in place.
There are plenty of GUIs.  We just don't give help through them because we don't know how you've rearranged stuff and describing buttons is annoying.  As far as time investment, how much time did you invest learning Windows?  My mom spent the last 10 years trying to figure out how to use Windows and she's BAD at it.  I gave her Ubuntu at Thanksgiving.  She loves it.  The GUI makes sense to her, she can find what she needs easily because the Applications menu divides by type while the Start Menu throws everything in there and that confuses her.  It took her a month to become better with Ubuntu than she was with Windows.  By the way, for config GUIs, Feisty+1 will include Xorg 7.3.  That version will no longer have xorg.conf and instead will have a GUI configuration utility.

> 8.   Open Office is a great start but Linux needs an Outlook equivalent (Evolution ain't even close).  NVU is no Frontpage and I doubt it ever will be.   And the myriad of linux database systems are no replacement for Foxpro, Access (ugh), or dBase.  I've yet to find a decent customer database package (e.g. ACT!, Goldmine), or a Linux replacement for Quicken, Quickbooks, or Simply Accounting.  Linux for small business people like me is DOA until there are comparable substitutes for these essential applications.
Have you looked at Thunderbird?  GnuCash is an accounting program.

> 9.  Driver nightmares -- I don't expect drivers to be written for every cruddy piece of hardware out there.  Really, I don't.  I fully expect to buy new hardware on occassion.  It's just silly to expect otherwise.  However, Linux needs a universal driver standard and a universal installer.
Agreed. Completely.  If you know any really good coders or reverse engineers, recruit them to write some good drivers.  I'mgetting my boyfriend to write a driver for [something](http://www.engadget.com/2005/05/02/the-keypix-digital-picture-keychain/) I got for Christmas.  I'll get him to GPL it.

> I started out in Assembler 
Oh, you're low-level!  Can you write drivers?

Just two days ago I played around with my hardware on my old computer (the entire playing around consisting of removing one ethernet card) and had a really funny experience.

I have a Logitech USB mouse and another old serial mouse. After the playing around, my USB mouse stopped working in Windows. It worked fine in Ubuntu. My mom has some Windows-specific software (some ophthalmic stuff) so I found the old serial mouse, disconnected the modem and connected it there (it's the only place i can put it). No problem, it works. I rebooted. Now, the serial mouse stopped working. Now connected both mice, rebooted, and the USB mouse works and the serial one doesn't. Disconnect the serial mouse and leave the USB mouse in. Reconnect my modem. Now I'm back to how everything was working fine before I started out. 

Frankly, I prefer mucking around in config files because if I mess up my software I can reinstall, if I mess up my hardware I'm out. I'm one of those lucky people for whom Linux works perfectly. ### Post by maniacmusician on 2007-01-21
Anhaedra; you see the terminal as an evil, disgusting, horrible thing, but we see it as a really powerful tool! GUI tools are good for some things, but try doing a "sudo apt-get install" vs using synaptic, and you'll see the true efficiency of the terminal beginning to shine through. If you need to search for something, you can use "sudo apt-cache search". It's way quicker, and way easier. I think the problem is that we don't do enough to educate users on how to make the most of their machine.

I haven't addressed the rest of your issues not because I can't, but because I'm really really tired and have to go to bed now. Just remember, the terminal is your friend. And if you don't like figuring it out, then ask someone on here, they'll give you an answer, and you can just copy and paste the command. If you can't even do that; well, perhaps Linux isn't for you then. [shrug] can't please everyone.

---

### Post by wersdaluv on 2007-01-21

### Post by darrenm on 2007-01-21
> **Anhaedra said:**
> So have I... It works fine... What problems have you had?

Personally:

My SATA controller not being recognised by the Windows installer so I have to use an F6 driver floppy except I can't because I don't have a floppy drive. Only solution is to buy a USB floppy drive.

Once I have bought a USB floppy drive and gone to another computer to download the F6 drivers for the SATA controller then installed Windows my onboard ethernet controller doesn't work so I can't go online to get any other drivers. Cue another trip to someone elses house to download stuff.

All this is assuming I know exactly which SATA controller I have, which ethernet controller I have, which mainboard I have etc.

In contrast with Ubuntu I put the live CD in, double click install, answer a few questions and browse the web for a bit while it installs (still can't get over how cool that is), reboot and everything is ready and working.

[sarcasm]But of course, Linux is not ready for the desktop and Windows is...[/sarcasm] Because Windows *is* the desktop and if Ubuntu is measured against how user-friendly Windows is then Windows has light years to go to catch up.

To answer the thread question - The biggest setback preventing widespread adoption of Linux is Microsoft threatening to cut the supply of sweeteners to the OEM's if they supply Linux OS's. I've personally been involved in this kind of thing so yes it does happen.

### Post by LookTJ on 2007-01-21

### Post by Pobega on 2007-01-21
> **wersdaluv said:**
> Yes, the terminal is so powerful, but it's not newb proof. Until now, I still don't know much with the terminal. I can't even install using "make" with the use of the terminal. 

The terminal isn't made to be noob proof, it's a tool everyone has to learn to use though. It's not like it's that hard of a concept to understand, you type in commands occasionally with arguments, and anything pertaining to changing your system you run as root.

> **wersdaluv said:**
> I have always believed that what Ubuntu needs is a very very very comprehensive terminal tutorial  because not everyone can learn it all with all the guides available online. Yes, those guides are good but not everybody comprehends well.

A terminal tutorial? The terminal is probably the easiest thing to learn about Linux, when I started two months ago I knew nothing about the terminal; After reading and posting on these forums I've learned the most important aspects to the terminal (aptitude, vim, cat) and the more obscure but useful commands (lspci, ifconfig, iwconfig, dmesg, using grep). 

Ubuntu, unlike most other distros offers a huge community made up of both Ubuntu newbs and Linux gurus. All you have to do is hang around the forums and you'll learn everything you need to learn, I promise.

### Post by Salim on 2007-01-21
The problem is in my oppinion:
There are too many (different) open-source "stuff" for the same purpose. Instead of becoming easy, it's getting more and more difficult to develop for linux platforms. GTK, Qt,... Gnome, KDE, XFCE,.... Different Distributions which are in my oppinion not that different: SuSE, Ubuntu, Mandrake/Mandriva, FedoraCore, and many more. If there would be one, or at least one big system they could/would really base on, one really great instead of many different partially incomplete or buggy stuff, it would be perfect.
Why is there Kontact, Evolution, ..? Why not one? Why is there GAIM, Kopete, ... why not one working with many features? And there should be more and better and stricter specifications. Look at MacOS X, its specifications and strictness is in my opinion very good. It's somehow degenerating. It's frustrating.

---

### Post by Henry Rayker on 2007-01-21
> **Anhaedra said:**
> Can you tell me a linux distro that never requires touching a terminal, or any obscurely named programs? I've never seen one... I've used SuSE, Debian, Ubuntu, Vector, Mandrake, Knoppix, and Beatrix, and none of them worked 100% correctly upon install without opening the terminal and installing some stuff, or editing some config files buried under obscurely named directories, both of which would confuse the hell out of the average user. A mainstream OS is one which works correctly on a fresh install on almost any hardware, requires no mucking about in config files, no terminal, etc... such as Windows (love it or hate it), or Mac OS X. And not so much that it costs money as much as how many people use it.

OSX uses installation from source as well through a terminal (in some cases).
Some windows apps have config files, they have the command line too (and I've had to use it on every installation I've done just to get the ethernet working properly).

Have you EVER done a fresh install of Windows? (I'm talking from a retail version of the OS, no bundled with the PC "restore disk" or any of that). You need drivers for EVERYTHING!!! My wireless card in Windows came with a severely scratched installation disk...good thing I had Ubuntu (which had a semi-working driver in the kernel) so I could download the Windows driver for installation.

The obscurely named programs? "Outlook Express" = email? Honestly, the fact that the names are different doesn't imply that the names are obscure.

@Salim:
You think the diversity is harmful, I think it's helpful. All of the projects feed off of one another. Ubuntu takes from Debian (directly) but also takes from Red Hat and others as well. Basically, it's a survival of the fittest sort of situation; the apps that are supported the most by the community are the apps that survive.

### Post by euler_fan on 2007-01-22
I would say the big three issues are 

1) Exposure: I know several of my peers (I am in college) who would be willing to try or switch if there was more exposure of the current state of Linux. E.g. not all that hard to do a stable 32-bit install from a live CD and most things work great. One (who tried and now duel boots) simply never knew anyone who could point them in a good direction.
2) Hardware support: this feeds into number 1, as it makes it easier and easier to make a basic install work, and thus the "truth about Linux" gets better and better, making it an easier and easier sell.
3) A major sponsor: Basically, when Dell, HP, and Levono are offering Linux as a preloaded and configured option on their machines (even if each of them picks a different "supported" distro) that would be a big step.

Just a thought or three. :)

### Post by harley_frog on 2007-01-22
> **darrenm said:**
> Nope, I don't believe it. PC gaming is dead, or at least its taking its final gasp of breath. Next gen consoles such as the Wii and... well just the Wii really have put the final nail in the coffin. Theres still the hardcore gamer playing WoW etc. but its dwindling down. The PC is going back to where it started, as a glorified word processor. Cheap, fully integrated PCs that you can buy from Dell or HP for £300 are becoming the PC to purchase for Joe Average, no-one buys gamer PC's any more.

For years, the main sticking point preventing computers to use the television as a monitor has been resolution.  Now, with the rising popularity (nay, now nearly required, thanks to the FCC) of HD TVs, that barrier has been breached.  The advent of the Home Entertainment PC, couple with the latest generation game consoles (X-Box 360, PS 3, Wii) means that there will be a convergence of the two (the Wii being a perfect example).  The latest game consoles are nothing more than highly modded PCs.  Question is, who's going to be the first to make the convergence complete?

But, enough with that.  The question was "What is the biggest setback that is preventing Linux to widespread?".  To be perfectly honest, I think the biggest setback is computer users themselves.  People like to have their comfort zones and are reluctant, even hostile, to change.  My mother-in-law is a perfect example of the extreme.  Little over a year ago, she decided to buy a new computer and my wife suggested to her to ask for my advice (I work as a sysadmin).  When I suggested that she buy a Mac Mini, she flatly refused and went and purchased an HP computer.  Now, she's having some issues (big surprise).  Why did she buy the PC?  Because it was what she was used to and she didn't want to learn a new system.

I also feel that Linux users have some share of the blame, too (and I include myself in this category).  Since most (if not all) Linux users (at least the ones that use it at home) got it for free off the Internet, they feel some sense of entitlement that everything having to do with Linux has to be either free (as in cost) and/or open source.  Granted, one of the reasons I love Linux is because it's free and so many programs that run on it are as well.  However, I'm not such an open-source evangelist to turn my nose up at propriety, even closed-source Linux programs like Adobe's Flash, Real Player, etc.  To many, closed-source programs is just a step away from the evil empire (read M$).  A few years ago, Corel put out WordPerfect for Linux, but pulled it after only the first release.  And this is a program that has a dwindling market share in the PC industry.  I believe that Linux is a terrific platform for a wide range of applications (even more so that Windoze) and the market (and the recent CES) bears this out.  Linux users need to get over themselves and accept the fact that Linux won't truly thrive and expand beyond its current state without support from the major software publishers.  (I'll be donning my Nomex suit now :wink: )

---

### Post by darrenm on 2007-01-22
> **harley_frog said:**
> For years, the main sticking point preventing computers to use the television as a monitor has been resolution.  Now, with the rising popularity (nay, now nearly required, thanks to the FCC) of HD TVs, that barrier has been breached.  The advent of the Home Entertainment PC, couple with the latest generation game consoles (X-Box 360, PS 3, Wii) means that there will be a convergence of the two (the Wii being a perfect example).  The latest game consoles are nothing more than highly modded PCs.  Question is, who's going to be the first to make the convergence complete?

Aha the old living room PC debate. Again this is something I have experience in so sorry for being the know-it-all here.

I used to work for a UK-based OEM / SI, one of the biggest in the UK. My job was in R&D but nothing as technical as it sounds, I was basically like an integrator, I had to design the PC's with the hardware and made sure they all worked together.

The biggest thing I did was when Windows Media Center came on the scene. Microsoft only released the first version (Harmony) to OEM's, no retail so we were the beta testers for them. Once we got all the issues out the way they went on to release Symphony to retail.

We did *everything* we possibly could to get those machines quiet. We used 5400rpm HDDs with the firmware programmed to slower seek times to cut down on noise, used fan speed reducers to slow the fans down, used passive cooling wherever possible, noise cancelling material on the inside of cases etc.

In the end we still couldn't get them quiet enough so users were happy with the noise. You can't get a PC that is powerful enough to do anything PC-wise yet quiet enough for the lounge, it isn't possible. Yes you can use lower powered laptop style components etc. but in the end when a TV is on a fairly quiet comfortable listening volume ANY noise that a PC makes is too loud.

So the only thing you can do is have something very low powered that will also play media and go on the internet. Enter the next gen consoles. They are already there, PC's have already gone. The media devices that are passive and run from a server like the Pinnacle ShowCenter are great too. I hardly ever use my PC anymore. When I get a USB keyboard for my Wii which will allow me to use it with Opera then my PC will only be switched on to capture DV. Once something comes out for a console that does that the PC goes in the bin.

### Post by blackened on 2007-01-29
I'm sure someone may have already said this, but I'm not going to take the time to read through all 123 pages of posts in this thread.

All of the reasons in the poll point back to the only true answer: Microsoft's business model.

Linux lacks third party support because it is not a "mainstream" OS. This is because Microsoft locks in the entire OEM market through strongarm business strategies. If you doubt this, then look at the release of Vista this week. Pick up any Sunday paper and look at the computer adverts.

Linux lacks (good) publicity because Microsoft actively propagandizes against it in popular industry publications. Only recently have they made any sort of alliance with a Linux distibutor (to much controversy), and they say that it was in an attempt to allay the fears of businesses who use Linux that they would be sued by Microsoft for infringement of intellectual property.

Which brings us to the final point: Intellectual property. Microsoft has, for many years, had a track record of patenting pre-existing technology. The reason that they're able to do this is because of their longstanding market advantage (and the gains from it). Few have the legal funding to challenge them in court battles that can drag on for years, and those that do often have a hard time proving their case with prior art due to the dynamic nature of the internet and technology (try finding a link to an archaic app like a Virtual Window Manager, and then try to prove that the code was written prior to Microsoft's patent).

I apologize for the rant, but there is only one real option in this poll. The rest are moot.

---

HOPE FOR GAIN: WINDOW$/APPLE
when people write programs, develop peripherals, invent the i-Pod or whatever, it's because they hope to MAKE A LOT OF MONEY.  the best way to make a lot of money in personal computing these days is to jump on the micro$oft/apple juggernaut and ride.  there is nothing wrong with this, it's called the free market.  as a matter of fact, if you want to be a busy, busy person who makes a lot of money, go get a law degree and become a Micro$oft lawyer. ha ha ha!

(different money motivation than trying to make a lot of money).  UBUNTU represents freedom to me, freedom from proprietary formats, freedom from viruses, freedom from endless senseless hardware upgrades to run new "features" that are barely better than the last GREATEST THING, freedom to choose my own desktop!!!  freedom to run XUBUNTU on my daughter's CYRIX II 250Mhz computer that we got for FREE and turned into a father/daughter project.

SADLY:

CONCLUSION:
I will continue to UBUNTU as much as i can and try everyday to get further from the ALMIGHTY DOLLAR TREADMILL that is Micro$oft/Apple.  i will also spread the word about a great FREE operating system that is (and has the most potential to be) much better than THE OTHERS.  i will burn and freely distribute UBUNTU disks to anyone who will take and use them.

I want LINUX  to feed my dog/cat/mouse when i'm away.  i want LINUX seemlessly and effortlessly networked to my car!!!!!  why do i need a seperate security system for my house when i have a big old LINUX machine running in my living room?  when are we going to move beyond the paradigm of "computer plays music, computer stores pictures, computer prints documents"  that's caveman way of think, ugh.

what i'm not doing a very good job of saying is that LINUX is going to have to go in a different direction than windows to survive.  there is no way a that we can beat them because the playing field is not level!!!!  billions and billions of dollars does not a level playing field make!!!  SO IF WE CAN'T PLAY ON A LEVEL PLAYING FIELD - A NEW FIELD MUST BE CREATED ! !   ! ! !   

the beauty of UBUNTU over WINDOW$ is that the development cycle is so much shorter.  my GOD, a new release of UBUNTU every six months!!!  it takes window$  FIVE YEARS between major releases.  by the laws of exponetial growth, LINUX should be outperforming THE OTHER OS by factors of thousands within 10 years.  does anyone disagree with me?

BUT WE HAVE TO GET AWAY FROM PUTTING FILES IN FOLDERS AND STARING AT COMPUTER MONITORS.

### Post by presbp on 2007-01-30
There are so many different distributions of Linux out there.. many of them are for techies while much less of them are for the average user.  I know distributions of Ubuntu and a distro called PCLinuxOS are more for  the average user but what is it going to take for Linux to go mainstream.  I have spoken to people I know and a very big thing that keeps most of them away from going to Linux completely is the ability to play the most popular commercial PC videogames.  There are many other things like some programs that people require for work that they cannot use in Linux.

  The only way I see for Linux to become mainstream is if it somehow becomes more centralized and gets into the knowledge of the average consumer.

### Post by lyceum on 2007-01-30
I have a feeling that MS will just be Xbox some day, until then, this question will be asked and there will be lots of different answers. What will it take? It will take mainstream users using Linux. There is no "one disrto fits all" but Ubuntu is a good stating point. If you want to help increase the market for Linux/Gnu then use and share it with a friend. Until it is sold in stores (bug #1), I really do not see it "going mainstream" and there are too many answers to say why. I would say though that most people do not know what it is or why it is or what it is about and if they have some idea it does not fit their needs or they do not know enough or care enough to switch. my 2 cents

### Post by az on 2007-01-30
> **presbp said:**
> There are so many different distributions of Linux out there.. many of them are for techies while much less of them are for the average user.  I know distributions of Ubuntu and a distro called PCLinuxOS are more for  the average user but what is it going to take for Linux to go mainstream..

For free-libre software (in any form) to occupy about ten percent of the marketshare.  Once that happens, hardware vendors and independant software makers will offer their services to FLOSS as well as proprietary software to make money.  

Until the numbers are there, it is not economically feasable to do that at this point for a lot of people.

> **presbp said:**
> I have spoken to people I know and a very big thing that keeps most of them away from going to Linux completely is the ability to play the most popular commercial PC videogames.  .

The solution is not to make an emulator or some sort of compatibility layer.  The games need to be made for linux.  Many currently are.

> **presbp said:**
> There are many other things like some programs that people require for work that they cannot use in Linux.

The only way I see for Linux to become mainstream is if it somehow becomes more centralized and gets into the knowledge of the average consumer.


You should have seen things just a few years ago.  The rate at which this is growing is staggering.  Issues like usability and anticipating the needs of the user before the users knows what they will be are being tackled in a way that they have never before been addressed in FLOSS.

Wait.

### Post by scbonney on 2007-02-11
Marketing.

Microsoft and IBM were working together to put together Windows in the 1990's.  IBM was developing the kernel.  Microsoft was doing the user interface.  The usual cutthroat business deal and IBM had their kernel and Microsoft was using their own inferior kernel and their user interface. That's Windows 95.

IBM did not market the kernel and it ended up being used by business in things like ATM machines that must not crash.   

Microsoft spent the advertising$$$$ to become a household word.


The Umbuntu marketing platform seems throughly thought through. Gathering momentum.

I'm in love with my computer again.

                                                           :guitar:

### Post by Rodneyck on 2007-02-12

### Post by Adamant1988 on 2007-02-12
[list]
[*] **General ease of use** (installing applications, preforming basic maintanence) 
[*] **Release cycles are too fast and too minor**. 
Users do *not* want to be installing the latest version of Ubuntu every 6 months, but they are used to upgrading to the latest and greatest in many instances.  There's always a great buzz about the latest and greatest releases of the hottest distributions, so users feel the need to upgrade to get all the cool new features.   A new release every couple of years with GREAT new features (rather than evolutionary ones) is a good thing for OEMs and Users alike. 
[*] **Installation options. **
OEMs need to get behind at least OFFERING Linux distributions that they can support.  People usually just go with what is installed on their PC so OEM support would be key to gaining traction.  [/list]

---

### Post by Brunellus on 2007-02-12
> **Adamant1988 said:**
> [list]
[*] **General ease of use** (installing applications, preforming basic maintanence) 
[*] **Release cycles are too fast and too minor**. 
Users do *not* want to be installing the latest version of Ubuntu every 6 months, but they are used to upgrading to the latest and greatest in many instances.  There's always a great buzz about the latest and greatest releases of the hottest distributions, so users feel the need to upgrade to get all the cool new features.   A new release every couple of years with GREAT new features (rather than evolutionary ones) is a good thing for OEMs and Users alike. 
[*] **Installation options. **
OEMs need to get behind at least OFFERING Linux distributions that they can support.  People usually just go with what is installed on their PC so OEM support would be key to gaining traction.  [/list]
you want Debian.

They don't release until they're "done."  The last Debian Stable was Woody, which ran a 2.4 kernel.  Sarge is out now, which was revolutionary--kernel 2.6.  Etch has been delayed another six months to whenever they can get a release out.

The semiannual Ubuntu release cycle tracks GNOME's release cycle.  I'm in favor of it--it tends to light a fire under developers' asterisks.  A regular fork/feature freeze/release cycle encourages quicker and more efficient development.  If you don't know when something is supposed to be done BY, you'll have a pretty poor idea of when something is DONE, because your targets keep shifting.

---

### Post by Adamant1988 on 2007-02-12
> **Brunellus said:**
> you want Debian.

They don't release until they're "done."  The last Debian Stable was Woody, which ran a 2.4 kernel.  Sarge is out now, which was revolutionary--kernel 2.6.  Etch has been delayed another six months to whenever they can get a release out.

The semiannual Ubuntu release cycle tracks GNOME's release cycle.  I'm in favor of it--it tends to light a fire under developers' asterisks.  A regular fork/feature freeze/release cycle encourages quicker and more efficient development.  If you don't know when something is supposed to be done BY, you'll have a pretty poor idea of when something is DONE, because your targets keep shifting.

No, I hate debian's development methods with a burning passion deep within my soul.  I want to be clearly told when the development begins that "This is when we are hoping to have this release out".  I don't like the "When it's done" development method, as that just favors the "I can fix it next month" attitude.   Clear deadlines are important for me, even if there are delays, just set deadlines so we have an idea of when to expect something from you. 

A development method I would prefer to see is "Official" releases, and "Testing" releases. 

Let the testing releases go just like any other Linux project,  quick evolutionary releases, but don't make a big to-do about them.  Make them available to the geeks who will of course be happy to act as beta testers and so forth.  

And every 2-years (or whenever has been determined to be most friendly for OEMs and Users) come out with a real "official release" and parade around and show off what a great new release it is over the past official release. Hype up the official releases, and let the quick rapid fire ones release quietly into the communities.  

Hell, maybe even make them two separate distributions, just keep them separate somehow.

---

### Post by shining on 2007-02-12
> **Adamant1988 said:**
> No, I hate debian's development methods with a burning passion deep within my soul.  I want to be clearly told when the development begins that "This is when we are hoping to have this release out".  I don't like the "When it's done" development method, as that just favors the "I can fix it next month" attitude.   Clear deadlines are important for me, even if there are delays, just set deadlines so we have an idea of when to expect something from you. 

A development method I would prefer to see is "Official" releases, and "Testing" releases. 

Let the testing releases go just like any other Linux project,  quick evolutionary releases, but don't make a big to-do about them.  Make them available to the geeks who will of course be happy to act as beta testers and so forth.  

And every 2-years (or whenever has been determined to be most friendly for OEMs and Users) come out with a real "official release" and parade around and show off what a great new release it is over the past official release. Hype up the official releases, and let the quick rapid fire ones release quietly into the communities.  

Hell, maybe even make them two separate distributions, just keep them separate somehow.

You can keep the testing branch of debian daily.. You don't need to reinstall for upgrading, you just upgrade directly from the mirrors. And if you have to reinstall, there are also daily built iso images (as well as a few rc), so you won't get an outdated one. I prefer that way much better.
And etch had a deadline too, it was just delayed a lot (maybe several times), but it's not a huge problem, since tracking testing and unstable works fine. It also gives me the confidence that stable releases are not rushed out, and will have the potential to be stable.

That's maybe not perfect, but it isn't awful. (just like ubuntu model)
